| Bias correction | Yes (approximate) | Yes (exact to 1st order) |
| Works for | General, including non-smooth statistics | Smooth statistics (differentiable) |
| Fails for | Very small samples; non-smooth stats (e.g., median sometimes) | Non-smooth functionals (e.g., quantiles) |
| Origin | Efron (1979) | Quenouille (1949), Tukey (1958) |

> **ISS tip:** Bootstrap resamples **with replacement** from the original sample (not from the population). Jackknife uses \(n\) leave-one-out samples. Bootstrap bias = \(\bar{\theta}^* - \hat{\theta}\); jackknife bias = \((n-1)(\bar{\theta}_{(.)} - \hat{\theta})\). The \((n-1)\) factor in jackknife bias is a direct MCQ target.

sections.label: Hypothesis Testing — Fundamentals
sections.content:
## Hypothesis Testing — Fundamentals

### Basic Terminology

**Statistical hypothesis:** A statement about the population parameter \(\theta\) (or the distribution).

- **Simple hypothesis:** Specifies \(\theta\) completely — \(H: \theta = \theta_0\)
- **Composite hypothesis:** Does not specify \(\theta\) completely — \(H: \theta > \theta_0\) or \(H: \theta \ne \theta_0\)

**Null hypothesis \(H_0\):** The hypothesis being tested (status quo).

**Alternative hypothesis \(H_1\):** The research hypothesis.

---

### Two Kinds of Error

| | \(H_0\) True | \(H_0\) False |
|---|---|---|
| **Reject \(H_0\)** | Type I error (\(\alpha\)) | Correct (Power) |
| **Do not reject \(H_0\)** | Correct (\(1-\alpha\)) | Type II error (\(\beta\)) |

\[
\alpha = P(\text{Type I error}) = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})
\]
\[
\beta = P(\text{Type II error}) = P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})
\]
\[
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})
\]

**Trade-off:** For fixed \(n\), decreasing \(\alpha\) increases \(\beta\) (and decreases power). Both can be reduced simultaneously only by increasing \(n\).

---

### Critical Region (Rejection Region)

The **critical region** \(C\) is the set of sample values for which \(H_0\) is rejected:
\[
C = \{\mathbf{x}: T(\mathbf{x}) > c\} \quad \text{(one-sided right-tail)}
\]

**Level of significance:** \(\alpha = \sup_{\theta \in H_0} P_\theta(\mathbf{X} \in C)\)

**Size of test:** When \(H_0\) is simple: size = \(P_{\theta_0}(\mathbf{X} \in C) = \alpha\)

---

### Power Function

The **power function** of a test with critical region \(C\):
\[
\beta(\theta) = P_\theta(\mathbf{X} \in C), \quad \theta \in \Theta
\]

- For \(\theta \in H_0\): \(\beta(\theta) \le \alpha\) (Type I error probability)
- For \(\theta \in H_1\): \(\beta(\theta) = 1 - P(\text{Type II error})\) (Power)
- **Ideal power function:** = \(\alpha\) on \(H_0\), = 1 on \(H_1\)

**Power curve:** Plot of \(\beta(\theta)\) vs \(\theta\). It should:
- Be low (\(\le \alpha\)) under \(H_0\)
- Increase as \(\theta\) moves away from \(H_0\)
- Approach 1 as sample size \(n \to \infty\)

---

### Types of Critical Regions

**One-sided (right):** \(C = \{T > c\}\) — for \(H_1: \theta > \theta_0\)

**One-sided (left):** \(C = \{T < c\}\) — for \(H_1: \theta < \theta_0\)

**Two-sided:** \(C = \{|T - \theta_0| > c\}\) — for \(H_1: \theta \ne \theta_0\)

**Similar region:** \(C\) is a **similar region** of size \(\alpha\) if:
\[
P_\theta(\mathbf{X} \in C) = \alpha \quad \text{for all } \theta \in H_0
\]
(Power function is **constant** = \(\alpha\) on \(H_0\), not just bounded by \(\alpha\))

**Neyman structure:** A test has **Neyman structure** with respect to a sufficient statistic \(T\) for \(\theta\) under \(H_0\) if the conditional rejection probability given \(T\) is \(\alpha\):
\[
E_\theta[\phi(\mathbf{X}) \mid T = t] = \alpha \quad \text{for all } \theta \in H_0
\]

> **ISS tip:** Similar region has power function = \(\alpha\) (constant, not just \(\le\)) on \(H_0\). Every UMP unbiased test is similar. Type I error is the probability of rejecting a true \(H_0\); reducing \(\alpha\) without increasing \(n\) automatically increases \(\beta\) — a guaranteed MCQ.

sections.label: Neyman–Pearson Lemma & UMP Tests
sections.content:
## Neyman–Pearson Lemma & Most Powerful Tests

### Neyman–Pearson Fundamental Lemma

> **Theorem (NP Lemma):** For testing the **simple** hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs simple alternative \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\), the **Most Powerful (MP)** level-\(\alpha\) test has the critical region:
> \[
> C^* = \left\{\mathbf{x}: \frac{L(\theta_1; \mathbf{x})}{L(\theta_0; \mathbf{x})} > k\right\}
> \]
> where \(k \ge 0\) is chosen so that \(P_{\theta_0}(\mathbf{X} \in C^*) = \alpha\).

**The MP test rejects \(H_0\) when the likelihood ratio (LR) is large** — data is much more likely under \(H_1\) than under \(H_0\).

**Randomised test** (for discrete distributions): When no \(k\) gives exact size \(\alpha\), define:
\[
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Lambda > k \\ \gamma & \text{if } \Lambda = k \\ 0 & \text{if } \Lambda < k \end{cases}
\]
where \(\gamma \in [0,1]\) is chosen to achieve exact size \(\alpha\).

---

### Uniformly Most Powerful (UMP) Tests

A test \(\phi\) is **UMP** level-\(\alpha\) for \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta > \theta_0\) (one-sided) if it is MP for every simple alternative \(\theta_1 > \theta_0\).

**Monotone Likelihood Ratio (MLR):** The family \(\{f(x;\theta)\}\) has **MLR in \(T(x)\)** if for \(\theta_1 > \theta_0\):
\[
\frac{f(x;\theta_1)}{f(x;\theta_0)} \text{ is a non-decreasing function of } T(x)
\]

**Karlin–Rubin Theorem:** If the family has MLR in \(T\), then:
- For \(H_0: \theta \le \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta > \theta_0\): UMP test rejects for large \(T\)
- For \(H_0: \theta \ge \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta < \theta_0\): UMP test rejects for small \(T\)

**MLR property holds for:**

| Family | MLR in |
|---|---|
| \(N(\theta, \sigma^2)\) | \(\bar{X}\) |
| \(\text{Poisson}(\theta)\) | \(\sum X_i\) |
| \(\text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\) | \(\sum X_i\) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\) | \(\bar{X}\) |
| Exponential family | Natural sufficient statistic |

**UMP test DOES NOT exist** for two-sided alternatives \(H_1: \theta \ne \theta_0\) in general (for families with MLR, any one-sided test has decreasing power on one side).

---

### UMP Unbiased (UMPU) Tests

**Unbiased test:** A test \(\phi\) of level \(\alpha\) is **unbiased** if:
\[
\beta(\theta) \ge \alpha \quad \text{for all } \theta \in H_1
\]
(Power is at least as large as the size for all alternatives.)

For two-sided \(H_1: \theta \ne \theta_0\) in one-parameter exponential families, the **UMPU** test exists and is the familiar two-sided t-test / z-test (equal tails).

**Example — Two-sided test for Normal mean:**

Reject \(H_0: \mu = \mu_0\) if \(|\bar{X} - \mu_0| > z_{\alpha/2}\cdot\sigma/\sqrt{n}\). This is UMPU.

---

### Randomised Tests

A **randomised test** (or randomised critical function) is defined by:
\[
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = P(\text{reject} \mid \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{x})
\]

- \(\phi(\mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]\) for all \(\mathbf{x}\)
- **Non-randomised test:** \(\phi(\mathbf{x}) \in \{0,1\}\)
- Power of test: \(\beta(\theta) = E_\theta[\phi(\mathbf{X})]\)
- Level: \(E_{\theta_0}[\phi(\mathbf{X})] = \alpha\)

Randomisation is needed for discrete distributions to achieve **exact** size \(\alpha\).

> **ISS tip:** The NP Lemma critical region is \(\Lambda = L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\) — the ratio, not the difference. UMP tests exist for one-sided alternatives in MLR families. UMP does NOT generally exist for two-sided alternatives — use UMPU instead. For discrete distributions, randomised tests are required for exact level \(\alpha\).

sections.label: Likelihood Ratio Test
sections.content:
## Likelihood Ratio Test

### Definition

For testing \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1 = \Theta \setminus \Theta_0\), the **Likelihood Ratio (LR) test statistic** is:
\[
\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} L(\theta; \mathbf{x})}{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta} L(\theta; \mathbf{x})} = \frac{L(\hat{\theta}_0)}{L(\hat{\theta})}
\]

where \(\hat{\theta}_0\) = restricted MLE (under \(H_0\)) and \(\hat{\theta}\) = unrestricted MLE.

**Note:** \(0 \le \Lambda \le 1\). Reject \(H_0\) when \(\Lambda < c\) (equivalently, \(-2\ln\Lambda > \chi^2\) critical value).

---

### Wilks' Theorem (Asymptotic Distribution)

Under \(H_0\) and regularity conditions, as \(n \to \infty\):
\[
-2\ln\Lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_r
\]
where \(r = \dim(\Theta) - \dim(\Theta_0)\) = number of restrictions imposed by \(H_0\).

**This allows approximate critical values without deriving the exact distribution of \(\Lambda\).**

---

### LRT for Common Tests

**One-sample Normal mean (\(\sigma^2\) known):**
\[
-2\ln\Lambda = \frac{n(\bar{X}-\mu_0)^2}{\sigma^2} = Z^2 \sim \chi^2_1 \quad \text{under } H_0
\]

**Two-sample (testing equality of means, \(\sigma^2\) known):**
\[
-2\ln\Lambda = \frac{n_1 n_2}{n_1+n_2}\cdot\frac{(\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2)^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_1
\]

**Testing \(H_0: \boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}\) in regression (Normal):**
LRT coincides with the F-test.

**Multinomial goodness-of-fit:**
\[
G^2 = 2\sum_i O_i \ln(O_i/E_i) \sim \chi^2_{k-1-p}
\]
(\(G^2\) is the **likelihood ratio chi-square**; compare with Pearson's \(X^2 = \sum(O-E)^2/E\))

---

### Properties of LRT

- **Generality:** Applicable to any parametric hypothesis — simple, composite, one-sided, two-sided
- **Consistency:** LRT is consistent — power → 1 as \(n \to \infty\) for any fixed alternative
- **Relation to NP:** For simple vs simple hypothesis, LRT reduces to the NP lemma critical region
- **Asymptotic optimality:** LRT is asymptotically equivalent to the score test and Wald test (all three give the same asymptotic \(\chi^2\) distribution)

---

### Three Asymptotically Equivalent Tests

For \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\), all have asymptotic \(\chi^2_r\) distribution:

| Test | Statistic |
|---|---|
| **Likelihood Ratio** | \(-2\ln\Lambda = -2[\ell(\hat{\theta}_0) - \ell(\hat{\theta})]\) |
| **Wald** | \(W = (\hat{\theta} - \theta_0)'[I(\hat{\theta})]^{-1}(\hat{\theta} - \theta_0) \cdot n\) |
| **Score (Rao)** | \(S = \frac{[\ell'(\theta_0)]^2}{nI(\theta_0)}\) |

**Finite-sample order:** \(W \ge \text{LRT} \ge \text{Score}\) (for one-sided tests in exponential families).

> **ISS tip:** \(-2\ln\Lambda \sim \chi^2_r\) where \(r\) = number of independent restrictions under \(H_0\). For simple vs simple, LRT = NP test. For multinomial, \(G^2 = 2\sum O\ln(O/E)\) is the LR statistic (not Pearson's \(X^2 = \sum(O-E)^2/E\)). All three tests (LR, Wald, Score) are asymptotically equivalent — a frequent ISS MCQ.

sections.label: Wald's SPRT, OC & ASN Functions
sections.content:
## Wald's Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)

### Motivation

Classical tests fix \(n\) in advance. **Sequential tests** allow the sample size to be a random variable — sampling continues until enough evidence is obtained. This can achieve the same error probabilities as fixed-\(n\) tests with a **smaller expected sample size**.

---

### SPRT — Definition

For testing \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\), at each stage \(n\), compute the **cumulative likelihood ratio**:
\[
\Lambda_n = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{f(X_i;\theta_1)}{f(X_i;\theta_0)} = \frac{L_n(\theta_1)}{L_n(\theta_0)}
\]

**Decision rule:** Choose constants \(A < 1 < B\) (with \(A < B\)):

\[
\begin{cases}
\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ (accept } H_1) & \text{if } \Lambda_n \ge B \\
\text{Accept } H_0 & \text{if } \Lambda_n \le A \\
\text{Continue sampling} & \text{if } A < \Lambda_n < B
\end{cases}
\]

**Equivalently, work with log-likelihood ratios:** \(Z_n = \ln\Lambda_n = \sum_{i=1}^n z_i\) where \(z_i = \ln[f(X_i;\theta_1)/f(X_i;\theta_0)]\).

The boundaries become \(\ln A\) and \(\ln B\) for \(Z_n\).

---

### Wald's Approximation for Boundaries

Specify desired error probabilities:
- Type I error: \(\alpha = P(\text{Reject }H_0 \mid H_0)\)
- Type II error: \(\beta = P(\text{Accept }H_0 \mid H_1)\)

Wald's approximation:
\[
A \approx \frac{\beta}{1-\alpha}, \qquad B \approx \frac{1-\beta}{\alpha}
\]

or equivalently:
\[
\ln A \approx \ln\frac{\beta}{1-\alpha}, \qquad \ln B \approx \ln\frac{1-\beta}{\alpha}
\]

**Actual error probabilities** (with these approximate boundaries) satisfy:
\[
\alpha' \le \frac{\alpha}{1-\beta}, \qquad \beta' \le \frac{\beta}{1-\alpha}
\]
Thus \(\alpha' + \beta' \le \alpha + \beta\) (sum of actual errors \(\le\) sum of specified errors).

---

### OC Function (Operating Characteristic)

The **OC function** \(L(\theta)\) gives the probability of **accepting \(H_0\)** when the true parameter is \(\theta\):
\[
L(\theta) = P_\theta(\text{Accept } H_0) = P_\theta(\Lambda_n \le A \text{ before } \Lambda_n \ge B)
\]

- \(L(\theta_0) = 1 - \alpha\) (probability of correct acceptance under \(H_0\))
- \(L(\theta_1) = \beta\) (probability of incorrect acceptance under \(H_1\))
- \(L(\theta)\) decreases from \(1-\alpha\) to \(\beta\) as \(\theta\) moves from \(\theta_0\) to \(\theta_1\)

**Wald's formula for OC (via exponential tilting):**

For exponential families, if \(h(\theta)\) satisfies \(E_\theta[e^{h\cdot z_i}] = 1\) (where \(z_i = \ln\frac{f(X_i;\theta_1)}{f(X_i;\theta_0)}\)), then:
\[
L(\theta) \approx \frac{B^h - 1}{B^h - A^h}
\]

---

### ASN Function (Average Sample Number)

The **ASN function** gives the **expected sample size** under parameter \(\theta\):
\[
E_\theta(N) = \text{ASN}(\theta)
\]

**Wald's approximation:**
\[
E_\theta(N) \approx \frac{L(\theta)\ln A + [1-L(\theta)]\ln B}{E_\theta(z_i)}
\]

where \(E_\theta(z_i) = E_\theta\!\left[\ln\frac{f(X;\theta_1)}{f(X;\theta_0)}\right]\) (Kullback–Leibler divergence type quantity).

**Special cases:**
\[
\text{ASN}(\theta_0) \approx \frac{(1-\alpha)\ln A + \alpha\ln B}{E_{\theta_0}(z_i)}
\]
\[
\text{ASN}(\theta_1) \approx \frac{\beta\ln A + (1-\beta)\ln B}{E_{\theta_1}(z_i)}
\]

---

### Optimality of SPRT

**Wald–Wolfowitz Theorem:** Among all sequential tests (including fixed-\(n\) tests) with Type I error \(\le \alpha\) and Type II error \(\le \beta\), the SPRT minimises:
- \(E_{\theta_0}(N)\) and \(E_{\theta_1}(N)\) **simultaneously**

This is a remarkable optimality property — no test does better in ASN at both hypotheses.

---

### SPRT for Normal Distribution (Known Variance)

For \(X_i \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), testing \(H_0: \mu = \mu_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu = \mu_1 > \mu_0\):
\[
Z_n = \frac{(\mu_1-\mu_0)}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i - \frac{n(\mu_1^2-\mu_0^2)}{2\sigma^2}
\]

Continue sampling while \(\ln A < Z_n < \ln B\); stop and decide when \(Z_n \ge \ln B\) (reject \(H_0\)) or \(Z_n \le \ln A\) (accept \(H_0\)).

**Sequential probability boundaries** reduce to comparing a **cumulative sum** (CUSUM-like) to linear boundaries.

> **ISS tip:** Wald's boundary approximations: \(A \approx \beta/(1-\alpha)\) and \(B \approx (1-\beta)/\alpha\). The OC function is the probability of **accepting** \(H_0\); ASN is the **expected sample size**. Wald–Wolfowitz theorem: SPRT simultaneously minimises ASN under both \(\theta_0\) and \(\theta_1\) — the key optimality result.

sections.label: Elements of Decision Theory
sections.content:
## Elements of Decision Theory

### Basic Framework

**Components:**
- **Parameter space:** \(\Theta\) — set of possible states of nature \(\theta\)
- **Action space:** \(\mathcal{A}\) — set of possible decisions/actions \(a\)
- **Loss function:** \(L(\theta, a)\) — cost of taking action \(a\) when true state is \(\theta\)
- **Decision rule (estimator):** \(d(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathcal{A}\) — a function of the observed data

---

### Risk Function

The **risk** of decision rule \(d\) at \(\theta\) is the expected loss:
\[
R(\theta, d) = E_\theta[L(\theta, d(\mathbf{X}))] = \int L(\theta, d(\mathbf{x}))\, f(\mathbf{x};\theta)\, d\mathbf{x}
\]

**Common loss functions:**

| Loss | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Squared error (SEL) | \((d - \theta)^2\) | Risk = MSE = Var + Bias² |
| Absolute error | \(|d - \theta|\) | Risk = mean absolute error |
| 0-1 loss (testing) | \(\mathbb{1}[d \ne \theta]\) | Risk = probability of error |
| Linex loss | \(e^{a(d-\theta)} - a(d-\theta) - 1\) | Asymmetric; used in forecasting |

---

### Admissibility

A decision rule \(d_1\) **dominates** \(d_2\) if:
\[
R(\theta, d_1) \le R(\theta, d_2) \text{ for all } \theta, \quad \text{with strict inequality for some } \theta
\]

A rule \(d\) is **admissible** if no rule dominates it.

A rule \(d\) is **inadmissible** if there exists a rule \(d'\) that dominates it.

**Stein's phenomenon:** For \(p \ge 3\) dimensions, the sample mean \(\bar{\mathbf{X}}\) is **inadmissible** under SEL — dominated by the James–Stein estimator:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{JS} = \left(1 - \frac{(p-2)}{\|\bar{\mathbf{X}}\|^2}\right)\bar{\mathbf{X}}
\]

---

### Minimax Criterion

A decision rule \(d^*\) is **minimax** if it minimises the **maximum risk**:
\[
d^* = \arg\min_d \sup_\theta R(\theta, d)
\]

This is a pessimistic (worst-case) criterion.

**Minimax theorem:** Under regularity conditions, a minimax rule is a Bayes rule with respect to the **least favourable prior** \(\pi^*\) (the prior that maximises the Bayes risk).

---

### Bayes Decision Theory

**Prior:** \(\pi(\theta)\) — a probability distribution over \(\Theta\)

**Posterior:** \(\pi(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto f(\mathbf{x}\mid\theta)\pi(\theta)\) (Bayes' theorem)

**Bayes risk** of \(d\) under prior \(\pi\):
\[
r(\pi, d) = E_\pi[R(\theta, d)] = \int_\Theta R(\theta, d)\, \pi(\theta)\, d\theta
\]

**Bayes rule** \(d^\pi\): minimises the Bayes risk \(r(\pi, d)\).

**Bayes rule under common losses:**

| Loss | Bayes estimator |
|---|---|
| SEL: \((d-\theta)^2\) | Posterior mean \(E(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})\) |
| Absolute: \(|d-\theta|\) | Posterior median |
| 0-1 loss | Posterior mode (MAP) |

---

### Complete Class Theorem

Under squared error loss and appropriate regularity:
- Every admissible rule is a **Bayes rule** (or limit of Bayes rules)
- Every Bayes rule with respect to a proper prior is admissible

This establishes a deep connection: **admissible ↔ Bayes**.

---

### Sufficient Statistics & Decision Theory

**Key result:** For any decision problem, the optimal (Bayes or minimax) rule depends on \(\mathbf{X}\) only through the **sufficient statistic**. Non-sufficient decision rules are inadmissible (by Rao–Blackwell).

> **ISS tip:** Bayes estimator under SEL = posterior mean; under absolute loss = posterior median; under 0-1 loss = posterior mode (MAP). Minimax ↔ Bayes under least favourable prior. James–Stein estimator shows sample mean is inadmissible in \(p \ge 3\) dimensions (Stein's shrinkage). Every admissible estimator is Bayes (Complete Class Theorem).

sections.label: Statistical Inference & Hypothesis Testing PYQ Cheatsheet (2017–2025)
sections.content:
## Statistical Inference & Hypothesis Testing — ISS PYQ Master Cheatsheet (2017–2025)

---

## 1. Point Estimation — Unbiasedness

### Definition & Basic Properties
An estimator \(T\) is **unbiased** for \(\theta\) if \(E(T) = \theta\) for all \(\theta \in \Theta\).

**Key facts (repeatedly tested):**
- \(T\) unbiased for \(\theta\) \(\not\Rightarrow\) \(T^2\) unbiased for \(\theta^2\) (2017Q28, 2021Q17) — **FALSE in general**
- \(T\) unbiased for \(\theta\) \(\Rightarrow\) \(T\) consistent **only if** \(V(T) \to 0\) (2018Q24, 2021Q36)
- Unbiased estimators may **not** always exist (2021Q35 ✓)
- Unbiased estimators are **not** always unique (2021Q35 ✗ for uniqueness; 2019Q68: infinitely many unbiased estimators of \(\theta\) exist)
- \(T\) consistent for \(\theta\) \(\not\Rightarrow\) \(T\) unbiased (2021Q36 ✗ — consistency does not imply unbiasedness)

**PYQ (2017Q29 — \(N(\mu,1)\)):** \(t = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2\). Since \(E(X_i^2) = \text{Var}(X_i) + [E(X_i)]^2 = 1 + \mu^2\), so \(E(t) = 1+\mu^2\). Thus \(t\) is unbiased for \(\mu^2+1\).

**PYQ (2022Q13 — unbiased for \(\theta^2\)):**
- Exponential mean \(\theta\): \(\bar{X}\) is unbiased for \(\theta\). \(E(\bar{X}^2) = V(\bar{X}) + [E(\bar{X})]^2 = \theta^2/n + \theta^2 = \theta^2(1+1/n)\). So \(\frac{n}{n+1}\bar{X}^2\) is unbiased for \(\theta^2\). Statement I ✓
- Bernoulli \(p\): \(\hat{p}=\bar{X}\). \(E(\hat{p}^2) = V(\hat{p}) + p^2 = p(1-p)/n + p^2\). So \(\left(\sum x_i\right)^2/n^2\) is NOT unbiased for \(p^2\). Statement II ✗

**PYQ (2017Q23 — unbiased estimator of \(\sigma\)):** \(T = k\sum|X_i-\mu|\). Since \((X_i-\mu)/\sigma \sim N(0,1)\), \(E|X_i-\mu| = \sigma\sqrt{2/\pi}\). So \(E(T) = kn\sigma\sqrt{2/\pi} = \sigma\) iff \(k = \sqrt{\pi/2}/(n) = \sqrt{\pi/(2n^2)}\). Correct: \(k = \sqrt{\pi/2}/n\).

**PYQ (2023Q21 — unbiased for \(\sigma\)):** \(T = k\sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i-\mu)^2}{n}}\). \(\sum(X_i-\mu)^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2(n)\). \(E\sqrt{\chi^2(n)} = \sqrt{2}\cdot\Gamma((n+1)/2)/\Gamma(n/2)\). So \(k = \sqrt{n}\cdot\Gamma(n/2)/(\sqrt{2}\Gamma((n+1)/2))\).

**PYQ (2018Q21 — unbiased for \(\lambda^2\), Poisson):** \(E(\bar{X}^2) = V(\bar{X}) + [E(\bar{X})]^2 = \lambda/n + \lambda^2\). So unbiased estimator of \(\lambda^2 = \bar{X}^2 - \bar{X}/n = \frac{\bar{X}(n\bar{X}-1)}{n}\).

**PYQ (2019Q66 — unbiased for \(\mu^2\)):** \(V = 1\), so \(E(\bar{X}^2) = 1/n + \mu^2\). Unbiased estimator of \(\mu^2\): \(\bar{X}^2 - 1/n\).

**PYQ (2021Q19 — unbiased estimator of \(e^{4\theta(c-1)}\)):** \(X \sim \text{Poi}(\theta)\), single obs. \(E(5^X) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} 5^x e^{-\theta}\theta^x/x! = e^{-\theta}e^{5\theta} = e^{4\theta}\). So \(5^x\) is unbiased for \(e^{4\theta}\).

**PYQ (2025Q26 — Bernoulli):** \(T = \frac{1}{3n}\mathbf{1}(X_1+X_2+X_3=1)\). \(P(X_1+X_2+X_3=1) = 3p(1-p)^2\). \(E(T) = \frac{3p(1-p)^2}{3n} = \frac{p(1-p)^2}{n}\).

**PYQ (2023Q1 — area of circle):** \(X_i \sim N(r, \sigma^2)\). \(E(\bar{x}) = r\), \(E(s^2) = \sigma^2\). Area \(= \pi r^2 = \pi[E(\bar{x}^2) - \sigma^2] = \pi[E(\bar{x}^2) - E(s^2)]\). Unbiased estimator: \(\pi(\bar{x}^2 - s^2)\).

**PYQ (2025Q24 — single obs from \(f(x,\theta)=2(x-\theta)/(1-\theta)^2\)):**
\(E(X) = \int_\theta^1 \frac{2x(x-\theta)}{(1-\theta)^2}dx = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{\theta}{3} = \frac{2+\theta}{3}\). So \(\theta = 3E(X)-2\). Unbiased estimator: \(3X-2\) (but must check range).

---

## 2. Consistency

### Definition
\(T_n\) is **consistent** for \(\theta\) if \(T_n \xrightarrow{p} \theta\), i.e., for all \(\epsilon>0\): \(P(|T_n-\theta|>\epsilon) \to 0\) as \(n\to\infty\).

**Key results:**
- If \(T_n\) unbiased for \(\theta\) and \(V(T_n)\to0\), then \(T_n\) is consistent (2018Q24 — by Chebyshev) ✓
- \(T_n\) consistent for \(\theta\) \(\Rightarrow\) \(g(T_n)\) consistent for \(g(\theta)\) for any continuous \(g\) (2017Q60 ✓)
- Method of moments gives consistent estimators (2017Q60 ✓, 2023Q3)
- MLE is consistent under regularity conditions (2017Q43 ✓, 2018Q30)
- \(T\) consistent for \(\theta\) \(\Rightarrow\) \(T^2\) consistent for \(\theta^2\) (2021Q17 ✓ — by continuous mapping theorem)
- Sum and product of consistent estimators are consistent (2021Q18 — both ✓)
- \(T\) consistent need NOT be unique (2021Q33 ✓)

**PYQ (2020Q6 — Cauchy):** Sample mean is NOT consistent for Cauchy (no finite mean); sample median IS consistent (2023Q23 ✓). Statement 1 ✗, Statement 2 ✓.

**PYQ (2020Q11 — \(U(0,\theta)\)):** \(X_{(n)} \xrightarrow{p} \theta\) ✓; \(2\bar{X} \xrightarrow{p} \theta\) ✓. Both are consistent.

**PYQ (2018Q19 — \(Z = nX_{(1)}\), exponential):** Min of \(n\) i.i.d. Exp\((\theta)\) has \(nX_{(1)} \sim \text{Exp}(\theta)\) (exponential with same rate). So \(E(Z) = 1/\theta\) ✓ (unbiased). But \(V(Z) = 1/\theta^2\) (constant, not \(\to 0\)) ✗ — NOT consistent.

**PYQ (2024Q9):** \(f/n\) is consistent for \(p\), so \((f/n)(1-f/n)\) is consistent for \(p(1-p)\) ✓. But \(f/n\) is consistent for \(p\), NOT for \(1/p\) ✗.

---

## 3. Efficiency & Cramer-Rao Lower Bound

### Fisher Information
\[
I(\theta) = E\left[\left(\frac{\partial \log f}{ \partial \theta}\right)^2\right] = -E\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f}{\partial \theta^2}\right]
\]
For a sample of size \(n\): \(I_n(\theta) = n I_1(\theta)\).

**PYQ (2020Q27 — \(T = X_1-X_2-X_3\), \(X_i \sim N(\theta,1)\)):**
\(T \sim N(-\theta, 3)\). Fisher info in \(T\): \(I_T(\theta) = [E(\partial\log f_T/\partial\theta)]^2/V_T\). Actually \(f_T = N(-\theta, 3)\): score \(= (T+\theta)/3\cdot(-1)\cdot(-1) = ?\). Use: for \(N(a\theta+b, \sigma^2)\), \(I(\theta) = a^2/\sigma^2\). Here \(E(T) = -\theta\), so \(a=-1\), \(I_T = 1/3\). (Full sample has \(I_n=3\); \(T\) captures \(1/3\) of that.)

### Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)
\[
V(T) \ge \frac{[\psi'(\theta)]^2}{n I(\theta)}
\]
where \(T\) is unbiased for \(\psi(\theta)\) and regularity conditions hold.

**Equality holds iff** \(\frac{\partial \log L}{\partial \theta} = A(\theta)[T - \psi(\theta)]\) for some function \(A(\theta)\) — i.e., the score is a linear function of \(T\). Equivalently, \(f\) belongs to the **one-parameter exponential family**.

**Key CRLBs (all tested):**
| Distribution | Parameter | CRLB |
|---|---|---|
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\sigma^2\) known | \(\mu\) | \(\sigma^2/n\) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\mu\) known | \(\sigma^2\) | \(2\sigma^4/n\) (2018Q34) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), both unknown | \(\mu\) | \(\sigma^2/n\) (2024Q4) |
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\) | \(\lambda\) | \(\lambda/n\) (2019Q75) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\) (mean \(1/\theta\)) | \(\theta\) | \(\theta^2/n\) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\) (mean \(\theta\)) | \(\theta\) | \(\theta^2/n\) (2019Q37, 2021Q26) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\) | \(\theta\) | Does not exist (regularity fails) (2019Q36) |
| Cauchy | \(\theta\) | \(2/n\) |
| \(B(n,p)\) for \(p/(1+p)\) | — | use \([\psi'(p)]^2/(nI)\) (2022Q15) |

**PYQ (2018Q60 — CRLB for \(\theta^r\) from \(N(0,1)\)):**
\(I(\theta) = n\) (for \(N(\theta,1)\)). \(\psi(\theta) = \theta^r\), \(\psi'(\theta) = r\theta^{r-1}\). CRLB \(= r^2\theta^{2r-2}/n\).

**PYQ (2022Q15 — CRLB for \(p/(1+p)\), Binomial \(B(n,p)\), \(m\) obs):**
\(I_1(p) = n/[p(1-p)]\) (wait — for \(B(n,p)\) single obs: \(I = n/[p(1-p)]\)). \(\psi = p/(1+p)\), \(\psi' = 1/(1+p)^2\). CRLB \(= \frac{p(1-p)(1+p)^{-4}}{mn/(np^{-1}(1-p)^{-1})} = \frac{p(1-p)}{m\cdot n/(p(1-p))}\cdot\frac{1}{(1+p)^4}\).

**PYQ (2023Q10 — CRLB for \(\ln\theta\)):** \(f(x,\theta) = \theta^2(x-(\theta+1))\)... \(\psi(\theta) = \ln\theta\), \(\psi'=1/\theta\). CRLB \(= 1/(\theta^2 nI_1(\theta))\).

**PYQ (2023Q34 — CRLB for \(\theta^3\)):** Context: find \(I(\theta)\) from \(f\), then CRLB \(= (3\theta^2)^2/(nI(\theta))\).

**MVB estimator exists iff** the log-likelihood score can be written as \(A(\theta)[T - \psi(\theta)]\) (2022Q34). This is equivalent to \(f\) being in one-parameter exponential family.

**Cauchy distribution:** MVB estimator does NOT exist (regularity conditions fail) (2021Q45, 2023Q48 ✓).

**PYQ (2019Q34 — MVB attained):** \(\frac{\partial \log L}{\partial \theta} = c(\theta)[t-\psi(\theta)]\) for some function \(c(\theta)\).

**Bhattacharya bound (2020Q20, 2020Q31):** Generalisation of CR bound using higher-order derivatives of the log-likelihood. For \(g(\theta) = \theta^2\) with \(N(\theta,1)\) sample: Bhattacharya bound \(= 4\theta^2/n + 2/n^2\).

**Efficiency of \(T_2\) w.r.t. \(T_1\) (UMVUE):**
\[
e = \frac{V(T_1)}{V(T_2)}, \quad \rho(T_1,T_2) = \sqrt{e}
\]
(2019Q50, 2022Q33, 2023Q7): If efficiency of \(T_2\) is 0.64, then \(\rho = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8\).

**PYQ (2018Q20):** \(t_1\) most efficient, \(t_2\) has efficiency \(e\), correlation \(\rho\). Then \(\rho^2 = e\), so \(\rho = \sqrt{e}\). Also \(\text{Cov}(t_1, t_2-t_1) = \text{Cov}(t_1,t_2) - V(t_1) = \rho\sqrt{V(t_1)V(t_2)} - V(t_1)\).

**PYQ (2020Q25):** If \(A\) most efficient, \(B\) less efficient with efficiency \(e\): \(\text{Cov}(A, B-A) = \text{Cov}(A,B) - V(A) = \rho\sqrt{V_A V_B} - V_A\). Since \(\rho = \sqrt{e}\) and \(V_B = V_A/e\): \(= \sqrt{e}\cdot V_A/\sqrt{e} - V_A = V_A - V_A = 0\). So \(\text{Cov}(A, B-A) = 0\).

**PYQ (2024Q31):** \(T_1, T_2\) equally efficient with efficiency 0.8. \(\rho(T_1,T^*) = \sqrt{0.8}\) and \(\rho(T_2,T^*) = \sqrt{0.8}\). By Cauchy-Schwarz, \(\rho(T_1,T_2) \ge 2\times0.8 - 1 = 0.6\). So \(\rho \in [0.6, 1]\).

**Efficiency of median vs mean (2020Q21):** For normal population: efficiency of sample median \(= 2/\pi \approx 0.6366\).

## 4. Sufficient Statistics

### Fisher-Neyman Factorisation Theorem
\(T\) is sufficient for \(\theta\) iff:
\[
L(\mathbf{x};\theta) = g(T(\mathbf{x}), \theta) \cdot h(\mathbf{x})
\]
where \(h(\mathbf{x})\) does not depend on \(\theta\). Named: **Fisher-Neyman** factorisation (2020Q63, 2022Q18).

**Properties of sufficient statistic:**
1. Conditional distribution of sample given \(T\) is free of \(\theta\) (2017Q54 ✓)
2. Contains all information about \(\theta\) in the sample (2017Q54 ✓)
3. Likelihood can be factored through \(T\) (2017Q54 ✓)
4. \(T\) sufficient \(\Rightarrow\) \(T^2\) sufficient (for appropriate \(\theta\)) — depends on injectivity (2021Q17)
5. If \(T\) sufficient and \(\phi\) 1-1, then \(\phi(T)\) sufficient
6. MLE is a function of sufficient statistic if MLE is unique (2017Q42 ✓)
7. Sufficient statistic need NOT be unique (in fact any 1-1 function of a sufficient statistic is also sufficient)

**Standard sufficient statistics:**
| Distribution | Sufficient Statistic |
|---|---|
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), both unknown | \((\sum x_i, \sum x_i^2)\) or \((\bar{x}, S^2)\) (2023Q22: I✓,II✓,III✗) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\mu\) known | \(\sum(x_i-\mu)^2\) (2024Q12) |
| \(N(\theta,1)\) | \(\bar{X}\) or \(\sum x_i\) (2020Q29) |
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\) | \(\sum x_i\) (2019Q67, 2021Q24, 2022Q58) |
| \(B(1,p)\) | \(\sum x_i\) (2022Q59: only I✓, not \(X_1+2X_2+X_3\)) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\) | \(\sum x_i\) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\) | \(X_{(n)} = \max\) (2018Q35, 2020Q9) |
| \(U(\theta,\theta+1)\) | \((X_{(1)}, X_{(n)})\) (2020Q36) |
| \(U(-\theta,\theta)\) | \(\max|X_i|\) |
| \(N(\theta,\theta^2)\) | \((\sum x_i, \sum x_i^2)\) (2023Q28, 2025Q18) |
| \(U(\alpha,\beta)\) | \((X_{(1)}, X_{(n)})\) (2019Q71) |
| \(\Gamma(\alpha,\beta)\) | \((\sum x_i, \sum\log x_i)\) (2022Q71) |
| \(f(x)=\theta x^{\theta-1}\) | \(\prod x_i\) or \(\sum\log x_i\) (2020Q9, 2025Q32) |
| \(\text{Laplace}(\theta)\) | \(X_{(1)}\) = sample median (for location) |
| \(f(x,\mu)=e^{-(x-\mu)}\) | \(X_{(1)} = \min\) (2020Q28, 2023Q20) |

**PYQ (2018Q22 — \(B(1,p)\)):** Among \(X_1, X_1+X_2, X_1+X_2+X_3, X_1X_2X_3\): only \(\sum X_i\) is sufficient; \(X_1\) alone is NOT sufficient.

**PYQ (2017Q58, 2018Q58 — \(X_1+2X_2\) not sufficient):** Because it is not a 1-1 function of \(\sum x_i\); the conditional distribution of \((X_1,X_2)\) given \(X_1+2X_2=t\) depends on \(\lambda\).

**PYQ (2023Q41,Q42):** From \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\): \(T_1 = \sum x_i\) ✓ sufficient; \(T_2 = (x_1, \sum_{i\ge2} x_i)\) ✓ sufficient (contains same info). \(T_3\) ✓ sufficient (any partition works for exponential families); \(T_4\) ✓ sufficient.

**PYQ (2022Q37 — Discrete Uniform \(\{1,...,N\}\)):** \(T = \max(X_1,X_2)\) is sufficient for \(N\).

**PYQ (2021Q41 — \(U(\theta-1/2, \theta+1/2)\)):** Both \((X_{(1)},...,X_{(n)})\) and \((X_{(1)},X_{(n)})\) are sufficient. ✓ both.

**PYQ (2025Q28):** Both statements I and II are correct.

### Complete Sufficient Statistics
\(T\) is **complete** for \(\theta\) if \(E[g(T)] = 0\) for all \(\theta\) \(\Rightarrow\) \(g(T) = 0\) a.s.

- Minimal sufficient + complete \(\Rightarrow\) UMVUE can be found via Rao-Blackwell + Lehmann-Scheffé
- \(\bar{X}\) is complete sufficient for \(N(\theta,1)\) (2020Q29 ✓)
- \(X_{(n)}\) is complete sufficient for \(U(0,\theta)\) (2025Q17 ✓, 2025Q20 ✓)
- \(\sum x_i\) complete sufficient for Poisson (2019Q67 ✓)
- Minimal sufficient need NOT be complete in general (2025Q41: I ✗)
- Complete statistic is independent of every ancillary statistic (Basu's theorem)

**PYQ (2023Q43,Q44):** \(x_1\) from \(N(0,\theta)\): unbiased ✗ (\(E(x_1)=0\ne\theta\)), complete ✗, sufficient ✗. \(x_1^2\): not complete, not sufficient. Both statements false in Q44.

**PYQ (2024Q20):** \(N(0,\theta)\) sample: \(T_1=x_1\) not complete (single obs, not sufficient for whole family); \(T_2=x_1^2\) is not complete either. Neither I nor II.

---

## 5. Maximum Likelihood Estimation

### Definition & Properties
\[
\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_\theta L(\theta; \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_\theta \log L(\theta; \mathbf{x})
\]

**Properties under regularity conditions:**
1. **Consistent** (2018Q30 ✓, 2017Q43 ✓)
2. **Asymptotically normal**: \(\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}-\theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1/I(\theta))\) (2018Q30 ✓, 2022Q57 ✓)
3. **Asymptotically efficient** (attains CRLB asymptotically)
4. **Invariance**: if \(\hat{\theta}\) is MLE of \(\theta\), then \(g(\hat{\theta})\) is MLE of \(g(\theta)\) for any \(g\) (2018Q29, 2021Q38: NOT always unique; NOT always unbiased)
5. MLE is NOT always unbiased (2018Q30 ✗ for unbiasedness, 2022Q57 ✓)
6. MLE is NOT always unique (2021Q38 ✓, 2018Q30 ✓)
7. MLE need not always exist (2024Q10 ✓)
8. MLE is always a function of sufficient statistic when it exists (2024Q10 I ✓)

**Standard MLEs:**
| Distribution | Parameter | MLE |
|---|---|---|
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) | \(\mu,\sigma^2\) | \(\hat{\mu}=\bar{x}\), \(\hat{\sigma}^2=\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2\) — \(\hat{\mu}\) unbiased, \(\hat{\sigma}^2\) **biased** (2019Q31) |
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\) | \(\lambda\) | \(\bar{x}\) — unbiased, sufficient, consistent (2022Q12 ✓) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\) | \(\theta\) | \(X_{(n)}\) — biased, consistent, sufficient (2022Q12 ✓) |
| \(U(0,5\theta)\) | \(\theta\) | \(X_{(n)}/5\) (2020Q16) |
| \(U(\theta,\theta+1)\) | \(\theta\) | any value in \([X_{(n)}-1, X_{(1)}]\) — **not unique** (2023Q16) |
| \(U(\alpha,\beta)\) | \(\alpha,\beta\) | \(X_{(1)}, X_{(n)}\) (2021Q43) |
| \(U(-\theta,\theta)\) | \(\theta\) | \(\max|X_i|\) (2024Q32: \(\max(|7|,|-12|)=12\)) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) mean \(1/\lambda\) | \(\lambda\) | \(1/\bar{x}\) |
| \(f(x,\theta)=e^{-(x-\theta)}\) | \(\theta\) | \(X_{(1)} = \min\) (2017Q41, 2018Q28) |
| \(f=\theta x^{\theta-1}\) | \(\theta\) | \(-n/\sum\log x_i\) (2017Q52) |
| \(f=\frac{1}{\theta}e^{-x/\theta}\) | \(\theta\) | \(\bar{x}\) (MLE of median: \(\theta\ln 2\) by invariance — 2017Q44) |
| \(\text{Laplace}(\theta)\) | \(\theta\) | Sample median (2017Q30, 2024Q30) |
| \(B(n,p)\), \(m\) obs | \(p\) | \(\bar{x}/n = \sum x_i/(mn)\) (2020Q32) |
| \(\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\lambda)\) | \(\alpha,\lambda\) | System of equations (2019Q9) |
| Hardy-Weinberg | \(\theta\) | \((2n_1+n_2)/(2n)\) (2017Q25) |
| \(f(x)=2(x-\theta)/(1-\theta)^2\) | \(\theta\) | \(2X_{(1)}-1\) or similar (2018Q23: MLE = \(X_{(1)}\) or related) |
| \(U(\theta-1/2,\theta+1/2)\) | \(\theta\) | any point in \([X_{(n)}-1/2, X_{(1)}+1/2]\) (2020Q24, 2023Q8 MLE = midrange) |

**MLE invariance (2023Q18):** Statement I: \(\log T\) is MLE of \(\log\theta\) — **Invariance property** ✓. Statement II: distribution of MLE is asymptotically normal — **Asymptotic normality** ✓.

**PYQ (2023Q5 — constrained MLE):** \(f(x,\theta)=e^{-(x-\theta)}\), \(\theta\le3\). Unconstrained MLE \(=\min x_i = 4\). Since \(4>3\), constrained MLE \(= 3\). So MLE of \(e^\theta = e^3\).

**PYQ (2021Q32 — constrained MLE for Bernoulli):** \(p\in[a/(a+b), b/(a+b)]\). If \(\hat{p}\) unconstrained falls outside this interval, project onto boundary.

**PYQ (2021Q55,Q56 — MLE of \(\mu,\sigma\)):** For \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\): \(\hat{\mu}=\bar{x}\), \(\hat{\sigma}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}\).

**PYQ (2023Q9 — discrete parameter space):** \(N(\mu,1)\), \(\Omega=\{-2,-1,1,2\}\), \(\bar{x}=(4.5+3.8+2.5+5.2)/4=4\). MLE = arg max over \(\{-2,-1,1,2\}\): maximize \(L \propto e^{-n(\bar{x}-\mu)^2/2}\), so choose \(\mu\) closest to \(\bar{x}=4\): \(\hat{\mu}=2\).

## 6. Method of Moments

**Equate population moments to sample moments:**
\[
E(X^k) = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i^k, \quad k=1,2,...
\]
Solve for parameters. Gives **consistent** estimators always (2017Q60, 2023Q3 ✓).

**Standard MOM estimators:**
| Distribution | MOM |
|---|---|
| \(f(x)=(1+\theta)x^\theta\) | \(\hat{\theta} = \frac{2\bar{x}-1}{1-\bar{x}}\) (2017Q24: \(\bar{x}=0.6\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=0.5\)) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) mean \(1/\lambda\) | \(\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{x}\) (2017Q34) |
| \(\Gamma(\alpha,\beta)\) | \(\hat{\alpha}=\bar{x}^2/s^2\), \(\hat{\beta}=\bar{x}/s^2\) (2017Q64: \(m_1=10,m_2=150\Rightarrow\hat{\alpha}=2,\hat{\beta}=0.2\)) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\) | \(\hat{\theta}=2\bar{X}\) |
| \(U(\theta-1/2,\theta+1/2)\) | \(\hat{\theta}=\bar{X}\) |
| \(U(\theta,2\theta)\) | \(E(X)=3\theta/2\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=2\bar{x}/3\) (2025Q15: \(\bar{x}=1.15\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=0.767\)) |
| \(f=2(\theta-x)/\theta^2\) | \(E(X)=\theta/3\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=3\bar{X}\) (2020Q40) |
| \(f(x)=\theta x^{-\theta-1}\) | \(E(X)=\theta/(\theta-1)\Rightarrow\) solve for \(\hat{\theta}\) |
| Geometric \(f=\theta(1-\theta)^{x-1}\) | \(E(X)=1/\theta\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=1/\bar{X}\) (2021Q20: \(\bar{x}=4\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=1/4\)) |
| \(f=x e^{-x^2/(2\theta)}/\theta\) | \(E(X)=\sqrt{\pi\theta/2}\Rightarrow\hat{\theta}=2\bar{x}^2/\pi\) (2021Q37) |

**MOM vs MLE (2025Q38):** They are the same for exponential family when the natural sufficient statistic equals its mean.

---

## 7. UMVUE — Rao-Blackwell & Lehmann-Scheffé

### Rao-Blackwell Theorem
If \(T\) is unbiased for \(\psi(\theta)\) and \(S\) is sufficient for \(\theta\), then \(\phi(S) = E[T|S]\) satisfies:
\[
V(\phi(S)) \le V(T)
\]
The improved estimator \(\phi(S)\) is called the **Rao-Blackwellised** estimator (2017Q59, 2025Q44).

### Lehmann-Scheffé Theorem
If \(S\) is **complete sufficient** and \(\phi(S)\) is unbiased for \(\psi(\theta)\), then \(\phi(S)\) is the unique **UMVUE**.

**Key relationships (2021Q44):**
- MVBUE (attains CR bound) \(\Rightarrow\) UMVUE ✓ (Statement I)
- MVBUE must be sufficient statistic ✗ (Statement II — MVBUE need not be a sufficient statistic itself)
- UMVUE \(\not\Rightarrow\) MVBUE (Statement III ✗) — UMVUE may not attain CR bound

**PYQ (2021Q25):** CR inequality provides lower bound to variance of **any unbiased estimator** (not just sufficient) ✓. MVB estimator exists iff sufficient estimator exists ✓.

**Standard UMVUEs:**
| Model | Function | UMVUE |
|---|---|---|
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(e^{-\lambda}\) | \((1-1/n)^T\) (2021Q39, not attaining CR) |
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(e^{-2\lambda}\) | \(((n-2)/n)^T\) (2024Q16) |
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(P(X=k)=e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\) | \(\binom{T}{k}(1/n)^k(1-1/n)^{T-k}\) (2023Q24) |
| \(B(1,p)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(p^3\) | \(\frac{T(T-1)(T-2)}{n(n-1)(n-2)}\) (2018Q17) |
| \(B(1,p)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(p(1-p)\) | \(\frac{T(n-T)}{n(n-1)}\) (2023Q6 for \(\theta(1-\theta)\)) |
| \(B(2,p)\), \(T=\sum x_i\) | \(p^2\) | \(T(T-1)/(2n(2n-1))\) (2021Q27) |
| \(N(\mu,1)\), \(T=\bar{X}\) | \(\mu^2\) | \(\bar{X}^2 - 1/n\) (2020Q18) |
| \(N(0,\theta)\), \(T=\sum x_i^2\) | \(\theta^2\) | \(\frac{(\sum x_i^2)^2 - 2n\theta^2}{n(n+2)} =\) use \(E(\sum x_i^2)^2\) (2019Q40) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\), \(T=X_{(n)}\) | \(\theta^r\) | \(\frac{n+r}{n}X_{(n)}^r\) (2020Q33) |
| \(U(0,\theta)\), \(T=X_{(n)}\) | \(\theta^3\) | \(\frac{n+3}{n}X_{(n)}^3\) (2021Q22) |
| \(f(x)=\theta x^{\theta-1}\) | \(\theta\) | \((n-1)/(-\sum\log x_i) = \frac{n-1}{Z}\) (2017Q53) |
| \(\text{Exp}(\alpha)\) mean \(\alpha\) | \(\alpha\) | \(\bar{X}\) (2021Q15) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) both unknown | \(\mu,\sigma^2\) | \(\bar{X}\) and \(S^2=\frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n-1}\) (2024Q27) |
| Discrete Uniform \(\{1,...,N\}\) | \(N\) | \(\frac{n+1}{n}X_{(n)}-1\) (2022Q79,Q80) |

**UMVUE and Rao-Blackwell connection (2019Q43):** If \(T_1\) sufficient, \(T_2\) unbiased, then \(E(T_2|T_1)\) is an improved estimator with smaller variance.

**PYQ (2020Q12, 2021Q12):** \(U = 2\bar{X}\), \(T=X_{(n)}\) from \(U(0,\theta)\).
\(E[U|T=t]\): Given \(X_{(n)}=t\), remaining \(n-1\) order statistics are uniform on \((0,t)\) and the last is \(X_{(n)}=t\). \(E[\bar{X}|X_{(n)}=t] = \frac{(n-1)t/2+t}{n} = \frac{(n+1)t}{2n}\). So \(E[U|T=t] = 2\cdot\frac{(n+1)t}{2n} = \frac{(n+1)t}{n}\).

## 8. Confidence Intervals

### Pivotal Quantities
A **pivot** \(Q(X,\theta)\) has distribution free of \(\theta\).

| Situation | Pivot | CI |
|---|---|---|
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\sigma\) known | \(Z = \frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)\) | \(\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sigma/\sqrt{n}\) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\sigma\) unknown | \(t = \frac{\bar{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1}\) | \(\bar{x} \pm t_{n-1,\alpha/2}S/\sqrt{n}\) (2020Q67) |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(\mu\) known | \(\chi^2 = \sum(X_i-\mu)^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_n\) | \((2022Q60)\) |
| \(N(0,\sigma^2)\), sample | \(\sum x_i^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_n\) | for \(\sigma^2\) (2021Q16) |
| Large sample, \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\) mean \(1/\theta\) | \(2n\theta\bar{X} \sim \chi^2_{2n}\) | (2023Q12) |

**PYQ (2019Q12 — 95% CI for \(\mu\)):** \(n=64, \bar{x}=60.4, \sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2=6300\). \(S^2=6300/63=100\), \(S=10\). Large \(n\Rightarrow\) use \(Z\): \(60.4\pm1.96\times10/8 = 60.4\pm2.45\).

**PYQ (2021Q28 — Poisson CI):** \(n=64, \sum x_i=256\Rightarrow\bar{x}=4\). For large \(n\): \(\lambda\approx\bar{x}\pm z_{0.025}\sqrt{\bar{x}/n} = 4\pm1.96\sqrt{4/64} = 4\pm1.96\times0.25 = 4\pm0.49\).

**PYQ (2021Q46 — Poisson 95% CI large \(n\)):** \(\bar{x}\pm1.96\sqrt{\bar{x}/n}\).

**PYQ (2020Q34 — 98% CI for proportion):** \(n=500, \hat{p}=60/500=0.12\). \(SE=\sqrt{0.12\times0.88/500}=0.01453\). CI: \(12\%\pm2.33\times1.453\% = 12\%\pm3.39\%\) i.e., \((8.61\%, 15.39\%)\).

**PYQ (2018Q38 — 95% CI for proportion):** \(n=60, \hat{p}=40/60=2/3\). CI: \(2/3\pm1.96\sqrt{2/9\times60}\)... \(SE=\sqrt{(2/3)(1/3)/60}=\sqrt{2/540}=0.0609\). CI: \(0.667\pm0.119\).

**PYQ (2020Q61 — width when variance doubled):** Width \(\propto\sigma\). If \(\sigma^2\) doubled, \(\sigma\to\sqrt{2}\sigma\), width increases by factor \(\sqrt{2}\).

**PYQ (2020Q62 — sample mean from CI):** CI \((18000, 22000)\): midpoint \(= 20000 = \bar{x}\).

**PYQ (2025Q34):** 95% CI \((200,220)\): \(\bar{x} = (200+220)/2 = 210\).

**PYQ (2022Q16 — t-CI):** \(n=16, \bar{x}=40, s=8\). CI: \(40\pm t_{15,0.025}\times8/4 = 40\pm2.13\times2 = 40\pm4.26 = (35.74, 44.26)\).

**PYQ (2023Q47 — exponential shifted):** \(X_{(1)}-\theta \sim \text{Exp}(n)\). Pivot: \(n(X_{(1)}-\theta) \sim \text{Exp}(1)\).
\(P(\theta\in(x_{(1)}-\ln4/n, x_{(1)}+\ln2/n)\)) = \(P(-\ln2/n < \theta - x_{(1)} < \ln4/n)\) = \(P(e^{-\ln4} \lt e^{-n(x_{(1)}-\theta)} \lt e^{\ln2})\) = \(P(1/4 \lt e^{-W} \lt 2)\) where \(W\sim\text{Exp}(1)\). \(=P(-\ln2 \lt W \lt \ln4) = e^{-\ln(1/4)}-e^{-\ln4} = ... = 1/4+1/2 = 3/4\). Confidence coefficient = **3/4**.

**Shortest CI (2018Q40, 2020Q67):** For \(\mu\) with unknown \(\sigma^2\): use \(t_{n-1}\) distribution, **central** CI (equal tails) gives shortest expected length.

**PYQ (2025Q7):** If \(\sigma\) known: can choose \(n\) for fixed width ✓. If \(\sigma\) unknown: cannot pre-specify \(n\) for exact fixed width ✗ (but approximately possible). Statement II ✗.

**Bootstrap (2023Q13):** Number of distinct bootstrap samples with replacement of size \(n=5\) from \(n=5\): \(\binom{n+n-1}{n} = \binom{9}{5} = 126\).

## 9. Hypothesis Testing — Fundamentals

### Error Types & Test Properties
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Size (level) \(\alpha\) | \(P(\text{Reject }H_0 \mid H_0\text{ true}) = P(\text{Type I error})\) |
| Power \(= 1-\beta\) | \(P(\text{Reject }H_0 \mid H_1\text{ true})\) |
| Type II error \(\beta\) | \(P(\text{Accept }H_0 \mid H_1\text{ true})\) |
| Unbiased test | Power \(\ge\) size for all \(\theta\in\Theta_1\) (2020Q68, 2021Q47, 2025Q36) |
| Consistent test | Power \(\to 1\) as \(n\to\infty\) |

**PYQ (2017Q22):** Correct: Power \(=1-P(\text{Type II error})\) ✓; Size \(=P(\text{Type I error})\) ✓.

**Robustness (2017Q27):** t-test is robust to non-normality for large samples (\(n=100\) ✓); should NOT be used for small clearly skewed samples (\(n=10\) ✗). All three statements correct.

**Composite vs simple hypothesis (2019Q13, 2020Q35, 2020Q70):**
- Simple: parameter completely specified (one point)
- Composite: \(\Theta_0\) contains >1 point

**PYQ (2017Q45):** Critical region area depends on **level of significance \(\alpha\)**.

**PYQ (2020Q69):** Paired t-test with 10 pairs: df \(= n-1 = 9\). (2025Q35: 20 pairs \(\Rightarrow\) df=19).

### Neyman-Pearson Lemma
For testing \(H_0: f = f_0\) vs \(H_1: f=f_1\), the **most powerful test** of size \(\alpha\) rejects \(H_0\) when:
\[
\Lambda(x) = \frac{f_1(x)}{f_0(x)} \ge k
\]
where \(k\) is chosen so that \(P(\Lambda \ge k | H_0) = \alpha\) (2018Q42, 2024Q28).

**PYQ (2017Q36):** \(f(x,\theta)=\theta e^{-\theta x}\), \(H_0:\theta=1\) vs \(H_1:\theta=2\), CR: \(x\ge1\).
- Type I error: \(P(X\ge1|\theta=1) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368\)
- Type II error: \(P(X<1|\theta=2) = 1-e^{-2} \approx 0.865\)

**PYQ (2019Q14):** \(U(0,\theta)\), CR: \(x>1\), \(H_0:\theta=1\): Size \(= P(X>1|\theta=1) = 0\) (since \(U(0,1)\) cannot exceed 1). Size = 0.

**PYQ (2019Q15):** \(X,Y\sim U(0,\theta)\) independent, CR: \(X+Y>0.75\), \(H_0:\theta=1\) vs \(H_1:\theta=2\).
- Type I error: \(P(X+Y>0.75|\theta=1)\). \(P(X+Y\le t) = t^2/2\) for \(0\le t\le1\). \(P(X+Y>0.75)=1-0.75^2/2=1-0.28125=0.71875\).
- Power: \(P(X+Y>0.75|\theta=2)\). \(U(0,2)\) case: \(P(X+Y\le t) = t^2/8\) for \(0\le t\le 2\). \(P(X+Y>0.75) = 1-0.75^2/8 = 1-0.0703 = 0.9297\).

**PYQ (2019Q19 — BCR for \(N(\mu,1)\), \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) vs \(H_1:\mu>\mu_0\)):**
Likelihood ratio: \(\Lambda = \exp(n(\mu_1-\mu_0)\bar{x} - ...)\). Since \(\mu_1>\mu_0\), CR: \(\bar{X} > c\) where \(c = \mu_0 + z_\alpha/\sqrt{n}\).

**PYQ (2019Q20 — Exponential, \(H_1:\theta<\theta_0\)):** NP lemma gives CR: \(\bar{X} < c\) (reject for small \(\bar{X}\)).

**PYQ (2022Q19 — \(N(0,\sigma^2)\), \(H_1:\sigma<\sigma_0\)):** LR: \(\Lambda = (\sigma_0/\sigma_1)^n\exp(-\frac{1}{2}(1/\sigma_1^2-1/\sigma_0^2)\sum x_i^2)\). Since \(\sigma_1<\sigma_0\), reject for **large** \(\sum x_i^2\). CR: \(\sum x_i^2 > c\).

**PYQ (2025Q12):** \(H_0: f=x/2\) vs \(H_1: f=3x^2/8\) on \((0,2)\). LR \(= 3x/4\ge k\). CR: \(x>c\).
Size \(=P(X>c|H_0) = \int_c^2 x/2\,dx = (4-c^2)/4 = 0.19\Rightarrow c^2 = 3.24\Rightarrow c=1.8\).
Power \(=P(X>1.8|H_1) = \int_{1.8}^2 3x^2/8\,dx = [x^3/8]_{1.8}^2 = 1-5.832/8 = 1-0.729=0.271\).

**PYQ (2025Q13):** \(H_0: f=2x\) vs \(H_1:f=3x^2\) on \((0,1)\). LR \(=3x/2\ge k\Rightarrow x\ge c\).
Size: \(P(X>c|H_0)=\int_c^1 2x\,dx = 1-c^2=0.36\Rightarrow c=0.8\).
Power: \(P(X>0.8|H_1)=\int_{0.8}^1 3x^2\,dx = 1-0.512=0.488\) ✓. Test is unbiased since \(0.488>0.36\) ✓.

---

## 10. UMP Tests & MLR Property

### Uniformly Most Powerful (UMP) Tests
- UMP test exists for **one-sided alternatives** against exponential family distributions (MLR property)
- For **two-sided** alternatives: UMP generally does NOT exist for standard distributions (2017Q61)
- UMP \(\Rightarrow\) unbiased (2019Q17 ✓)
- Consistent test \(\not\Rightarrow\) UMP (2019Q17 ✗)
- UMP is unique (almost surely) (2017Q57)

**Monotone Likelihood Ratio (MLR) property:** Family \(f(x,\theta)\) has MLR in \(T(x)\) if \(f(x,\theta_1)/f(x,\theta_0)\) is monotone in \(T(x)\) for \(\theta_1>\theta_0\).

**PYQ (2023Q14):** MLR property: Bernoulli ✓, Poisson ✓, Cauchy ✗, Logistic ✓. Three distributions have MLR.

**PYQ (2019Q18 — \(U(0,\theta)\), \(H_0:\theta=2\)):**
- vs \(H_1:\theta>2\): UMP exists (reject for large \(X_{(n)}\))
- vs \(H_1:\theta\ne2\): same UMP (since power only improves for \(\theta>2\)). Both hypotheses give **same** UMP.

**UMPU test (2019Q63, 2023Q45):** For two-sided \(H_1:\theta\ne\theta_0\) in exponential family:
\[
\phi(x) = \begin{cases}1 & T < c_1 \text{ or } T > c_2 \\ 0 & c_1 < T < c_2\end{cases}
\]
where \(c_1, c_2\) determined by \(E_{\theta_0}[\phi] = \alpha\) and \(E_{\theta_0}[T\phi] = \alpha E_{\theta_0}[T]\) (unbiasedness condition).

**PYQ (2023Q45 — \(N(\theta,1)\)):** UMPU rejects for \(\sum x_i < n\theta_0 - z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{n}\) or \(>n\theta_0+z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{n}\), i.e., \(c_1 = n\theta_0 - z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{n}\), \(c_2 = n\theta_0+z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{n}\).

---

## 11. Likelihood Ratio Tests (LRT)

### Definition
\[
\lambda = \frac{\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_0} L(\theta)}{\sup_{\theta\in\Theta} L(\theta)}, \quad 0\le\lambda\le1
\]
Reject \(H_0\) when \(\lambda\) is small (i.e., \(\lambda \le \lambda_0\)).

**Wilks' theorem:** Under \(H_0\), \(-2\log\lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2(r)\) where \(r = \dim\Theta - \dim\Theta_0\) (2018Q45, 2021Q29, 2024Q29 ✓).

**Properties:**
- \(0\le\lambda\le1\) ✓ (2021Q29, 2024Q29 ✓)
- \(-2\log\lambda \sim \chi^2\) asymptotically ✓
- LRT is consistent ✓ (2021Q29, 2023Q50)
- LRT is a function of sufficient statistic (2024Q29 III ✓)
- For simple vs simple: LRT \(\equiv\) NP test (2019Q44, 2024Q29 I ✓)
- LRT is NOT always UMP (2023Q50 I ✗ — generally, but coincides for one-sided exponential family)

**PYQ (2018Q43 — \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\)):** LRT equivalent to **two-sample t-test** (i.e., \(t_{n-1}\) test).

**PYQ (2018Q44 — \(N(\mu_i,1)\), test all \(\mu_i=0\)):** LRT statistic: \(\sum X_i^2 \sim \chi^2(n)\) under \(H_0\).

**PYQ (2023Q29 — Poisson LRT):** LRT for one-sided \(H_1:\lambda>\lambda_0\) gives UMP ✓; for \(H_1:\lambda<\lambda_0\) gives UMP ✓; for two-sided gives UMPU ✓. All three statements correct.

**PYQ (2021Q50 — Poisson, 12 months, \(H_0:\lambda=2\) vs \(H_1:\lambda=3\)):**
LR \(= \frac{L(3)}{L(2)} = \frac{e^{-36}3^{\sum x_i}}{e^{-24}2^{\sum x_i}} = e^{-12}(3/2)^{\sum x_i}\).

**PYQ (2023Q46 — t-test via LRT):** \(n=9, \bar{x}=9.8, S=1.3\). Test \(H_0:\mu=8.8\) vs \(H_1:\mu>8.8\):
\(t = (9.8-8.8)/(1.3/3) = 1/(1.3/3) = 3/1.3 \approx 2.31\).

**PYQ (2024Q37 — LRT for \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), \(H_0:\mu=0\)):** LRT statistic \(=\sqrt{n}|\bar{x}|/S\sim t_{n-1}\) under \(H_0\). With \(n=4\): \(|\bar{x}|/S > t_{3,0.025}\). So \(k = t_{3,0.025}/\sqrt{n} = t_{3,0.025}/2\).

---

## 12. Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)

### Wald's SPRT
At stage \(m\), compute likelihood ratio:
\[
\Lambda_m = \prod_{i=1}^m \frac{f(x_i,\theta_1)}{f(x_i,\theta_0)}
\]

**Decision boundaries (2017Q39, 2017Q46, 2018Q46):**
\[
B = \frac{\beta}{1-\alpha} \le \Lambda_m \le A = \frac{1-\beta}{\alpha} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ Continue sampling}
\]
\[
\Lambda_m \ge A = \frac{1-\beta}{\alpha} \Rightarrow \text{ Reject } H_0
\]
\[
\Lambda_m \le B = \frac{\beta}{1-\alpha} \Rightarrow \text{ Accept } H_0
\]

**Properties:**
- Probability of SPRT terminating = 1 (2017Q65 ✓; Statement I: "sometimes provides a decision" ✗ — always terminates)
- SPRT is optimal (minimises expected sample size among all sequential tests with same error probabilities)
- \(E(N|H_0) < E(N|H_1)\) generally

**PYQ (2017Q47, 2018Q47):** N is stopping time; \(E(N)<\infty\) — finite expected stopping time.

**PYQ (2021Q14 — \(a_m=0.90+0.05m\), \(r_m=2.25+0.05m\)):** Data \(\{1,1,0,1,0,0\}\), \(s_6=3\). At \(m=6\): \(a_6=1.2, r_6=2.55\). Since \(s_6=3>2.55\), reject \(H_0\).

**SPRT for Normal mean (2021Q30 — \(f=N(\mu,4)\)):**
Log LR: \(\ell_m = \sum_{i=1}^m x_i\cdot\frac{\mu_1-\mu_0}{\sigma^2} - \frac{m(\mu_1^2-\mu_0^2)}{2\sigma^2}\)
For \(H_0:\mu=4\) vs \(H_1:\mu=6\), \(\sigma^2=4\): \(\ell_m = \frac{s_m}{2} - \frac{5m}{4}\). Boundaries on \(s_m\).

**OC function (2023Q30):** Under SPRT, OC function \(L(\theta)\) (probability of accepting \(H_0\)):
\[
L(\theta) = \frac{A^{h(\theta)}-1}{A^{h(\theta)}-B^{h(\theta)}}
\]
where \(h(\theta)\) satisfies \(E(e^{Zh(\theta)})=1\), \(Z=\log(f_1/f_0)\).
Under \(H_0\): \(L(\theta_0) = 1-\alpha\) (approximate). Under \(H_1\): \(L(\theta_1) = \beta\).

**PYQ (2024Q19 — SPRT, \(\text{Exp}(\theta)\), \(H_0:\theta=1\) vs \(H_1:\theta=2\), \(\alpha=\beta=0.1\)):**
\(A=0.9/0.1=9\), \(B=0.1/0.9=1/9\). LR after one obs \(x_1\): \(\Lambda_1 = 2e^{-x_1}\). Continue if \(1/9 < 2e^{-x_1} < 9\), i.e., \(\ln(2/9) < -x_1 < \ln(9/2)\), i.e., \(x_1 \in (0, \ln(9/2)) \approx (0, 1.504)\).
\(P(\text{no decision}|H_0) = P(X_1 \in (0,1.504)|\theta=1) = 1-e^{-1.504} \approx 0.778\).

---

## 13. Bayesian Estimation

### Bayes Estimator
- Under **squared error loss** \(L(\theta,d)=(\theta-d)^2\): Bayes estimator \(= E(\theta|x)\) = posterior mean (2017Q50, 2018Q50)
- Under **absolute error loss** \(L(\theta,d)=|\theta-d|\): Bayes estimator \(=\) posterior median (2020Q10)
- Under **0-1 loss**: Bayes estimator \(=\) posterior mode

**Risk function:** \(R(\theta,\delta) = E[L(\theta,\delta(X))]\) = MSE when \(L = (\theta-d)^2\) (2017Q48, 2018Q48)

**Bayes risk:** \(r(\delta) = E_\theta[R(\theta,\delta)] = \int R(\theta,\delta)\pi(\theta)d\theta\)

**PYQ (2017Q49, 2018Q49):** \(X|\mu \sim N(\mu,1)\), prior \(\mu\sim N(0,1)\). Posterior \(\mu|X \sim N(\bar{x}n/(n+1), 1/(n+1))\). Bayes estimator \(= E(\mu|x) = n\bar{x}/(n+1)\). Bayes risk = \(E[V(\mu|x)] + V[E(\mu|x)] = ?\) Use \(r = 1/(n+1)\).

**Minimax (2024Q35):**
- Bayes estimator with constant risk is minimax ✓ (Statement I)
- Unique minimax must be admissible ✓ (Statement II)

---

## 14. MSE, Bias-Variance Decomposition

\[
\text{MSE}(T) = V(T) + [\text{Bias}(T)]^2
\]

**For unbiased estimator:** MSE = Variance.

**PYQ (2024Q1,Q2):**
- Statement I (Q1): Concentration high when MSE low is true; converse not necessarily true ✓.
- Statement II (Q1): No estimator with uniformly minimum MSE for all \(\theta\) ✓.
- For unbiased: MSE = Variance ✓ (Q2, Statement I). For biased: MSE \(\ne\) proportional to bias (Statement II ✗).

**PYQ (2023Q27 — MSE of \(\hat{\sigma}^2=\frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n}\)):**
\(\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{n-1}{n}S^2\). \(\text{Bias} = \sigma^2/n\). \(V(\hat{\sigma}^2) = \frac{(n-1)^2}{n^2}\cdot\frac{2\sigma^4}{n-1} = \frac{2(n-1)\sigma^4}{n^2}\). MSE \(= \frac{2(n-1)\sigma^4}{n^2} + \frac{\sigma^4}{n^2} = \frac{(2n-1)\sigma^4}{n^2}\).

**PYQ (2020Q39):** \(\hat{\theta}_1 = \bar{X}/n\) (NOT \(\bar{X}\) for Binomial \(B(1,p)\)... this is a specific PYQ context). \(\hat{\theta}_2=1/3\). MSE\((\hat{\theta}_2) = (1/3-p)^2 < \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_1)\)? Solve for \(n\).

**PYQ (2020Q38 — efficiency of \(2\bar{X}\) vs \(\frac{n+1}{n}X_{(n)}\) for \(U(0,\theta)\)):**
\(V(2\bar{X}) = 4\cdot\frac{\theta^2/12}{n} = \theta^2/(3n)\). \(V(\frac{n+1}{n}X_{(n)}) = \frac{\theta^2}{n(n+2)}\). Efficiency \(= \frac{\theta^2/(n(n+2))}{\theta^2/(3n)} = \frac{3}{n+2}\).

---

## 15. Ancillary Statistics & Basu's Theorem

**Ancillary statistic:** Distribution is free of \(\theta\) (2019Q49).

**Basu's theorem:** If \(S\) is complete sufficient and \(A\) is ancillary, then \(S\) and \(A\) are **independent** (2019Q48).

**PYQ (2024Q5):** \(U(\theta-1/2,\theta+1/2)\): Range \(W = X_{(n)}-X_{(1)}\) is ancillary (distribution free of \(\theta\)). Both statements I and II are correct.

**PYQ (2025Q8):** \(T = T_2-T_1 = X_{(n)}-X_{(1)}\) for \(U(\theta-1/2,\theta+1/2)\) is ancillary, hence NOT sufficient (Statement I ✗) and NOT complete (Statement II ✗).

**PYQ (2025Q41):** Minimal sufficient is NOT always complete ✓ (counterexample: location family with two-point prior). Statement II: A statistic independent of every ancillary need not be complete ✓ (without completeness assumption, Basu's theorem converse fails).

---

## 16. Key Summary Tables

### Properties of Standard Estimators
| Model | \(\hat{\theta}\) | Unbiased | Consistent | Sufficient | UMVUE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| \(\text{Poi}(\lambda)\): \(\bar{X}\) | \(\lambda\) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| \(U(0,\theta)\): \(X_{(n)}\) | \(\theta\) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| \(U(0,\theta)\): \(\frac{n+1}{n}X_{(n)}\) | \(\theta\) | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ |
| \(U(0,\theta)\): \(2\bar{X}\) | \(\theta\) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\): \(\bar{X}\) | \(\mu\) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\): \(\frac{1}{n}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2\) | \(\sigma^2\) | ✗ | ✓ | — | ✗ |
| \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\): \(S^2=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2\) | \(\sigma^2\) | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ |
| Cauchy: \(\bar{X}\) | \(\theta\) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Cauchy: Sample median | \(\theta\) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | — |

### Error Relationships
\[
\text{Size} = \alpha = P(\text{Type I}), \quad \text{Power} = 1-\beta, \quad \beta = P(\text{Type II})
\]
\[
\text{Unbiased test}: \text{Power}(\theta) \ge \alpha \; \forall\,\theta\in\Theta_1
\]

### MLE Invariance Examples
- \(\hat{\mu}_{MLE}\) of \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) is \(\bar{x}\), so MLE of \(e^\mu = e^{\bar{x}}\)
- \(\hat{\theta}_{MLE}\) of Exp = \(1/\bar{x}\), so MLE of median \(=\theta\ln2=\ln2/\bar{x}\cdot\bar{x}=\bar{x}\ln2\)
- MLE of \(\theta^2\) from \(U(0,\theta)\) \(= X_{(n)}^2\) (biased, by invariance)

> **ISS Tips — Statistical Inference:**
1. **Unbiasedness traps:** \(T\) unbiased for \(\theta\) \(\not\Rightarrow\) \(T^2\) unbiased for \(\theta^2\); never assume this.
2. **CRLB doesn't exist for \(U(0,\theta)\)** — regularity conditions fail (support depends on \(\theta\)).
3. **Efficiency and correlation:** \(\text{eff}(T_2) = e \Leftrightarrow \rho(T_1,T_2) = \sqrt{e}\). Memorise.
4. **Sufficient statistics for standard families:** Poisson \(\to \sum x_i\); \(U(0,\theta)\to X_{(n)}\); \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\to (\bar{x}, S^2)\); exponential \(\to \bar{x}\).
5. **MLE is BIASED for \(U(0,\theta)\) and \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) variance** — both classic examples.
6. **UMVUE route:** Complete sufficient \(S\) + any unbiased estimator \(T\) \(\Rightarrow\) \(E[T|S]\) is UMVUE.
7. **\(-2\log\lambda \sim \chi^2(r)\)** asymptotically (Wilks), df = number of constraints tested.
8. **SPRT boundaries:** \(A = (1-\beta)/\alpha\), \(B = \beta/(1-\alpha)\). Reject \(H_0\) at upper boundary.
9. **Bayes under SEL = posterior mean; under AEL = posterior median.**
10. **Sample median efficiency = \(2/\pi \approx 0.637\) for normal** — comes up in efficiency comparisons.

inference.tips:
**High-priority topics for ISS Statistical Inference section (Paper II):**

- **MLE properties:** Consistency, asymptotic normality, invariance (\(g(\hat{\theta})\) is MLE of \(g(\theta)\)), asymptotic efficiency — all guaranteed MCQs
- **MLE of \(\sigma^2\):** Uses divisor \(n\) (biased); unbiased estimator uses \(n-1\) — classic MCQ trap
- **CRLB:** \(\text{Var}(T) \ge 1/[nI(\theta)]\) for unbiased \(T\); does NOT apply when support depends on \(\theta\) (e.g., \(U(0,\theta)\))
- **Fisher information:** \(I(\theta) = E[S^2] = -E[\partial^2\ln f/\partial\theta^2]\); for Normal: \(I(\theta) = 1/\sigma^2\)
- **Factorisation theorem:** \(f(\mathbf{x};\theta) = g(T(\mathbf{x}),\theta)\cdot h(\mathbf{x})\) — practise for all standard distributions
- **UMVUE strategy:** Complete sufficient statistic + Rao–Blackwell + Lehmann–Scheffé
- **Rao–Blackwell:** \(E(U|T)\) is at least as good as \(U\); reduces variance without bias
- **NP Lemma:** MP test rejects when \(L(\theta_1)/L(\theta_0) > k\) — simple vs simple hypotheses only
- **UMP test:** Exists for one-sided alternatives in MLR families (Karlin–Rubin); does NOT exist for two-sided alternatives in general
- **Wilks' theorem:** \(-2\ln\Lambda \sim \chi^2_r\) where \(r\) = number of restrictions
- **SPRT boundaries:** \(A = \beta/(1-\alpha)\), \(B = (1-\beta)/\alpha\); ASN at \(H_0\) and \(H_1\) both minimised simultaneously (Wald–Wolfowitz)
- **OC function:** Probability of ACCEPTING \(H_0\); ASN = expected sample size
- **Bayes estimators:** SEL → posterior mean; absolute loss → posterior median; 0-1 → posterior mode
- **Bhattacharya bounds:** Order-1 = CRLB; increasing order gives tighter bounds
- **Bootstrap:** Resamples WITH replacement; bias = \(\bar{\theta}^* - \hat{\theta}\)
- **Jackknife:** Leave-one-out; bias = \((n-1)(\bar{\theta}_{(.)} - \hat{\theta})\) — the \((n-1)\) factor is tested directly
- **Type I vs II:** Decreasing \(\alpha\) without increasing \(n\) always increases \(\beta\)
- **Similar region:** Power function = \(\alpha\) (constant) on \(H_0\), not just \(\le \alpha\)

official.title: Official Statistics

sections.label: Nature, Need & Principles of Official Statistics
sections.content:
## Official Statistics — Nature, Need & Principles

### What are Official Statistics?

**Official Statistics** are quantitative and qualitative data produced by national governments and international organisations to describe the economic, demographic, social, and environmental phenomena of a country.

**Key distinction:** Official statistics are produced by or on behalf of governments — they have an **obligation of public trust**. They differ from academic or private statistics in terms of mandatory reporting powers, neutrality, and public dissemination.

---

### Need for Official Statistics

- **Policy formulation:** Budgets, Five-Year Plans (now NITI Aayog strategies), welfare schemes, and fiscal policy all depend on reliable data
- **Monitoring & evaluation:** Tracking progress of schemes (e.g., poverty reduction, healthcare coverage)
- **Resource allocation:** Union Budget transfers, grants-in-aid to states
- **International obligations:** IMF (SDDS/GDDS), UN System of National Accounts (SNA), ILO conventions
- **Public accountability:** Parliament, CAG, judiciary, and citizens use official data to hold government accountable
- **Research & planning:** Academic institutions, businesses, NGOs

---

### Users of Official Statistics

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Government** | Cabinet, Parliament, Planning bodies, Ministries |
| **Central Banks** | RBI — monetary policy, credit data |
| **International bodies** | IMF, World Bank, UN agencies, WTO |
| **Private sector** | Industry associations, corporates, market researchers |
| **Academic & research** | Universities, think tanks, ISI, IGIDR |
| **Civil society** | NGOs, media, citizens, courts |

---

### UN Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics (1994, reaffirmed 2013)

Adopted by the UN Statistical Commission, there are **10 Fundamental Principles**:

1. **Relevance, impartiality, equal access** — statistics serve all of society; released impartially to all users simultaneously
2. **Professional standards, scientific methods** — methods chosen on professional, not political, grounds
3. **Accountability and transparency** — sources, methods, and procedures disclosed
4. **Prevention of misuse** — statistics agencies entitled to comment on misinterpretations
5. **Sources of official statistics** — may be administrative records, censuses, surveys
6. **Confidentiality** — individual data protected; used only for statistical purposes
7. **Legislation** — national statistics law supports quality and professionalism
8. **National coordination** — avoid duplication; national agency coordinates the system
9. **Use of international standards** — for international comparability
10. **International cooperation** — bilateral and multilateral statistical cooperation

---

### Desirable Properties of Official Statistics

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Relevance** | Meets actual user needs |
| **Reliability** | Accurate and consistent; low sampling and non-sampling errors |
| **Timeliness** | Released close to the reference period |
| **Accessibility** | Freely available to all users simultaneously |
| **Comparability** | Consistent over time and across regions/countries |
| **Coherence** | Different datasets can be combined meaningfully |
| **Transparency** | Methods, sources, and limitations fully disclosed |

---

### Limitations of Official Statistics

- **Coverage gaps:** Large informal sector; unorganised economy not fully captured
- **Non-sampling errors:** Response errors, enumerator bias, misreporting
- **Timeliness vs accuracy trade-off:** Advance estimates released quickly are revised later
- **Political pressure:** Risk of data manipulation or suppression (e.g., 2017-18 Consumer Expenditure Survey not released by NSSO)
- **Definitional issues:** Different ministries may use different definitions for the same concept
- **Under-coverage of women's work:** Unpaid domestic work excluded from GDP

---

### Compilation, Collection, Processing and Dissemination

**Collection methods:**
- Complete enumeration (Census)
- Sample surveys (PLFS, HCES, NFHS, NSS rounds)
- Administrative records (GST, MCA-21, PFMS, e-Vahan)
- Remote sensing and satellite data (crop area estimates)

**Processing:** Editing → validation → imputation for missing data → tabulation → estimation → dissemination

**Dissemination channels:**
- MoSPI website (mospi.gov.in)
- Press releases via PIB
- Statistical Year Book India
- Advance Release Calendars (ARC) published in advance
- Open Government Data Platform (data.gov.in)

> **ISS tip:** The 10 UN Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics are a direct MCQ source — especially Principle 1 (impartiality, equal access) and Principle 6 (confidentiality). Know the distinction between sampling and non-sampling errors. Advance Release Calendar ensures equal access to all users simultaneously.

sections.label: National Statistical Organisation — NSO, CSO & NSSO
sections.content:
## National Statistical Organisation — NSO, CSO & NSSO

### Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI)

**Formed:** 1999 by merging the Department of Statistics and Department of Programme Implementation.

**Two wings:**
1. **Statistics Wing (National Statistical Office — NSO):** Comprises the former CSO, NSSO, and Computer Centre
2. **Programme Implementation Wing:** Three divisions — (i) Twenty Point Programme (TPP), (ii) Infrastructure Monitoring & Project Monitoring, (iii) Member of Parliament Local Area Development Scheme (MPLADS)

**Also under MoSPI:**
- **National Statistical Commission (NSC)** — oversight body (autonomous)
- **Indian Statistical Institute (ISI), Kolkata** — institute of national importance by Act of Parliament

---

### National Statistical Office (NSO)

**Formed:** 23 May 2019 — by merging NSSO and CSO under a single umbrella.

**Head:** Secretary, Statistics and Programme Implementation (who is also the Chief Statistician of India).

**Structure:** Director Generals (DGs) head various divisions and report to the Secretary.

---

### Central Statistics Office (CSO) — Now part of NSO

**Established:** 1951 (reorganised from earlier bodies)

**Headquarters:** New Delhi (Sardar Patel Bhawan)

**Key functions (now NSO functions):**

| Function | Details |
|---|---|
| **National Accounts** | GDP, GVA, GNP, NNP — production, income and expenditure approaches |
| **IIP** | Index of Industrial Production — monthly; base year now 2022-23 (earlier 2011-12) |
| **CPI (Urban)** | Consumer Price Index for Urban areas — MoSPI; base year being revised to 2024 (from 2012) |
| **Statistical standards** | Evolves and maintains national statistical standards, concepts, and classifications |
| **State statistics development** | Assists states and UTs in developing their statistical systems |
| **Annual Survey of Industries (ASI)** | Conducted by Industrial Statistics wing of CSO; covers organised manufacturing sector |
| **Economic Census** | Complete enumeration of all establishments; last: 7th Economic Census (2019) |
| **Statistical Year Book India** | Annual flagship publication |
| **MCA-21** | Uses Ministry of Corporate Affairs data for corporate sector GDP estimation |

**Key publications (CSO/NSO):**
- National Accounts Statistics (NAS) — annual
- Statistical Year Book India — annual
- Monthly Abstract of Statistics
- Index of Industrial Production (IIP) — monthly
- Energy Statistics India — annual
- Women and Men in India — annual (gender statistics)
- Millennium Development Goals India Country Report

---

### National Sample Survey Office (NSSO) — Now part of NSO

**Established:** 1950 (originally as National Sample Survey Directorate, Finance Ministry; moved to Cabinet Secretariat 1957; under MoSPI since 1999)

**Headquarters:** New Delhi (with major offices in Kolkata and Faridabad)

**Merged into NSO:** 23 May 2019

**Four Divisions of NSSO:**

| Division | Function |
|---|---|
| **Survey Design & Research Division (SDRD)** | Technical survey planning, sampling design, schedules, tabulation frameworks; based in Kolkata |
| **Field Operations Division (FOD)** | Field data collection through zonal, regional, and sub-regional offices across India |
| **Data Processing Division (DPD)** | Data processing, validation, software development, tabulation; based in Kolkata and Faridabad |
| **Coordination & Publication Division (CPD)** | Publication of reports; released the journal *Sarvekshana* (quarterly) |

**Key surveys conducted by NSSO (now NSO):**

| Survey | Content | Periodicity |
|---|---|---|
| **NSS Rounds** (socio-economic) | Consumer Expenditure, Employment-Unemployment, Housing, Health, etc. | Quinquennial (5-yearly) for major; annual |
| **Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES)** | Formerly Consumer Expenditure Survey; 2022-23 results published | ~5 years |
| **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** | Employment, unemployment, wages | Annual (since 2017-18); quarterly for urban |
| **Annual Survey of Industries (ASI)** | Organised manufacturing sector performance | Annual |
| **MSME survey** | Unorganised manufacturing & services | Periodic |

**Staff:** Members of Indian Statistical Service (ISS — appointed by UPSC) and Subordinate Statistical Service (SSS — by SSC).

---

### Vision and Mission of MoSPI/NSO

**Vision:** To be a premier, credible and globally comparable national statistical system that serves the entire spectrum of users and helps in policy-making and democratic governance.

**Mission:** To develop an integrated and comprehensive statistical system for the country through coordination, standardisation, training, and international cooperation in the field of statistics.

> **ISS tip:** NSSO was established in **1950**; NSO formed on **23 May 2019** by merging NSSO + CSO. The four NSSO divisions (SDRD, FOD, DPD, CPD) are a direct MCQ target. Economic Census (complete enumeration of all establishments) is different from Population Census (persons). ASI covers the **organised** manufacturing sector; unorganised sector is covered through MSME surveys.

sections.label: National Statistical Commission & Legal Framework
sections.content:
## National Statistical Commission & Legal Framework

### National Statistical Commission (NSC)

**Background:** The Rangarajan Commission reviewed the Indian Statistical System in **2001** and recommended setting up a permanent NSC.

**Setup:** Government of India Resolution dated **1 June 2005** (Gazette Notification No. 85). NSC constituted with effect from **12 July 2006** (Notification No. R-16025/3/2005-NSC).

**Nature:** Autonomous body under MoSPI — not a statutory body (no Act of Parliament), created by executive resolution.

**Composition:**
- 1 part-time **Chairperson** — eminent statistician or social scientist; status of Minister of State
- 4 part-time **Members** (one each for): economic statistics; social & environment statistics; statistical operations; national accounts/statistical modelling — status equivalent to Secretary to GoI
- 1 **ex-officio Member** (Senior official, e.g., CEO NITI Aayog or equivalent)
- **Chief Statistician of India (CSI)** = Secretary of the Commission (also Secretary MoSPI)

**Tenure:** Chairperson — 3 years or till age 70; Members — 3 years or till age 65; CSI — 5 years or till age 62.

**Security of tenure:** Removal only by President after Supreme Court inquiry under Article 145.

**Current Chairperson (as of 2024):** Prof. Rajeeva Laxman Karandikar (appointed 30 Nov 2022).

---

### Functions of the NSC

1. Identify **core statistics** of national importance critical to economic development
2. Constitute **professional committees/working groups** on technical issues
3. Evolve **national policies and priorities** for the statistical system
4. Evolve standard **statistical concepts, definitions, classifications** and national quality standards
5. Evolve national strategies for **collection, tabulation and dissemination** of core statistics, including release calendars
6. Evolve strategies for **human resource development** in official statistics
7. **Improve data quality** and work with central and state agencies for implementation
8. Act as a **nodal body** for the Indian statistical system

---

### Need for NSC

- Eliminate fragmentation and duplication across ministries
- Ensure **independence** of statistical system from political pressure
- Set uniform standards for all official data producers
- Serve as a single apex body coordinating the entire national statistical system
- Provide oversight for NSSO surveys (now NSO surveys)

---

### Legal Framework — Key Acts

**1. Collection of Statistics Act, 2008**

The most important legislation for official statistics in India.

| Feature | Details |
|---|---|
| **Enacted** | 2008 (replaces the earlier Collection of Statistics Act, 1953) |
| **Purpose** | Empowers central and state governments to collect statistics |
| **Coverage** | Economic, social, demographic, scientific, and other activities |
| **Mandatory reporting** | Statistics officers have powers to collect data from establishments; penalties for non-compliance |
| **Confidentiality** | Individual data collected under the Act is confidential; cannot be disclosed |
| **Offences** | Willful non-compliance, furnishing false information — punishable with fine/imprisonment |
| **Schedule** | Lists subjects on which statistics may be collected |

**2. Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969**
- Mandates registration of births and deaths
- Civil Registration System (CRS) under Registrar General of India (RGI)
- Vital Statistics: Birth rate, death rate, IMR, MMR computed from CRS data

**3. Census Act, 1948**
- Legal basis for decennial Population Census
- Conducted under the Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India
- Census data is confidential (not used for taxation or prosecution)

**4. Indian Statistical Institute Act, 1959**
- Declares ISI Kolkata as an Institution of National Importance
- Empowers ISI to grant degrees in statistics

**5. Drugs and Cosmetics Act / Food Safety Act (sector specific)**
- Various sector-specific acts also mandate data collection (health statistics)

---

### Conference of Central and State Statistical Organisations (COCSSO)

- Annual conference bringing together MoSPI and state Directorates of Economics and Statistics (DES)
- Coordinates statistical activities; harmonises state and national standards
- Chaired by Chief Statistician of India

---

### International Statistical Framework

- **IMF Special Data Dissemination Standard (SDDS):** India is a subscriber — commits to transparency in data release
- **IMF General Data Dissemination System (GDDS)/e-GDDS:** Framework for countries building statistical capacity
- **System of National Accounts (SNA 2008):** UN standard that India follows for GDP compilation
- **International Labour Organisation (ILO):** Standards for employment and labour statistics
- **UN Statistical Commission:** India is a member; adopted the 10 Fundamental Principles

> **ISS tip:** NSC was set up by **executive resolution** (not by Act of Parliament) — hence not a statutory body. The **Collection of Statistics Act, 2008** is the primary legislation for data collection. **Census Act, 1948** is the legal basis for Population Census. NSC members can be removed only by the **President** after a Supreme Court inquiry — the security of tenure clause is a direct MCQ.

sections.label: Index Numbers — Types, Construction & Key Indices
sections.content:
## Index Numbers — Types, Construction & Key Indices in India

### What is an Index Number?

An index number measures the **relative change** in a variable (or a group of variables) over time or across space, expressed relative to a **base period** value of 100.

\[
\text{Simple Index} = \frac{P_1}{P_0} \times 100
\]

---

### Types of Index Numbers

**1. By subject:**
- **Price indices:** WPI, CPI, PPI
- **Quantity indices:** IIP (index of output volumes)
- **Value indices:** combine price and quantity changes

**2. By construction method:**

| Index | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Laspeyre's** | \(L = \frac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times 100\) — base-period weights |
| **Paasche's** | \(P = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_1} \times 100\) — current-period weights |
| **Fisher's Ideal** | \(F = \sqrt{L \times P}\) — geometric mean of Laspeyre and Paasche |
| **Marshall–Edgeworth** | \(\frac{\sum p_1(q_0+q_1)}{\sum p_0(q_0+q_1)} \times 100\) — average weights |
| **Dorbisch–Bowley** | Arithmetic mean of Laspeyre and Paasche |
| **Walsh** | \(\frac{\sum p_1\sqrt{q_0 q_1}}{\sum p_0\sqrt{q_0 q_1}} \times 100\) — geometric average weights |

**Tests for index numbers:**

| Test | Statement | Passes |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit test** | Index unaffected by change of units | All indices |
| **Time reversal test** | \(P_{01} \times P_{10} = 1\) | Fisher ✓, Laspeyre ✗, Paasche ✗ |
| **Factor reversal test** | \(P_{01} \times Q_{01} = V_{01}\) | Fisher ✓ only |
| **Circular test** | \(P_{01} \times P_{12} \times P_{20} = 1\) | Only simple geometric mean ✗ for weighted |

**Fisher's index is called "Ideal"** because it satisfies both the Time Reversal Test and Factor Reversal Test.

---

### Key Price Indices in India

#### 1. Wholesale Price Index (WPI)

| Feature | Details |
|---|---|
| **Measures** | Average price change of goods at the wholesale/producer level |
| **Current base year** | 2011-12 = 100 (new series, effective April 2017); revision to 2022-23 in progress |
| **Compiled by** | Office of Economic Adviser (OEA), DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry |
| **Periodicity** | Monthly (released on the 14th of every month, 2-week lag) |
| **Commodities** | 697 items in 3 groups: Primary Articles (22.6%), Fuel & Power (13.2%), Manufactured Products (64.2%) |
| **Uses** | GDP deflator; inflation indicator (historically); escalation clauses in contracts |
| **Covers** | Only goods — not services |
| **Formula** | Modified Laspeyre's formula |

**WPI three major groups (Base: 2011-12):**
- Primary Articles: Weight 22.62% (food articles, non-food articles, minerals)
- Fuel & Power: Weight 13.15%
- Manufactured Products: Weight 64.23%

**Note:** From 2013 onwards, CPI has replaced WPI as the **headline inflation indicator** for monetary policy. WPI now serves primarily as a **GDP deflator**.

---

#### 2. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Four series of CPI currently compiled in India:

| Series | Compiled by | Base year | Coverage | Periodicity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPI (Rural)** | MoSPI/NSO | 2012=100 (revision to 2024 in progress) | Rural India | Monthly |
| **CPI (Urban)** | MoSPI/NSO | 2012=100 | Urban India | Monthly |
| **CPI (Combined)** | MoSPI/NSO | 2012=100 | All India | Monthly (released ~12th) |
| **CPI (Industrial Workers) — CPI-IW** | Labour Bureau, Min. of Labour | 2016=100 | Industrial workers in 88 centres | Monthly |
| **CPI (Agricultural Labourers) — CPI-AL** | Labour Bureau | 2019=100 | Agricultural labourers | Monthly |
| **CPI (Rural Labourers) — CPI-RL** | Labour Bureau | 2019=100 | Rural labourers | Monthly |

**CPI (Combined) is the headline inflation index** — used by RBI for inflation targeting (target: 4% ± 2%).

**Major groups in CPI (Combined), 2012 base:**
- Food & Beverages: 45.86%
- Miscellaneous: 28.32%
- Housing: 10.07%
- Fuel & Light: 6.84%
- Clothing & Footwear: 6.53%
- Pan, Tobacco & Intoxicants: 2.38%

**CPI-IW** used for: Dearness Allowance (DA) to central government employees and pension revision.

---

#### 3. Index of Industrial Production (IIP)

| Feature | Details |
|---|---|
| **Measures** | Quantum of production in manufacturing, mining, and electricity sectors |
| **Compiled by** | NSO, MoSPI |
| **Current base year** | 2011-12=100; revision to 2022-23 in progress |
| **Periodicity** | Monthly (released with 6-week lag, on ~28th of following month) |
| **Coverage** | 407 item groups across 3 sectors |
| **Sectors and weights (2011-12 base)** | Manufacturing: 77.63%; Mining: 14.37%; Electricity: 7.99% |
| **Formula** | Laspeyre's formula |
| **Use-based classification** | Primary goods, Capital goods, Intermediate goods, Infrastructure/Construction goods, Consumer durables, Consumer non-durables |

---
