#### 4. Producer Price Index (PPI) — Proposed

MoSPI plans to introduce PPI to replace WPI; measures prices received by domestic producers. Unlike WPI, PPI covers both goods and services and excludes taxes.

---

### Human Development Index (HDI)

- Published by UNDP in annual Human Development Report
- Composite index of: Life Expectancy Index + Education Index + GNI per capita Index
- \(HDI = (\text{LEI} \times \text{EI} \times \text{GNII})^{1/3}\) (geometric mean since 2010)
- India's HDI rank: 134 out of 193 (2024 Human Development Report, value = 0.644)

---

### Sensex and Nifty

| Feature | Sensex | Nifty 50 |
|---|---|---|
| Full name | S&P BSE Sensex | NSE Nifty 50 |
| Exchange | Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) | National Stock Exchange (NSE) |
| No. of companies | 30 | 50 |
| Base year | 1978-79 = 100 | 3 Nov 1995 = 1000 |
| Method | Free-float market capitalisation | Free-float market capitalisation |

> **ISS tip:** Fisher's Ideal index satisfies both **Time Reversal Test** and **Factor Reversal Test** — only Fisher satisfies both simultaneously. WPI compiled by **DPIIT/OEA** (Ministry of Commerce), NOT MoSPI. CPI (Combined) compiled by **MoSPI**; CPI-IW by **Labour Bureau**. WPI base: **2011-12**; CPI base: **2012=100** (revision to 2024 ongoing); IIP base: **2011-12** (revision to 2022-23 ongoing).

sections.label: National Accounts — GDP, GNP & Key Concepts
sections.content:
## National Accounts Statistics

### Basic Concepts

**Gross Domestic Product (GDP):** Total monetary value of all **final goods and services** produced within the **domestic territory** of a country during a given period (usually one financial year).

**Three approaches to GDP (all yield the same result):**

| Approach | Method |
|---|---|
| **Production/Output** | Sum of Gross Value Added (GVA) at basic prices + taxes on products − subsidies on products |
| **Income** | Sum of factor incomes: Compensation of Employees + Operating Surplus + Mixed Income + taxes − subsidies |
| **Expenditure** | GDP = PFCE + GFCE + GFCF + Change in Stocks (CIS) + Valuables + Exports − Imports |

**GDP = GVA at Basic Prices + Taxes on Products − Subsidies on Products**

---

### Key Aggregates

| Aggregate | Definition |
|---|---|
| **GDP** | Value of final output in domestic territory |
| **GNP** | GDP + Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA) |
| **NNP** | GNP − Depreciation (Consumption of Fixed Capital, CFC) |
| **NDP** | GDP − Depreciation |
| **National Income (NI)** | NNP at factor cost = NNP at market prices − Net indirect taxes |
| **Personal Income (PI)** | NI − Undistributed profits − Corporate taxes − Social security + Transfer payments |
| **Disposable Personal Income** | PI − Personal taxes |
| **Per capita income** | NI / Mid-year population |

**Key relationship:**
\[
\text{GDP (Market Price)} = \text{GDP (Factor Cost)} + \text{Net Indirect Taxes}
\]
\[
\text{GNP} = \text{GDP} + \text{NFIA}
\]
\[
\text{NNP} = \text{GNP} - \text{Depreciation}
\]

---

### Real vs Nominal GDP

- **Nominal GDP** (GDP at current prices): Valued at prices prevailing in the same year; includes both price and volume changes
- **Real GDP** (GDP at constant prices): Valued at prices of the base year; reflects only volume changes

\[
\text{GDP Deflator} = \frac{\text{Nominal GDP}}{\text{Real GDP}} \times 100
\]

GDP deflator is a **broader price index** than WPI or CPI as it covers all goods and services produced in the economy.

---

### India's National Accounts — Institutional Framework

**Compiled by:** National Statistical Office (NSO), MoSPI

**International standard followed:** System of National Accounts (SNA 2008), adopted by UN Statistical Commission; India plans to shift to SNA 2025 in the next revision.

**Base year (new series, released 27 February 2026):** **2022-23** (9th series; replaces old base year 2011-12)

**Earlier series base years:** 1948-49, 1960-61, 1970-71, 1980-81, 1993-94, 1999-2000, 2004-05, 2011-12, **2022-23**

---

### Key Improvements in New GDP Series (Base Year 2022-23)

- Integration of **Supply and Use Tables (SUT)** to reduce discrepancy between production and expenditure sides
- Use of **GST data** for corporate sector estimation and cross-validation
- **PFMS data** for central government expenditure (actuals instead of revised estimates)
- **e-Vahan data** for road transport PFCE estimates
- **Denton proportional benchmarking** for quarterly estimates (replaces pro-rata method)
- **COICOP 2018 classification** for Private Final Consumption Expenditure (PFCE)
- **Double deflation** in manufacturing and agriculture
- Inclusion of **hired domestic workers** in GDP
- Better measurement of **informal/household sector** via ASUSE and PLFS

---

### Release Calendar — National Accounts (India)

| Estimate | Timing |
|---|---|
| **Advance Estimate (AE)** | January of the fiscal year |
| **Second Advance Estimate (SAE)** | February 28 |
| **Provisional Estimates (PE)** | May 31 |
| **First Revised Estimates (FRE)** | January of following year |
| **Second Revised Estimates (SRE)** | January of year after |
| **Third Revised Estimates (TRE/Final)** | Released progressively |

**Quarterly GDP estimates:** Released ~60 days after end of quarter (e.g., Q1 Apr-Jun: released in Aug-Sep).

---

### Components of GDP (Expenditure Approach)

\[
\text{GDP} = \underbrace{\text{PFCE}}_{\text{Private consumption}} + \underbrace{\text{GFCE}}_{\text{Govt spending}} + \underbrace{\text{GFCF}}_{\text{Investment}} + \underbrace{\text{CIS}}_{\text{Inventories}} + \underbrace{\text{Valuables}} + \underbrace{(\text{Exports} - \text{Imports})}_{\text{Net exports}}
\]

- **PFCE:** Private Final Consumption Expenditure (largest component in India ~55-60%)
- **GFCE:** Government Final Consumption Expenditure
- **GFCF:** Gross Fixed Capital Formation (investment in fixed assets; ~30%)
- **CIS:** Change in Inventories (change in stocks)

---

### Gross Value Added (GVA) by Sector (India 2022-23 base)

| Broad sector | Components |
|---|---|
| **Primary** | Agriculture (crops, livestock, forestry, fishing) + Mining & Quarrying |
| **Secondary** | Manufacturing + Electricity, Gas, Water + Construction |
| **Tertiary** | Trade, Hotels, Transport, Communication + Financial, Real Estate, Professional + Public Admin, Defence, Other services |

**Share of services (tertiary) > 50% of India's GVA** — largest contributor.

> **ISS tip:** GDP (Market Price) = GVA (Basic Prices) + Net Taxes on Products. GNP = GDP + NFIA. NNP = GNP − Depreciation. New GDP base year (released Feb 2026): **2022-23**. Fisher's Ideal satisfies both reversal tests; GDP deflator = Nominal/Real × 100. The 9 base year series of Indian national accounts is a common MCQ.

sections.label: Population Census
sections.content:
## Population Census of India

### What is a Census?

A **Population Census** is the complete enumeration of all persons residing in the country — every individual is counted. It is distinct from a **sample survey**, where only a portion of the population is covered.

**Legal basis:** **Census Act, 1948** — provides the legal framework for conducting the decennial census.

**Conducted by:** **Office of the Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India (RGI)**, under Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA).

---

### Periodicity and History

**Frequency:** Every 10 years (decennial) — the year ending in **1** (e.g., 1961, 1971, 1981, 1991, 2001, 2011).

**First modern census in India:** 1872 (non-synchronous, under British Raj) — synchronous census from **1881** onwards.

**Census years:** 1881, 1891, 1901, 1911, 1921, 1931, 1941, 1951, 1961, 1971, 1981, 1991, 2001, **2011** (most recent completed), **2021** (postponed due to COVID-19 — yet to be conducted as of 2026).

**2021 Census:** Delayed due to COVID-19; likely to be conducted with a **digital/mobile app-based** methodology; will also include **NPR (National Population Register)** update.

---

### Data Collected in Census

**House listing and Housing Census (Phase 1):**
- Number and type of houses
- Household amenities (drinking water, electricity, toilet, kitchen)
- Assets (vehicles, phones, computer)

**Population Enumeration (Phase 2):**

| Category | Data items |
|---|---|
| **Demographic** | Age, sex, marital status, date of birth |
| **Social** | Religion, caste/tribe (SC/ST), language/mother tongue |
| **Economic** | Main and marginal workers, occupation, industry, non-workers |
| **Educational** | Literacy, level of education attained |
| **Residential** | Place of birth, place of last residence (migration data) |
| **Disability** | Type and nature of disability |

---

### Methods of Data Collection

**De facto method:** Count people where they are on the census night (common internationally).

**De jure method:** Count people at their usual place of residence. **India follows a modified de jure method** — usually resident population is enumerated.

**Two phases:**
1. **House Listing and Housing Census:** Preliminary stage; lists all buildings and households
2. **Population Enumeration:** Individual-level data collected for each person in listed households

**Enumerators:** School teachers typically serve as enumerators; supervised by supervisors.

---

### Agencies Involved

- **Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India (RGI):** Apex body, New Delhi
- **Directorate of Census Operations:** One in each state/UT
- **District/Tehsil/Block level census officers:** Field implementation
- **Enumerators and supervisors:** Actual data collection

---

### Key Findings — Census 2011

| Indicator | Value |
|---|---|
| **Total population** | 121.09 crore (1,210.9 million) |
| **Sex ratio** | 943 females per 1000 males |
| **Child sex ratio (0-6 yrs)** | 919 |
| **Literacy rate** | 74.04% (Male: 82.14%, Female: 65.46%) |
| **Population density** | 382 persons per sq km |
| **Urban population** | 31.16% |
| **Decadal growth rate (2001-11)** | 17.64% |
| **Most populous state** | Uttar Pradesh |
| **Highest literacy state** | Kerala |
| **Highest sex ratio state** | Kerala (1084) |
| **Lowest sex ratio state** | Haryana (879) |

---

### Dissemination

- Census data is available through RGI's website (censusindia.gov.in)
- Released in stages: Primary Census Abstract, District Census Handbooks, Series publications
- **Census data is confidential** — individual-level data cannot be used for tax or prosecution purposes (Census Act, 1948, Section 15)
- **Open data:** Aggregate tables are publicly available

---

### Civil Registration System (CRS)

**Purpose:** Continuous recording of vital events — births, deaths, stillbirths, marriages, and divorces.

**Legal basis:** **Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969**

**Administered by:** RGI and state governments

**Publication:** *Report on Medical Certification of Cause of Death (MCCD)* and *Vital Statistics of India* — annual publications by RGI.

**Key vital rates derived from CRS:**
- **Crude Birth Rate (CBR):** Births per 1000 population
- **Crude Death Rate (CDR):** Deaths per 1000 population
- **Infant Mortality Rate (IMR):** Deaths under 1 year per 1000 live births
- **Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR):** Maternal deaths per 1,00,000 live births
- **Total Fertility Rate (TFR):** Average children per woman over her reproductive lifetime

**Sample Registration System (SRS):** Dual record system run by RGI that provides annual estimates of birth rate, death rate, IMR, TFR, etc. at the national and state level — supplements CRS.

> **ISS tip:** Census conducted by **RGI under Ministry of Home Affairs** (not MoSPI). Legal basis: **Census Act, 1948**. Decennial — year ending in **1**. Census 2011: Population = **121.09 crore**, Literacy = **74.04%**, Sex ratio = **943**. India follows **de jure** (usual residence) method. CRS is under **Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969**. SRS provides **annual** vital rates between censuses.

sections.label: Sector-wise Statistics & Important Surveys
sections.content:
## Sector-wise Statistics & Important Surveys and Censuses

### Agriculture Statistics

| Indicator/Survey | Agency | Periodicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Area, Production & Yield (APY)** of crops | Directorate of Economics & Statistics (DES), Ministry of Agriculture | Annual (Advance estimates in 4 stages) |
| **Agricultural Census** | Min. of Agriculture — DES | Every 5 years (2015-16, 2010-11 etc.) |
| **Input Survey** | Min. of Agriculture | Quinquennial |
| **Situation Assessment Survey (SAS)** | NSO (NSSO) | Periodic (NSS 70th round 2012-13; 77th round 2018-19) |
| **Cost of Cultivation Studies (CCS)** | Directorate of Economics & Statistics | Annual |
| **Livestock Census** | Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying | Every 5 years (20th Livestock Census 2018-19) |
| **Fisheries Statistics** | Ministry of Fisheries, Animal Husbandry & Dairying | Annual |

**Key agricultural indicators:**
- **MSP (Minimum Support Price):** Recommended by CACP (Commission for Agricultural Costs & Prices); based on cost of cultivation data
- **Food grain production:** Reported by Min. of Agriculture; India produced ~330 MT in 2023-24
- **DISHA (Dashboard for Integrated Statistics & Horticulture Area):** Horticulture statistics by NHB

---

### Health Statistics

| Survey/Census | Agency | Periodicity | Key content |
|---|---|---|---|
| **National Family Health Survey (NFHS)** | IIPS Mumbai; MoHFW | ~5 years | Fertility, child health, nutrition, domestic violence, HIV |
| **NFHS-5** | 2019-21 | — | TFR 2.0; IMR 35.2; Under-5 MR 41.9 |
| **Annual Health Survey (AHS)** | RGI | Annual (2010-13 only) | EAG states + Assam |
| **District Level Household Survey (DLHS)** | IIPS; MoHFW | Periodic | RCH services |
| **Sample Registration System (SRS)** | RGI | Annual | Birth rate, death rate, IMR, TFR |
| **MCCD Report** | RGI | Annual | Medical certification of cause of death |
| **Health Management Info System (HMIS)** | MoHFW | Continuous | Facility-level health data |

**Key health indicators (NFHS-5, 2019-21):**
- IMR: 35.2 per 1000 live births
- MMR: 97 per 1,00,000 live births (SRS 2018-20)
- TFR: 2.0 (below replacement level of 2.1 nationally for first time)
- Under-5 MR: 41.9 per 1000 live births
- Stunting (children under 5): 35.5%
- Full vaccination coverage: 76.4%

---

### Education Statistics

| Survey/Report | Agency | Periodicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Unified District Information System for Education (UDISE+)** | NIEPA / DoSEL, MoE | Annual |
| **Annual Status of Education Report (ASER)** | Pratham (NGO) | Annual |
| **All India Survey on Higher Education (AISHE)** | DoHE, MoE | Annual |
| **National Achievement Survey (NAS)** | NCERT / DoSEL | Every 3 years |
| **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** | NSO | Annual | (also covers educational attainment) |

**Key education indicators (UDISE+ 2022-23):**
- Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) — Primary (Classes 1-5): 98.4%
- GER Secondary (Classes 9-10): ~77.9%
- GER Higher Education (AISHE 2021-22): 28.4%
- Pupil-Teacher Ratio (PTR) — Primary: 26:1

---

### Women and Child Statistics

| Survey/Report | Agency | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **NFHS-5 (2019-21)** | IIPS + MoHFW | Women's empowerment, child nutrition, domestic violence |
| **Women and Men in India** | MoSPI/NSO | Annual compilation of gender statistics |
| **National Gender Statistics** | MoWCD + MoSPI | Indicators across sectors |
| **Rapid Survey on Children (RSOC)** | UNICEF + MoWCD | Child welfare indicators |
| **Poshan Tracker** | MoWCD | Real-time nutrition monitoring for Anganwadis |

**Key child/women indicators (NFHS-5):**
- Child sex ratio (0-6 years): 929 (improved from 918 in NFHS-4)
- Women with bank accounts: 78.6%
- Women who own a mobile phone: 53.9%
- Child marriage (women married before 18): 23.3%

---

### Employment & Labour Statistics

| Survey | Agency | Periodicity | Key data |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** | NSO, MoSPI | Annual (urban + rural); Quarterly (urban) | LFPR, WPR, UR by sector, status, gender |
| **Annual Survey of Industries (ASI)** | NSO (Industrial Statistics wing) | Annual | Organised manufacturing: output, employment, wages, capital |
| **Economic Census** | NSO | ~5-7 years (7th EC: 2019) | All non-agricultural establishments; employment |
| **Quarterly Employment Survey (QES)** | Labour Bureau | Quarterly | 9 major sectors |
| **Labour Bureau surveys** | Labour Bureau, Min. of Labour | Periodic | CPI-IW, CPI-AL, industrial disputes |

**Key PLFS concepts:**
- **LFPR (Labour Force Participation Rate):** (Employed + Unemployed) / Population × 100
- **WPR (Worker Population Ratio):** Employed / Population × 100
- **UR (Unemployment Rate):** Unemployed / Labour Force × 100
- **UPSS:** Usual Principal and Subsidiary Status (annual activity)
- **CWS:** Current Weekly Status (activity in past 7 days)
- **CDS:** Current Daily Status (most refined; measures underemployment)

---

### Other Important Censuses and Surveys

| Survey/Census | Agency | Periodicity | Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES 2022-23)** | NSO | ~5 years | MPCE (Monthly Per Capita Consumer Expenditure); poverty estimation |
| **All India Debt & Investment Survey (AIDIS)** | NSO | ~10 years | Household assets, liabilities, capital expenditure |
| **National Sample Survey (NSS) Rounds** | NSO | Annual/Quinquennial | Various socio-economic topics |
| **Socio Economic and Caste Census (SECC 2011)** | MoRD | 2011 (first since Independence) | BPL identification; caste data (not yet released) |
| **BPL Census / Surveys** | MoRD | State-specific | Identification of below-poverty-line households |
| **SEQI (State Energy Statistics India)** | MoSPI | Annual | Energy statistics |

> **ISS tip:** NFHS conducted by **IIPS Mumbai** on behalf of MoHFW — NOT by NSO. UDISE+ compiled by **NIEPA/DoSEL**. Agricultural Census is every **5 years** (not 10). PLFS replaced the old EUS (Employment-Unemployment Survey) from 2017-18. The three activity statuses in PLFS — UPSS, CWS, CDS — are a direct MCQ. CDS gives the highest unemployment rate as it is most sensitive.

sections.label: Socio-Economic Indicators, Gender Statistics & International Systems
sections.content:
## Socio-Economic Indicators, Gender Statistics & International Statistical System

### Key Socio-Economic Indicators

**Poverty:**

| Measure | Basis | Agency |
|---|---|---|
| **Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI)** | 3 dimensions: health, education, living standards (10 indicators) | UNDP + OPHI |
| **Tendulkar Poverty Line** | Based on calorie + non-food expenditure; revised 2009 | Planning Commission |
| **Rangarajan Committee (2014)** | Higher poverty line; MPCE-based | Planning Commission |
| **HCES 2022-23 data** | Used for updating poverty estimates; Gini coefficient | NSO/MoSPI |
| **SECC 2011** | Deprivation-based identification of poor | MoRD |

**India MPI (2023):** 11.28% of India's population (around 15 crore) classified as multidimensionally poor — significant reduction from 24.85% in 2015-16 (NITI Aayog MPI report).

**Inequality measures:**
- **Gini Coefficient:** 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); India Gini for consumption: ~0.28 (HCES 2022-23)
- **Lorenz Curve:** Graphical representation of income/consumption distribution
- **Palma Ratio:** Ratio of richest 10%'s income share to poorest 40%'s share

**Inflation:**
- **Headline inflation:** CPI (Combined) — RBI target 4% ± 2%
- **Core inflation:** CPI excluding food and fuel
- **WPI inflation:** Producer-level price changes; GDP deflator

**Human Development:**
- **HDI:** Life expectancy + Education + GNI per capita (geometric mean)
- **GII (Gender Inequality Index):** Reproductive health + empowerment + labour market participation; India rank: 108 (2023 HDR)
- **MPI:** Multidimensional poverty (10 indicators across 3 dimensions)

---

### Gender Statistics & Gender Awareness

**Gender statistics** are data that adequately reflect differences and inequalities in the situation of women and men in society.

**Key gender indicators:**

| Indicator | Source | Latest value (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sex ratio** | Census 2011 | 943 ♀ per 1000 ♂ |
| **Child sex ratio (0-6)** | Census 2011 | 919; NFHS-5: 929 |
| **Female literacy rate** | Census 2011 | 65.46% |
| **Female LFPR** | PLFS 2022-23 | ~37% (UPSS basis) — rising trend |
| **MMR** | SRS 2018-20 | 97 per 1,00,000 live births |
| **TFR** | SRS / NFHS-5 | 2.0 |
| **Women in Parliament** | Lok Sabha 2024 | ~13.6% |
| **GII rank (India)** | HDR 2023/24 | 108 out of 193 |

**Women and Men in India:** Annual publication by MoSPI compiling gender-disaggregated statistics across sectors.

**Beti Bachao Beti Padhao Scheme:** Targeted at improving child sex ratio in districts with low CSR; monitored through HMIS and UDISE+ data.

**Gender Budget:** Annual statement by Ministry of Finance showing expenditure on women-specific and women-inclusive schemes.

---

### National and International Statistical System

**National system — Key bodies:**

| Body | Ministry | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **NSO (MoSPI)** | MoSPI | GDP, IIP, CPI-U/R, surveys, economic census |
| **RGI** | Ministry of Home Affairs | Population census, CRS, SRS, NPR |
| **Labour Bureau** | Ministry of Labour | CPI-IW, CPI-AL, CPI-RL, QES |
| **DPIIT/OEA** | Ministry of Commerce | WPI |
| **RBI** | — | Money supply, banking, BoP statistics |
| **SEBI** | — | Capital market statistics |
| **DGFT** | Commerce Ministry | Trade statistics |
| **CAG** | — | Government accounts audit |
| **NITI Aayog** | GoI | SDG India Index, MPI, policy data |
| **State DES** | State govts | GSDP, state-level sector statistics |

**International statistical bodies:**

| Organisation | Role |
|---|---|
| **UN Statistical Commission (UNSC)** | Apex body; sets global statistical standards; adopted 10 Fundamental Principles |
| **UN Statistics Division (UNSD)** | Secretariat of UNSC; coordinates SNA, environment statistics |
| **IMF Statistics Department** | BoP statistics, Government Finance Statistics (GFS), monetary and financial statistics |
| **World Bank** | World Development Indicators (WDI); International Comparison Programme (ICP) — PPP |
| **ILO Statistics Department** | Labour force, employment, wages, occupational safety |
| **OECD Statistics Directorate** | Developed countries; OECD Better Life Index |
| **FAO Statistics Division (FAOSTAT)** | Agriculture, food security, fisheries, forestry |
| **WHO (Global Health Observatory)** | Health statistics: mortality, disease burden |
| **UNDP** | Human Development Report; HDI, GII, MPI |
| **UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS)** | Education statistics globally |

---

### Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) & India

**SDGs:** 17 Goals, 169 targets, 232+ indicators adopted by all UN member states in 2015 (Agenda 2030).

**SDG India Index:** Published by NITI Aayog annually — composite index measuring India's progress on SDGs at state level.

**Data framework for SDGs:** NSO/MoSPI has identified **115 indicators** out of 232 global SDG indicators for which data is available in India.

---

### Special Data Dissemination Standard (SDDS) & e-GDDS

- **SDDS:** IMF framework for countries with significant access to international capital markets; ensures timeliness, coverage, and transparency of key economic and financial data
- **India is a subscriber to SDDS** — commits to releasing Advance Release Calendar and following SDDS metadata standards
- **e-GDDS:** For countries building statistical capacity (lower data requirements than SDDS)

---

### Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)

- **PPP** adjusts exchange rates to equalise the purchasing power of different currencies
- **International Comparison Programme (ICP):** World Bank-coordinated; India participates; GDP in PPP terms makes international comparisons meaningful
- India's GDP (PPP basis) ranks 3rd globally (after USA and China) — higher than nominal rank (5th)

> **ISS tip:** CPI-IW is compiled by **Labour Bureau** (Ministry of Labour), NOT MoSPI — most-tested confusion. RGI is under **Ministry of Home Affairs**, not MoSPI. WPI compiled by **OEA/DPIIT** (Ministry of Commerce). NFHS conducted by **IIPS Mumbai**. India is an **SDDS subscriber** (not e-GDDS). SDG India Index is published by **NITI Aayog**. HDI = geometric mean of three indices (life expectancy, education, GNI). Female LFPR in India is rising — latest PLFS data should be tracked.

sections.label: Official Statistics (India) PYQ Cheatsheet (2017–2025)
sections.content:
## Official Statistics (India) — ISS PYQ Master Cheatsheet (2017–2025)

> **Note:** All content below is based strictly on official Indian government sources — MoSPI, NSSO, RGI, NSC, Census Act 1948, Collection of Statistics Act 2008, and UN Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics. No assumptions made.

---

## 1. Statistical System of India — Structure

### Nature of Indian Statistical System
**PYQ (2017Q66, 2019Q59):**
- Indian Statistical System is **decentralized** — both by federal structure (Centre-State) AND by subjects (Agriculture, Labour, Health etc.) ✓
- State Statistical System is **NOT centralized** — it too is decentralized ✗
- Statistical coordination is done by **CSO (now NSO)** ✓

**Decentralization types (2019Q59):**
- **Laterally decentralized** ✓ (subject-wise — different Ministries handle different stats)
- **Vertically decentralized** ✓ (Central → State → District level)
- NOT centralized

**Constitutional placement (2018Q67, 2023Q68):**
- **Statistics** is in the **Concurrent List** (List III, Entry 94) of the Seventh Schedule — both Centre and States can legislate ✓
- **Population Census** is in the **Union List** (List I, Entry 69) ✓ (2023Q78 ✓)
- Public Health: **State List** ✓
- Sanitation: **State List** ✓
- Marine Hospital: **Union List** ✓
- Population Control: **Concurrent List** ✓

### Nodal Ministry
**PYQ (2019Q60):** Nodal Ministry for Statistical System = **Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI)**

### Who Compiles Official Statistics (2021Q76)
Authorised:
- Central Government ✓
- State Government ✓
- **Panchayat Raj Institutions/Urban Local Bodies** ✓
All three are authorised.

### Subject-Specific Data Collection (2021Q77)
Collection of statistics for subject-specific areas (Agriculture, Labour, Health, Commerce) vests with the **respective Line Ministries/Departments** — NOT with a single central body.

---

## 2. Key Statistical Organisations

### MoSPI & NSO
**Central Statistics Office (CSO)** — now merged into **National Statistical Office (NSO)** along with NSSO:

**Main activities of CSO (2018Q68):**
- Coordination of statistical activities in the country ✓
- Evolving statistical standards ✓
- **NOT** collecting data through sample surveys (that is NSSO's role) ✗
- Providing guidance and training in official statistics ✓
Three of four correct (I, II, IV).

**NOT an activity of CSO (2019Q54):** Conducting household sample surveys (done by NSSO/FOD).

**Divisions of CSO (2022Q44):**
- National Accounts Division ✓
- Economic Statistics Division ✓
- **Field Operation Division ✗** (FOD is part of NSSO, not CSO)
Only I and II are parts of CSO.

**CSO — National Accounts Division publications (2017Q70):**
- National Accounts Statistics ✓
- Input-Output Transactions Table ✓
- **Economic Census ✗** (Economic Census is conducted by CSO's Economic Census division, but published separately)
I and II are publications of National Accounts Division.

**Environment Statistics (2019Q76):** Handled by **Social Statistics Division** of CSO/NSO.

### National Statistical Commission (NSC)
**Established:** 2005, by Government of India **resolution** (not legislation, not constitutional body) (2021Q78, 2024Q71).

**NSC is NOT a constitutional body** (2024Q71 — Statement II ✗).

**Composition (2024Q71):** NSC has a **Chairperson + 4 Members** ✓ (Statement III ✓). Statement I: set up in 2005 ✓.

**Secretary of NSC (2024Q64):** The **Chief Statistician of India (CSI)** also serves as Secretary to NSC and is the head of NSO.

**Mandate/Functions of NSC (2022Q61):**
- Serve as a nodal/empowered body for core statistical activities ✓
- Evolve, monitor and enforce statistical priorities and standards ✓
- Ensure statistical coordination ✓
All three correct.

**NOT a function of NSC (2019Q55):** Conducting sample surveys (that is NSSO's function).

**Responsibility of statistical audit:** Yes, NSC has this responsibility (2017Q67 ✓).

### National Sample Survey Office (NSSO)
**Functions:**
- Collects data by **sample surveys** ✓ (2017Q67)
- NOT conducting censuses (that is RGI for population census, and line ministries for others)

**Divisions of NSSO (2021Q70):**
1. **Survey Design and Research Division (SDRD)** ✓ — formulates sample design
2. **Field Operations Division (FOD)** ✓ — collects data from respondents; also field enumerators (2020Q76)
3. **Data Processing Division (DPD)** ✓ — processes data
4. **Survey Coordination Division (SCD)** ✓ — prepares survey reports
All four divisions correct.

**PYQ (2023Q73):**
- Collecting data: FOD ✓
- Sample design: SDRD ✓
- Disseminating to public: **NOT FOD** — done by SDRD/Data Dissemination Wing ✗
- Preparing survey reports: **NOT SCD** — done by SDRD ✗
Only first two correctly matched.

**Ultimate Stage Unit (2018Q64):** **Household** — the final/ultimate unit for socio-economic surveys of NSSO.

**Most common sampling design (2018Q65, 2020Q54):** **Stratified Multi-Stage Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR)** — also described as **Stratified Multistage Design**.

**Most common survey period (2020Q56):** **Annual** (July–June, one year).

**Rotational panel survey design (2020Q72):** Employed in **Annual Survey of Industries (ASI)** — wait, for NSSO: the **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)** uses a **rotational panel design** in urban areas.

### Registrar General of India (RGI)
**Activities (2019Q58, 2024Q61):**
- Conducting **Population Census** in India ✓
- Conducting **Sample Registration System (SRS)** ✓
- Preparing and maintaining **National Register of Indian Citizens (NRIC)** ✓
- **NOT** conducting NSSO surveys (NSSO is under MoSPI) ✗
- **NOT** conducting Economic Census (MoSPI/CSO does that) ✗
- **Civil Registration System (CRS)** ✓ (2024Q61 — I ✓)
- **Housing and Population Census** ✓ (2024Q61 — III ✓)
- **Mother Tongue Survey** ✓ (2024Q61 — IV ✓)
- **Socio-economic Indicators** ✗ (not a primary RGI activity)

**Vital statistics coordination (2022Q49):** RGI is responsible for coordinating and unifying death registration at central level.

**Vital statistics source (2020Q52):** RGI compiles and releases vital statistics regularly.

---

## 3. Legislative Framework

### Key Acts (2017Q68, 2024Q72)
1. **The Collection of Statistics Act, 2008** ✓ — legal authority for data collection
2. **The Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969** ✓ — CRS framework
3. **The Census Act, 1948** ✓ — Population Census

Legal support for official statistics (2024Q72): **Census Act 1948 (II) ✓** and **Collection of Statistics Act 2008 (IV) ✓**. Government of India (Allocation of Business) Rules 1961 ✓ (I). Representation of the People Act 1951 ✗.

### Collection of Statistics Act, 2008 (2018Q69, 2024Q68)
Applicable to:
- Industrial and business concerns ✓
- Individual and household ✓
- Government and private agencies/enterprises ✓
All three are covered.

**Subjects that may be collected (2024Q68):**
- Economic and demographic ✓
- Social and scientific ✓
- Environmental ✓
All three (I, II, III).

### Census Act, 1948 (2023Q67, 2025Q70)
**PYQ (2025Q70):**
- Population Census AND Housing Census both under Census Act 1948 ✓ (Statement I)
- Census may be taken in India or any part thereof ✓ (Statement II)
- Census may be taken whenever necessary/desirable ✓ (Statement III)
- Census Act 1948 was amended in **1994** ✓ (Statement IV)
All four correct.

**Census enumerators (2020Q74):** Can be:
- School teachers ✓
- Central government officials ✓
- Local government officials ✓
All three can be engaged.

**Reference point (2025Q79):** Reference point of population enumeration is normally **midnight of 28 February/1 March** (or the designated reference moment).

**Census House (2025Q68):** A Census House is defined as a structure or part of a structure with separate main entrance from the road, common courtyard or staircase, used or recognised as a separate unit — a **structure or part of a structure having a separate entrance**.

**Houselisting and Housing Census (2025Q72):** Information canvassed includes:
- Amenities available to the household ✓
- Assets possessed by the household ✓
- Details regarding floor, wall and roof ✓
All three (I, II, III).

**Census data uses (2020Q75):**
- Sampling frame for All-India Survey ✓
- Literacy rate ✓
- Average MPCE ✗ (from NSSO Consumer Expenditure Survey, not Census)
- Sex ratio ✓
I, II, IV correct.

---

## 4. Major Surveys & Censuses

### Population Census
- Conducted by **RGI** under Census Act 1948
- **Decennial** (every 10 years)
- Covers Social & cultural tables ✓, Migration tables ✓, Fertility tables ✓ (2023Q75 — all three)

### Agriculture Census
**Periodicity (2018Q78):** Every **5 years**

**Basic unit (2020Q60, 2022Q50):** **Operational Holding** (2022Q50 — Statement I ✓)

**Data collection method (2017Q71):** Through:
- Complete enumeration in non-land record states
- **Khasra registers retabulation** in Land Records States ✓ (Statement II)
- Sampling techniques in some states ✓ (Statement III)
II and III correct for Land Records States.

**Crop production estimation (2021Q72):** Output of agriculture crops is estimated using **crop-cutting experiments (CCE)** approach.

### Economic Census
**Periodicity (2022Q50):** Statement III says "ten years" — this is **INCORRECT**. Economic Census is conducted approximately every 5–7 years (not fixed ten-yearly). Statement III ✗.

**Handicraft/handloom (2024Q63):** Data for handicraft/handloom establishments was collected for the first time during the **6th Economic Census**.

**Censuses conducted by Govt of India (2024Q70):**
- Economic Census ✓
- Minor Irrigation Census ✓
- Labour Census ✓ (done by Labour Bureau)
- Livestock Census ✓ (done by Dept of Animal Husbandry)
All four are conducted — **4** censuses.

### Livestock Census (2025Q80)
- Conducted every **5 years** ✓ (Statement I)
- Enumerates **15 species** of domesticated animals — NOT 25 ✗ (Statement II ✗)
Only Statement I correct.

### NSSO Rounds
**76th Round (2019Q78, 2022Q42):** Disability; Drinking water, Sanitation, Hygiene and Housing conditions. (Household consumption expenditure was NOT in 76th round ✗)

**Agency for finalising subjects (2022Q42):** **Governing Council of NSSO** (or the Steering Committee) finalises subjects to be covered.

**78th Round (2020Q55):** Subject matter — **Land and Livestock Holdings of Households and Situation Assessment of Agricultural Households (SAS)**.

**80th Round (2025Q69):**
- 80th Round commenced from 1st January, 2025 ✓ (Statement I)
- Covers **Survey on Household Social Consumption-Health** AND **Comprehensive Modular Survey-Telecom** ✓ (Statement II)
Both correct.

**Time Use Survey (2021Q61):** The **Time Use Survey** being conducted by MoSPI in 2019 measures paid and unpaid work of both men and women.

### Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)
**Conducted by:** **NSO (National Statistical Office)** under MoSPI (2023Q74)

**Objectives (2025Q77):** Estimates annual employment/unemployment for rural + urban; quarterly estimates for urban; labour force participation rates. NOT collecting data on wages of Central Government employees (that is Labour Bureau's job).

**Labour force data sources (2023Q69):**
1. NSSO (PLFS) ✓
2. Labour Bureau and DGET ✓
3. **RGI ✗** (RGI does vital statistics, not labour force data)
Only I and II correct.

### SRS (Sample Registration System)
**PYQ (2017Q77):** SRS is a **dual record system** — continuous civil registration + periodic retrospective survey. Used to estimate birth/death rates, IMR etc.

### CRS (Civil Registration System)
**Matched correctly (2017Q69):** Civil Registration System — Registrar General and Census Commissioner ✓.

### National Family Health Survey (NFHS)
**Ministry (2017Q69):** NFHS — Ministry of Health and Family Welfare ✓

**NFHS as sampling frame (2019Q80):** Population Census ✓ and Economic Census ✓ can be used as sampling frames for other surveys. NFHS ✗ (it is itself a sample survey, not a sampling frame).

### Socio-Economic Caste Census (2025Q73)
Conduct in 2011 — used primarily for **identifying beneficiaries for government welfare schemes/programmes** (poverty-targeting).

---

## 5. National Accounts Statistics

### GDP & GNP
**GDP definition (2022Q46):**
- Market value of all final goods and services within a country in a given period ✓
- Measured at current and constant prices ✓
- NOT a measure of economic welfare ✗ (it is a measure of economic output/activity, not welfare)
Statements I and II correct.

**GDP — three equivalent approaches (2020Q77):**
- Production/Output approach ✓
- Income approach ✓
- Expenditure approach ✓
All three.

**GDP reflects (2021Q67):** The **value of all final goods and services produced within the domestic economy** in a given period.

**National Income (NI) = (2021Q74):** **Net National Product (NNP) at Factor Cost** — this is the standard definition of National Income in India.

**Difference GDP vs GNI (2024Q73):** The key difference is **Geographic** (II) — GDP is based on territory/residence concept (domestic), while GNI includes net factor income from abroad. Also **Monetary** in the sense of including/excluding foreign income.

**Primary sector in GDP (2024Q65):**
- Crops ✓
- Livestock ✓
- Forestry and logging ✓
- Fishing and aquaculture ✓
- Mining and quarrying ✗ (Mining is part of Secondary sector in India's GDP classification)
Four sectors (I, II, III, IV) but NOT V.

**GVA definition (2020Q57):** GVA by an enterprise = **Output − Intermediate Consumption** (also = wages+salaries + operating surplus + CFC).

**Domestic Economy concept (2020Q58):** Based on **Resident Units** concept (not geography alone).

**State Domestic Product (SDP) (2024Q74):** Computed by States using national methodology. **District Domestic Product** also computed in some States ✓. Urban/Rural Domestic Product ✗ — not standard official estimates.

**GDP and non-market production (2023Q71):**
- GDP includes some non-market production (defence, education services) ✓
- Not all productive work included ✓
- Voluntary work NOT included ✓
- GDP composed of goods and services produced for sale AND some non-market ✓
All four statements correct — **4**.

**Omitted from GDP (2023Q80):**
- Household production (meals, gardening) ✓
- Leisure time activities ✓
- Helping children with homework ✓
All three omitted.

**Wages from abroad (2021Q65):** 'Wages and salaries' earned by resident of country A from resident enterprise of country B is included in **Compensation of Employees (GNI of A)** — it is in **current account of Balance of Payments** as primary income.

**Growth rate of quarterly GDP (2021Q69):** Means **year-on-year** (same quarter of previous year) growth rate — standard Indian practice.

**Merchandise Trade Statistics (2021Q71):** Compiled from bills submitted by importers/exporters — example of **administrative records** data source.

---

## 6. Price Indices

### Consumer Price Index (CPI)
**CPI categories (2022Q62):** CPI is calculated for:
- **CPI-Rural (CPI-R)**
- **CPI-Urban (CPI-U)**
- **CPI-Combined (CPI-C)**
- **CPI-Industrial Workers (CPI-IW)**
- **CPI-Agricultural Labourers (CPI-AL)**
- **CPI-Rural Labourers (CPI-RL)**

**CPI-IW (2024Q69):**
- Computed by **Labour Bureau** ✓ (Statement I)
- Used as tool for measuring inflation ✓ (Statement II)
- Used for regulating wages and DA ✓ (Statement III)
- Computed for male, female, rural, urban ✗ (Statement IV — CPI-IW is computed for industrial workers overall, not disaggregated by sex/rural-urban)
I, II, III correct.

**CPI-AL/RL compiler (2020Q51):** **Labour Bureau** (Ministry of Labour & Employment).

**Retail inflation measurement (2018Q71):** **CPI-Combined** (CPI-C) — released by MoSPI — is the headline retail inflation measure.

**DA linkage (2018Q70):** Dearness Allowance for Central and State Government employees is linked to **CPI-Industrial Workers (CPI-IW)**.

**CPI statements (2021Q80):** All three correct:
1. Average change in prices over time for basket of goods/services ✓
2. Calculated using prices of representative items collected periodically ✓
3. Measure of both inflation AND deflation ✓

**Purchasing power (2017Q78):** If price index 2015 = 110.3 and price index 2005 = 98.4:
\[
\text{Purchasing power of 2005 in 2015 terms} = \frac{98.4}{110.3} \times 100 = 89.2\%
\]
Alternatively, purchasing power of money at 2005 compared to 2015: \(110.3/98.4 \times 100 = 112.1\%\).

**Real wage (2024Q80):** Money wage = ₹8,000; CPI (2012=100) = 200. Real wage = \(8000/200 \times 100 = \text{₹}4000\) (in 2012 prices). Purchasing power is halved.

### Wholesale Price Index (WPI)
**Released by (2018Q77):** **Office of the Economic Adviser, Ministry of Commerce and Industry**.

### Index of Industrial Production (IIP)
**What it measures (2022Q63):**
- Change in volume of industrial production of representative basket ✓
- Used to analyse performance of various sectors ✓
Both correct.

**IIP data source (2024Q78):** **Annual Survey of Industries (ASI)** is a major source; also includes data from industry associations and Ministries. The quick estimate uses data from **source agencies** (various ministries and associations).

**Manufacturing performance snapshot (2019Q51):** **Index of Industrial Production (IIP)** provides quick snapshot of manufacturing sector performance.

### Index Numbers — Theory
**Laspeyres vs Paasche (2022Q65):**
- Laspeyres uses **base period quantities** as weights ✓ (Statement I says current — ✗)
- Paasche uses **current period quantities** as weights ✓ (Statement II says base — ✗)
- For same data, Laspeyres ≥ Paasche ✓ (Statement III — true as general rule)
Statements I and II are NOT correct; Statement III IS correct. Statements I and II are wrong.

**PYQ (2021Q63):**
- Most index compilers use **Laspeyres'** index (upward bias) ✓ — Statement I correct
- Base period should be revised **periodically** (NOT as frequently as possible — frequent revision causes discontinuity) — Statement II ✗
Only Statement I correct.

**Fisher's Ideal Index (2022Q66):**
- Based on geometric mean ✓ (Statement I)
- Satisfies both **Time Reversal Test** and **Factor Reversal Test** ✓ (Statement II)
- Takes into account both current and base year quantities as weights ✓ (Statement III — geometric mean of Laspeyres and Paasche)
All three correct.

**Time Reversal Test (2025Q74):**
- Marshall-Edgeworth: ✓ satisfies TRT
- Laspeyres: ✗ does NOT satisfy TRT
- Fisher's Ideal: ✓ satisfies TRT
- Paasche: ✗ does NOT satisfy TRT
I and III satisfy TRT.

**Marshall-Edgeworth weights (2023Q72):** Uses **average of base and current period quantities** \(\frac{q_0+q_1}{2}\) as weights.

**Uses of index numbers (2022Q67):** All three correct:
1. Framing economic policies ✓
2. Assessing purchasing power of money ✓
3. Adjusting national income ✓

**Annual inflation rate (2018Q72):** Point-to-point: \(\frac{I_t - I_{t-12}}{I_{t-12}} \times 100\%\) (month t vs same month previous year) (2020Q79).

**Price deflators (2018Q73):** Used to obtain **real (constant price) values** from nominal (current price) values.

**Laspeyres method appropriate when (2025Q78):** Consumption pattern remains **static** for a longer period — Laspeyres is most convenient and economic (fixed base period weights don't need updating).

**CPI — what it reveals (2017Q79):** **The cost of living** and changes in the purchasing power of money.

---

## 7. SDGs & International Organisations

### Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)
**PYQ (2018Q61, 2019Q53, 2021Q64, 2022Q47):**
- **17 goals** ✓ (NOT 8 — MDGs had 8)
- Endorsed by **United Nations General Assembly** (NOT UNICEF, NOT UNDP alone) — UNDP supports/implements, UN adopted
- Came into effect **January 2016** ✓
- Adopted by **193 UN member countries** (NOT mandatory — voluntary framework) ✓
- Intended to be completed by **2030** ✓
- **NOT mandatory** — voluntary commitments ✓

**NOT an SDG (2022Q47):** All 17 SDGs include: No Poverty, Zero Hunger, Good Health, Quality Education, Gender Equality, Clean Water, Affordable Energy, Decent Work, Industry Innovation, Reduced Inequality, Sustainable Cities, Responsible Consumption, Climate Action, Life Below Water, Life on Land, Peace & Justice, Partnerships. Common traps: "Economic Growth alone" is part of SDG 8 but phrased differently.

**NOT an SDG (2020Q71):** "Enhance social protection measures" as a standalone goal — not one of the 17.

**SDG monitoring (2022Q43):** Goal 4 (Quality Education) monitored by:
- Proportion of computer literate adults ✓
- Enrolment ratio of children with disabilities ✓
- NOT de-addiction centres ✗
- NOT agriculture mandis ✗
I and III correct.

### UN Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics
**Designed by (2020Q80):** **United Nations Statistical Commission (UNSC)**.

**10 Fundamental Principles — Key ones tested:**
1. Relevance, impartiality and equal access ✓ (2022Q64)
2. Professional standards, scientific principles and ethics ✓
3. Accountability and transparency ✓
4. Prevention of misuse ✓ (Principle 4 — 2019Q77)
5. Sources of official statistics ✓ (Principle 5)
6. **Confidentiality** ✓ (Principle 6 — 2019Q77)
7. Legislation ✓ (2022Q64)
8. Coordination among statistical agencies ✓
9. International use of statistics ✓
10. International cooperation ✓ (2022Q64)

**Correctly matched (2019Q77):**
- Media misuse → Prevention of misuse (Principle 4) ✓
- Collection of Statistics Act → Sources (Principle 5) ✓
- Individual data disclosure → Confidentiality (Principle 6) ✓
All three correct.

**NOT a fundamental principle (2023Q66):** Statements like "maximising data collection" or "competition between agencies" — check against the 10 official principles.

**PYQ (2025Q75):** All three are UN Fundamental Principles:
- Laws under which statistical systems operate are made public ✓ (Principle 7 — Legislation)
- Bilateral and multilateral cooperation ✓ (Principle 10)
- Individual data strictly confidential ✓ (Principle 6)
All three correct.

**Official statistics as Public Good (2025Q66):**
- Financed from general tax revenue ✓
- Use by one does not affect use by others ✓ (non-rivalrous)
- Costly to produce but easily disseminated ✓
- NOT denied to private persons ✗ (equal access is a principle)
I, II, III correct.

**Desirable property NOT included (2018Q62):** Confidential handling of unit-level data (not a property of the output of official statistics — it is a process property).

**Official statistics statements (2024Q67):** All three correct:
- Fulfill test of practical utility ✓
- Drawn from all types of sources ✓
- Laws and measures made public ✓

**Reliability measured by (2018Q63):** Standard error or coefficient of variation of estimates.

**Metadata (2018Q66):** Data about data — describes the content, quality, condition, and other characteristics of data.

**Methods of data collection (2024Q75):** All four accepted:
- Census and surveys ✓
- Administrative records ✓
- Management information systems ✓
- Remote sensing ✓

**Longitudinal panel survey (2024Q66):** **A fixed set of respondents surveyed repeatedly over time** (Statement III) ✓ — only one correct.

**Statistical financing (2025Q71):** Normal sources:
- Government budgets ✓
- Multilateral trust funds ✓
- Bilateral donors and technical assistance ✓
- Market borrowings ✗ (not a normal/appropriate source)
I, II, IV correct.

### UNSC (2025Q76)
- Has **24 member countries** elected by UN Economic and Social Council ✓ (Statement I)
- India's four-year term at UNSC began January 2024 ✓ (Statement II — verify if current)
Both statements correct per available information.

### International Organisations (2023Q76)
- Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP) ✓ — statistical work
- NATO ✗ — not a statistical organisation
- Asian Development Bank (ADB) ✓ — statistical publications
- International Labour Organization (ILO) ✓ — labour statistics
I, III, IV are international statistical organisations.

---

## 8. Demographic & Vital Statistics

### Fertility Measures (2017Q80)
**General Fertility Rate (GFR) (2020Q53):**
\[
\text{GFR} = \frac{\text{Number of live births in a year}}{\text{Mid-year female population aged 15--49}} \times 1000
\]

**PYQ (2017Q80):** ASFR and related calculations using given data.

**IMR data sources (2018Q76):**
- **Sample Registration System (SRS)** — most reliable source for IMR ✓
- Civil Registration System (if complete)
SRS is the primary source.

### Employment/Unemployment Reference Periods (2017Q73)
NSSO uses **multiple reference periods**:
- **Usual Status** — reference period: **one year** ✓
- **Current Weekly Status** — reference period: **one week (7 days)** ✓
- **Current Daily Status** — reference period: **each day of reference week** ✓
All three reference periods are used (one year, one week, each day).

**Not in labour force (2019Q52):** A person is "not in labour force" if:
- Not working AND
- Not seeking work AND
- Not available for work
All three conditions (I, II, III) must hold. Someone with minor accident (IV) would be counted based on usual activity — Statement IV depends on duration, generally still in labour force if available. So I, II, III correct.

### Literacy & Education
**Effective literacy rate (2018Q79):** Literacy rate **excluding those below 7 years** (i.e., for population 7 years and above).

**GER in Grade I (2017Q72):** GER = Total enrolment in Grade I / Population of official age group × 100. Can exceed 100% if over-age/under-age children enrolled.

**GER statement (2024Q79):**
- GER of India at primary level > 100% indicates India can accommodate all primary school-age children ✓ (Statement I)
- Gender Parity Index (GPI) — check trend data; GPI has been improving at primary level ✗ (Statement II says decreasing — ✗)
Only Statement I correct.

---

## 9. Welfare Indices

### Human Development Index (HDI)
**Components:** Three dimensions:
1. **Long and healthy life** — Life Expectancy at Birth
2. **Knowledge** — Mean years of schooling + Expected years of schooling
3. **Decent standard of living** — GNI per capita (PPP)

**NOT in HDI (2018Q80, 2021Q66):** Per capita income alone ✗ (it uses GNI per capita, not income inequality); **Income inequality** is NOT a component of standard HDI (it appears in IHDI, inequality-adjusted HDI).

**Developed by:** UNDP ✓ (2018Q80).

### Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI) (2020Q78)
**Components (Morris Morris David Morris):**
1. Literacy rate ✓
2. Infant Mortality Rate ✓
3. Life Expectancy at age 1 ✓
**NOT included:** Per capita income ✗ (PQLI explicitly excludes income).

### Global Hunger Index (GHI) (2023Q77)
- 100-point scale: **0 = best, 100 = worst** ✓
- Consists of **4 indicators**: undernourishment, child wasting, child stunting, child mortality ✓
Both statements correct.

---

## 10. Prices & MSP

### Minimum Support Price (MSP)
**Recommended by (2019Q79):** **Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP)** — under Ministry of Agriculture.

**NOT correct about MSP (2018Q75):** MSP is a minimum price guarantee; it does NOT guarantee procurement (government MAY procure at MSP but is not mandated to procure all produce).

---

## 11. High Frequency Indicators (HFIs)

**PYQ (2023Q79, 2025Q67):** Monthly HFIs:
- Consumer Price Index ✓
- Rail Freight Traffic ✓
- E-way Bills ✓
- Power consumption ✓
- Port cargo traffic ✓
- Population ✗ (annual census/estimate, not monthly)

**All four (2025Q67):** Power consumption ✓, E-way bills ✓, Rail freight traffic ✓, Port cargo traffic ✓ — all 4 are HFIs.

---

## 12. Index Number — Computation (PYQ 2022Q68–70)

**Fisher's Ideal Index:**
\[
P_{01}^F = \sqrt{P_{01}^L \times P_{01}^P}
\]
\[
P_{01}^L = \frac{\sum p_1 q_0}{\sum p_0 q_0} \times 100, \quad P_{01}^P = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_1} \times 100
\]
**Time reversal:** \(P_{01}^F \times P_{10}^F = 1\) ✓
**Factor reversal:** \(P_{01}^F \times Q_{01}^F = \frac{\sum p_1 q_1}{\sum p_0 q_0}\) ✓

> **ISS Tips — Official Statistics:**
1. **Statistics in Concurrent List** (Entry 94); **Census in Union List** (Entry 69) — memorise.
2. **NSC**: set up 2005, by resolution (NOT constitutional), Chairperson + 4 Members.
3. **NSSO divisions**: SDRD (design), FOD (field collection), DPD (processing), SCD (coordination).
4. **Laspeyres uses base period quantities; Paasche uses current period quantities.** Laspeyres ≥ Paasche generally. Fisher = geometric mean of both — satisfies TRT and FRT.
5. **17 SDGs, effective January 2016, target 2030, voluntary** — designed by UN General Assembly.
6. **GDP**: Production + Income + Expenditure approaches all equivalent. Does NOT include household production, voluntary work, leisure.
7. **National Income = NNP at Factor Cost** (India).
8. **Agriculture Census**: 5-yearly, basic unit = operational holding.
9. **SRS = dual record system** for vital statistics; **CRS = Civil Registration System** under RGI.
10. **CPI-IW**: compiled by Labour Bureau; used for DA of government employees.

official.tips:
**High-priority topics for ISS Official Statistics section (Paper II):**

**Institutional knowledge (most-tested):**
- **NSO formed 23 May 2019** = merger of NSSO + CSO; under MoSPI; headed by Secretary (Statistics & PI)
- **NSSO established 1950;** 4 divisions: SDRD (Kolkata), FOD, DPD (Kolkata/Faridabad), CPD
- **NSC constituted 12 July 2006** — by executive resolution (NOT Act of Parliament) — Rangarajan Commission recommendation (2001)
- NSC: 1 Chairperson + 4 Members + 1 ex-officio; CSI = Secretary to NSC
- NSC members removed only by **President** after Supreme Court inquiry
- **RGI** under Ministry of **Home Affairs** (not MoSPI) — conducts Population Census and CRS

**Key legislation:**
- **Collection of Statistics Act, 2008** — primary legislation for data collection
- **Census Act, 1948** — Population Census legal basis
- **Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969** — CRS legal basis
- **ISI Act, 1959** — ISI Kolkata as Institute of National Importance

**Index numbers (most-tested):**
- **Fisher's Ideal Index:** Satisfies BOTH Time Reversal Test AND Factor Reversal Test — only index to do so
- **WPI:** Base 2011-12; compiled by **OEA/DPIIT** (Ministry of Commerce); measures wholesale prices; monthly (14th)
- **CPI (Combined):** Base 2012=100; compiled by **MoSPI**; headline inflation indicator; Food weight ~45.86%
- **CPI-IW:** Base 2016=100; compiled by **Labour Bureau** (Ministry of Labour) — used for DA calculation
- **IIP:** Base 2011-12; compiled by **NSO/MoSPI**; monthly (28th of next month + 6 weeks)
- New GDP base year (released 27 Feb 2026): **2022-23** (9th series; replaced 2011-12)

**National Accounts:**
- GDP (Market Price) = GVA (Basic Prices) + Net Taxes on Products
- GNP = GDP + NFIA; NNP = GNP − Depreciation; NI = NNP at Factor Cost
- GDP Deflator = Nominal GDP / Real GDP × 100
- India follows **SNA 2008**; plans to shift to SNA 2025 in next revision
- GDP release: AE (Jan) → SAE (Feb 28) → PE (May 31) → FRE (following Jan)

**Population Census:**
- Decennial — year ending in **1**; legal basis: **Census Act, 1948**
- Census 2011: Population **121.09 crore**; Literacy **74.04%**; Sex ratio **943**; Child sex ratio **919**
- India follows **de jure** (usual residence) method
- 2021 Census: postponed due to COVID; yet to be conducted

**Key surveys (frequently tested):**
- **PLFS** (from 2017-18): Annual + quarterly urban; replaces EUS; measures LFPR, WPR, UR by UPSS/CWS/CDS
- **NFHS:** Conducted by **IIPS Mumbai** (not NSO); NFHS-5 (2019-21); TFR = 2.0
- **HCES 2022-23:** Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (formerly CES); used for poverty estimation
- **ASI:** Annual Survey of Industries — organised manufacturing sector; by NSO
- **Economic Census:** Complete enumeration of all non-agricultural establishments; 7th EC in 2019
- **Agricultural Census:** Every 5 years — NOT 10 years
- **SRS:** Sample Registration System by RGI — annual vital rates (birth rate, death rate, IMR, TFR)

**Socio-economic indicators:**
- **HDI = geometric mean** of Life Expectancy Index × Education Index × GNI Index
- **Gini coefficient:** 0 = perfect equality; 1 = perfect inequality
- **CDS** gives highest unemployment rate; UPSS gives lowest — CDS most sensitive to underemployment
- **SDG India Index:** Published by **NITI Aayog**
- India is **SDDS subscriber** to IMF — commits to Advance Release Calendar and transparency

sampling.title: Sampling Techniques

sections.label: Basic Concepts & NSSO Methodology
sections.content:
## Basic Concepts, Need for Sampling & NSSO Methodology

### Basic Concepts — Population, Sample & Need for Sampling

**Population (Universe):** The complete set of all units (individuals, households, farms, establishments) about which information is sought. Denoted by size \(N\).

**Sample:** A subset of \(n\) units selected from the population to draw inferences about the whole.

**Sampling unit:** The unit selected at each draw (person, household, village, block).

**Sampling frame:** A list or map of all sampling units in the population from which the sample is drawn (e.g., voter list, census house-listing schedule, land records).

**Parameter:** A numerical characteristic of the population (e.g., population mean \(\bar{Y}\), total \(Y\), proportion \(P\)).

**Statistic / Estimator:** A function of sample observations used to estimate a parameter (e.g., sample mean \(\bar{y}\)).

---

### Complete Enumeration vs Sampling

| Feature | Complete Enumeration (Census) | Sample Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | All \(N\) units | \(n\) units (\(n \ll N\)) |
| Cost | Very high | Low |
| Time | Long | Short |
| Errors | Only non-sampling errors | Both sampling + non-sampling errors |
| Feasibility | Infeasible for destructive testing | Feasible |
| Accuracy | 100% coverage (if perfect) | Subject to sampling error |
| Use case | Decennial Census, Economic Census | NSS rounds, NFHS, PLFS |

---

### Sampling vs Non-Sampling Errors

**Sampling error:** Arises because only a part of the population is observed. It is the difference between the estimate and the true parameter value.
\[
\text{Sampling error} = \bar{y} - \bar{Y}
\]
- Decreases as \(n\) increases; disappears in complete enumeration
- Can be estimated from the sample itself

**Non-sampling error:** Arises from causes other than sampling — present in both census and surveys.

| Type | Examples |
|---|---|
| Coverage error | Omission/duplication of units in frame |
| Response error | Respondent gives wrong information |
| Enumerator error | Incorrect recording, interviewer bias |
| Processing error | Coding and data entry mistakes |
| Non-response error | Selected unit refuses or is absent |

> Non-sampling errors **do not decrease** with increase in sample size — they may even increase.

---

### NSSO Methodology in Sample Surveys

**Questionnaires:** Schedules (not questionnaires in NSSO terminology) are pre-tested, bilingual, covering the survey topic. **Schedule 1.0** (Household consumer expenditure) is a classic example. **Update:** Schedule 10 has been replaced by the **Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS)**.

**Sampling design used by NSSO (now NSO):**
- For PLFS, the NSO uses a **Rotational Panel Sampling design** in urban areas (visiting households 4 times with a 25% replacement rate) to accurately capture short-term changes in employment.
- **Stratified Multi-Stage Simple Random Sampling** is the standard design
- **First stage units (FSU):** Census villages (rural) / Urban Frame Survey (UFS) blocks (urban)
- **Second stage units (SSU):** Households within selected villages/blocks
- **Stratification:** By state, sector (rural/urban), population size
- **Within stratum:** Circular Systematic Sampling with PPS (probability proportional to size) for FSU selection; SRSWOR for household selection

**Field investigation:** Canvassed by trained investigators; pilot surveys conducted; data scrutinised at regional and central levels before tabulation.

---

### Probability vs Non-Probability Sampling

**Subjective (Purposive) Sampling:**
- Units selected based on investigator's judgment
- No probability mechanism; no valid measure of sampling error
- Fast and cheap but results not generalisable
- Example: Selecting "typical" villages to study rural income

**Probability Sampling (Random Sampling):**
- Every unit has a **known, non-zero** probability of selection
- Sampling error can be estimated objectively
- Results are unbiased and generalisable
- Forms the scientific basis of all large-scale surveys

sections.label: Simple Random Sampling (SRS)
sections.content:
## Simple Random Sampling — SRSWOR & SRSWR

### Definitions

**SRSWR (With Replacement):** At each draw, the selected unit is returned before the next draw. Same unit can appear more than once. Draws are independent.

**SRSWOR (Without Replacement):** Selected unit is not returned. No unit appears more than once. All \(\binom{N}{n}\) samples are equally likely.

In practice, **SRSWOR is preferred** (more efficient, no repetitions).

---

### Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| \(N\) | Population size |
| \(n\) | Sample size |
| \(Y_i\) | Value of \(i\)-th population unit |
| \(\bar{Y} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N Y_i\) | Population mean |
| \(Y = N\bar{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^N Y_i\) | Population total |
| \(S^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^N(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2\) | Population variance (finite pop.) |
| \(\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2\) | Population mean square |
| \(y_i\) | Value of \(i\)-th sampled unit |
| \(\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n y_i\) | Sample mean |
| \(s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\bar{y})^2\) | Sample variance |
| \(f = n/N\) | Sampling fraction |

---

### Estimation of Population Mean — SRSWOR

**Estimator:** \(\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n y_i\)

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\bar{y}) = \bar{Y}\) ✓

**Variance:**
\[
V(\bar{y}) = \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right)\frac{S^2}{n} = \frac{N-n}{N} \cdot \frac{S^2}{n}
\]

The factor \(\left(1 - \dfrac{n}{N}\right) = \dfrac{N-n}{N}\) is the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)**.

- FPC \(\to 1\) (i.e., negligible) when \(n/N \to 0\) (large population relative to sample)
- When \(n = N\): \(V(\bar{y}) = 0\) (complete enumeration)

**Unbiased estimator of variance:**
\[
\hat{V}(\bar{y}) = \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right)\frac{s^2}{n}
\]

**Standard Error:** \(\text{SE}(\bar{y}) = \sqrt{\hat{V}(\bar{y})}\)

**95% Confidence Interval for \(\bar{Y}\):**
\[
\bar{y} \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SE}(\bar{y})
\]

---

### Estimation of Population Mean — SRSWR

**Variance:**
\[
V(\bar{y}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\cdot\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2
\]

Note: **No FPC** in SRSWR. Since \(S^2 = \dfrac{N}{N-1}\sigma^2\):
\[
V_{\text{WR}}(\bar{y}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} > V_{\text{WOR}}(\bar{y}) = \frac{N-n}{N(n)} S^2 \cdot\frac{N-1}{N-1}
\]

**SRSWOR is always more efficient than SRSWR** for the same \(n\).

---

### Estimation of Population Total

\[
\hat{Y} = N\bar{y}
\]
\[
V(\hat{Y}) = N^2 V(\bar{y}) = N^2\left(1-\frac{n}{N}\right)\frac{S^2}{n} = N(N-n)\frac{S^2}{n}
\]

---

### Estimation of Population Proportion

Let \(A\) = number of units in population having attribute; \(P = A/N\) = population proportion.

**Sample estimator:** \(\hat{p} = a/n\) where \(a\) = number of units in sample with the attribute.

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\hat{p}) = P\) ✓

**Variance (SRSWOR):**
\[
V(\hat{p}) = \frac{N-n}{N-1}\frac{PQ}{n}
\]
where \(Q = 1 - P\).

**Estimated variance:**
\[
\hat{V}(\hat{p}) = \left(1-\frac{n}{N}\right)\frac{\hat{p}\hat{q}}{n-1}
\]

---

### Sample Size Determination

For estimating \(\bar{Y}\) with margin of error \(d\) at confidence level \((1-\alpha)\):
\[
n = \frac{N\,z_{\alpha/2}^2\,S^2}{N\,d^2 + z_{\alpha/2}^2\,S^2}
\]

For large \(N\) (ignoring FPC):
\[
n \approx \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2\,S^2}{d^2}
\]

> **ISS tip (Descriptive):** In a descriptive answer on SRS, always state: (1) the estimator, (2) prove unbiasedness by taking expectation, (3) derive the variance formula showing FPC, (4) give the unbiased variance estimator. The FPC term \((1-n/N)\) distinguishes SRSWOR from SRSWR — examiners expect you to comment on it. SRSWOR is always more efficient than SRSWR.

sections.label: Stratified Sampling & Allocation
sections.content:
## Stratified Random Sampling & Allocation Methods

### Why Stratify?

- Divide the heterogeneous population into \(L\) **homogeneous** subgroups (strata)
- Apply SRS independently within each stratum
- Guarantees representation of all subgroups
- Reduces variance compared to SRS when strata are internally homogeneous

---

### Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| \(L\) | Number of strata |
| \(N_h\) | Population size of stratum \(h\) |
| \(n_h\) | Sample size from stratum \(h\) |
| \(N = \sum N_h\) | Total population size |
| \(n = \sum n_h\) | Total sample size |
| \(W_h = N_h/N\) | Stratum weight |
| \(\bar{Y}_h\) | Stratum population mean |
| \(S_h^2\) | Stratum population variance |
| \(\bar{y}_h\) | Stratum sample mean |
| \(s_h^2\) | Stratum sample variance |

---

### Estimation of Population Mean

\[
\bar{y}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^L W_h \bar{y}_h = \sum_{h=1}^L \frac{N_h}{N}\bar{y}_h
\]

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\bar{y}_{st}) = \bar{Y}\) ✓

**Variance:**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^L W_h^2 \left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)\frac{S_h^2}{n_h}
\]

**Unbiased estimator of variance:**
\[
\hat{V(\bar{y}_{st})} = \sum_{h=1}^L W_h^2 \left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)\frac{s_h^2}{n_h}
\]

---

### Allocation Methods

**Proportional Allocation:**
\[
n_h = n \cdot \frac{N_h}{N} = n W_h
\]

- Sample size in each stratum is proportional to stratum size
- Self-weighting: \(\bar{y}_{st} = \bar{y}\) (overall sample mean)

**Variance under proportional allocation:**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{prop}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h^2 - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h^2
\]

For large \(N\):
\[
V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{prop}} \approx \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h^2
\]

**Optimum (Neyman) Allocation:**

Minimise \(V(\bar{y}_{st})\) for fixed \(n\), or minimise \(n\) for fixed variance:
\[
n_h = n \cdot \frac{N_h S_h}{\sum_{h=1}^L N_h S_h} = n \cdot \frac{W_h S_h}{\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h}
\]

- Strata with larger \(N_h\) or larger \(S_h\) get more sample
- Reduces to proportional allocation when all \(S_h\) are equal

**Variance under optimum allocation:**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h\right)^2 - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h S_h^2
\]

For large \(N\):
\[
V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{opt}} \approx \frac{\left(\sum_h W_h S_h\right)^2}{n}
\]

**Cost-optimum allocation** (when cost per unit \(c_h\) varies by stratum):
\[
n_h \propto \frac{N_h S_h}{\sqrt{c_h}}
\]

---

### Comparison: Stratified vs SRS (Fixed \(n\))

\[
V(\bar{y})_{\text{SRS}} - V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{prop}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h(\bar{Y}_h - \bar{Y})^2 \ge 0
\]

\[
V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{prop}} - V(\bar{y}_{st})_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^L W_h(S_h - \bar{S}_w)^2 \ge 0
\]

where \(\bar{S}_w = \sum_h W_h S_h\).

**Inequality chain:**
\[
V_{\text{opt}} \le V_{\text{prop}} \le V_{\text{SRS}}
\]

```chart
{"compute":"variance-hierarchy","title":"Variance Hierarchy: V(SRS) ≥ V(prop) ≥ V(opt)"}
```

Stratified SRS with proportional allocation **always** performs at least as well as SRS.

---

### Covariance and Variance Function

For two characteristics \(Y\) and \(Z\) measured on the same sample:

**Population covariance:**
\[
S_{YZ} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^N (Y_i - \bar{Y})(Z_i - \bar{Z})
\]

**Estimated covariance (Stratified):**
\[
\hat{\text{Cov}}(\bar{y}_{st}, \bar{z}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^L W_h^2 \left(1-\frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)\frac{s_{hyz}}{n_h}
\]
where \(s_{hyz} = \frac{1}{n_h-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_h}(y_{hi}-\bar{y}_h)(z_{hi}-\bar{z}_h)\)

**Variance function:** For a linear combination \(T = a\bar{y}_{st} + b\bar{z}_{st}\):
\[
V(T) = a^2 V(\bar{y}_{st}) + b^2 V(\bar{z}_{st}) + 2ab\,\text{Cov}(\bar{y}_{st},\bar{z}_{st})
\]

This is used in ratio and regression estimation to derive variance expressions.

> **ISS tip (Descriptive):** In a stratified sampling question, always: (1) write the estimator \(\bar{y}_{st} = \sum W_h\bar{y}_h\), (2) show unbiasedness, (3) give the variance formula, (4) derive the specific allocation asked (proportional or Neyman). For comparison questions, prove \(V_{\text{SRS}} \ge V_{\text{prop}} \ge V_{\text{opt}}\) using the decomposition identity — examiners specifically look for this chain of inequalities.

sections.label: Ratio, Product & Regression Estimation
sections.content:
## Auxiliary Variable Methods — Ratio, Product & Regression Estimation

### Why Use Auxiliary Information?

When an auxiliary variable \(X\) (correlated with \(Y\)) is known for the entire population, use it to improve estimation of \(\bar{Y}\) or \(Y\).

**Population quantities:** \(\bar{X}\) = known population mean of auxiliary variable, \(R = \bar{Y}/\bar{X}\) = population ratio.

---

### Ratio Estimator

\[
\bar{y}_R = \frac{\bar{y}}{\bar{x}} \cdot \bar{X} = \hat{R} \cdot \bar{X}
\]

where \(\hat{R} = \bar{y}/\bar{x}\) is the sample ratio.

**Bias:** Ratio estimator is **biased** in general. To first order of approximation:
\[
\text{Bias}(\bar{y}_R) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}\left[R\,S_x^2 - S_{xy}\cdot\frac{1}{\bar{X}}\cdot\bar{X}\right]\cdot\frac{1}{\bar{X}}
\]

More precisely, letting \(e_y = \bar{y} - \bar{Y}\) and \(e_x = \bar{x} - \bar{X}\):
\[
\text{Bias}(\bar{y}_R) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}\frac{1}{\bar{X}}\left(R\,S_x^2 - S_{yx}\right)
\]

where \(S_{yx} = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum(Y_i-\bar{Y})(X_i-\bar{X})\), \(f = n/N\).

**Bias is small when:** \(n\) is large, or \(\rho_{xy}\) is close to \(C_y/C_x\) (where \(C\) = coefficient of variation).

**Variance (to first order of approximation):**
\[
V(\bar{y}_R) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}\left(S_y^2 + R^2 S_x^2 - 2R\,S_{yx}\right) = \frac{1-f}{n}\left(S_y^2 + R^2 S_x^2 - 2R\rho S_y S_x\right)
\]

**Estimated variance:**
\[
\hat{V}(\bar{y}_R) = \frac{1-f}{n}\left(s_y^2 + \hat{R}^2 s_x^2 - 2\hat{R}\,s_{yx}\right)
\]

**When is ratio estimator better than SRS?**
\[
V(\bar{y}_R) < V(\bar{y}) \iff \rho > \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{C_x}{C_y}
\]

i.e., when \(\rho > C_x/(2C_y)\). In practice, ratio estimator is preferred when \(\rho > 0.5\) and \(C_x \approx C_y\).

---

### Product Estimator

Used when \(Y\) and \(X\) are **negatively** correlated:
\[
\bar{y}_P = \bar{y} \cdot \frac{\bar{x}}{\bar{X}}
\]

**Bias (first order):**
\[
\text{Bias}(\bar{y}_P) \approx -\frac{1-f}{n}\frac{S_{yx}}{\bar{X}}
\]

**Variance (first order):**
\[
V(\bar{y}_P) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}\left(S_y^2 + S_x^2 - 2\rho S_y S_x\right) \cdot \frac{\bar{Y}^2}{\bar{X}^2}
\]

Wait — more precisely:
\[
V(\bar{y}_P) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}\bar{Y}^2\left(C_y^2 + C_x^2 + 2\rho C_y C_x\right)
\]

**Product estimator is better than SRS when** \(\rho < -C_x/(2C_y)\) (strong negative correlation).

---

### Regression Estimator

\[
\bar{y}_{lr} = \bar{y} + \hat{b}(\bar{X} - \bar{x})
\]

where \(\hat{b} = s_{yx}/s_x^2\) is the sample regression coefficient of \(Y\) on \(X\).

**Bias:** Regression estimator is approximately **unbiased** (bias of order \(1/n^2\), negligible for large \(n\)).

**Variance (first order):**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{lr}) \approx \frac{1-f}{n}S_y^2(1-\rho^2)
\]

**Key result:** Regression estimator **always** has smaller (or equal) variance than SRS:
\[
V(\bar{y}_{lr}) \le V(\bar{y}) \quad \text{since } (1-\rho^2) \le 1
\]

**Comparison:**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{lr}) \le V(\bar{y}_R)
\]

Regression estimator is at least as efficient as the ratio estimator (equality when \(\hat{b} = \hat{R}\), i.e., when regression line passes through origin).

---

### Comparison Summary

| Estimator | Bias | Variance (approx.) | Best when |
|---|---|---|---|
| SRS mean \(\bar{y}\) | 0 | \(\frac{1-f}{n}S_y^2\) | No auxiliary info |
| Ratio \(\bar{y}_R\) | \(O(1/n)\), small | \(\frac{1-f}{n}(S_y^2+R^2S_x^2-2RS_{yx})\) | \(\rho > 0\), \(Y \propto X\) (line through origin) |
| Product \(\bar{y}_P\) | \(O(1/n)\), small | \(\frac{1-f}{n}\bar{Y}^2(C_y^2+C_x^2+2\rho C_yC_x)\) | \(\rho < 0\) |
| Regression \(\bar{y}_{lr}\) | \(\approx 0\) | \(\frac{1-f}{n}S_y^2(1-\rho^2)\) | Any \(\rho \ne 0\); always \(\le\) SRS |

> **ISS tip (Descriptive):** Ratio estimator assumes the relationship passes through the origin (\(y = Rx\)), while regression does not (\(y = a + bx\)), explaining why regression always performs at least as well. Ratio estimator is **biased**; regression estimator is approximately **unbiased** — state this explicitly. The condition for ratio estimator to beat SRS is \(\rho > C_x/(2C_y)\). Regression estimator variance is \(S_y^2(1-\rho^2)/n\) × FPC — always \(\le\) SRS, with equality only when \(\rho = 0\). In derivations, write \(\bar{y}_R = \bar{Y}(1+e_y)(1+e_x)^{-1}\), expand using \((1+e_x)^{-1} \approx 1-e_x+e_x^2\), take expectation — examiners look for this Taylor expansion technique.

sections.label: Systematic Sampling
sections.content:
## Systematic Sampling — Estimation & Comparison with SRS

### Definition

Select every \(k\)-th unit from the population after a random start, where \(k = N/n\) is the **sampling interval**.

**Condition for this section:** \(N = nk\) — \(N\) is an exact integer multiple of \(n\).

**Procedure:**
1. Arrange \(N\) units in a list numbered \(1\) to \(N\)
2. Choose a random start \(r\) uniformly from \(\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}\)
3. Select units: \(r,\; r+k,\; r+2k,\; \ldots,\; r+(n-1)k\)

The selected units form **one systematic sample**; there are exactly \(k\) possible samples, each of size \(n\).

---

### Estimation of Population Mean

**Estimator:** \(\bar{y}_{sys} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} y_{r+jk}\) = mean of the selected systematic sample.

**Unbiasedness:** Since each of the \(k\) possible samples is equally likely (probability \(1/k\)):
\[
E(\bar{y}_{sys}) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{r=1}^k \bar{y}_r = \bar{Y} \quad \checkmark
\]

**Variance:**
\[
V(\bar{y}_{sys}) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{r=1}^k (\bar{y}_r - \bar{Y})^2
\]

This can be expressed in terms of the **intraclass correlation** \(\rho_w\) between units in the same systematic sample:
\[
V(\bar{y}_{sys}) = \frac{N-1}{N}\frac{S^2}{n}\left[1 + (n-1)\rho_w\right]
\]

---

### Comparison with SRS

\[
V(\bar{y}_{sys}) = \frac{N-1}{nN}S^2\left[1+(n-1)\rho_w\right]
\]
\[
V(\bar{y})_{\text{SRS}} = \frac{N-n}{Nn}S^2 = \frac{N-1}{nN}S^2 - \frac{S^2}{N^2}
\]

Ignoring the small term \(S^2/N^2\):
\[
V(\bar{y}_{sys}) \lessgtr V(\bar{y})_{\text{SRS}} \iff \rho_w \lessgtr -\frac{1}{n-1}
\]

| Condition | Systematic vs SRS |
|---|---|
| \(\rho_w < -1/(n-1)\) | Systematic **more efficient** than SRS |
| \(\rho_w = 0\) | Systematic \(\approx\) SRS (for large \(N\)) |
| \(\rho_w > 0\) | Systematic **less efficient** than SRS |
| \(\rho_w = 1\) | Systematic very inefficient (units within sample identical) |

**Population in random order** (\(\rho_w \approx 0\)): Systematic \(\approx\) SRS.

**Population in trend** (\(\rho_w < 0\)): Systematic **better** than SRS.

**Population in periodic order** matching \(k\) (\(\rho_w > 0\)): Systematic **worse** than SRS (major drawback).

---

### Standard Error — Practical Problem

**Problem:** Only one systematic sample is drawn, so \(V(\bar{y}_{sys})\) cannot be estimated from the sample itself (there is only one \(\bar{y}_r\)).

**Solutions:**
1. **Treat as SRS** and use \(\hat{V} = (1-f)s^2/n\) — valid if population is in random order
2. **Successive differences estimator (assuming random order):**
\[
\hat{V}(\bar{y}_{sys}) = \frac{1-f}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{2(n-1)}\sum_{j=1}^{n-1}(y_{r+jk}-y_{r+(j-1)k})^2
\]
3. **Stratified systematic sampling:** Divide population into strata, take one systematic sample per stratum

> **ISS tip (Descriptive):** Systematic sampling has ONE critical weakness — **no unbiased variance estimator** from a single systematic sample. State this clearly in exams. The formula \(V(\bar{y}_{sys}) \propto[1+(n-1)\rho_w]\) is the key result — derive it by expressing the population in a two-way table of \(n\) columns and \(k\) rows, then use the ANOVA decomposition. When \(N \ne nk\), adjustments (circular systematic sampling) are needed.

sections.label: PPS Sampling & HT Estimator
sections.content:
## PPS Sampling — Hansen–Hurwitz, Des Raj, Das & Horvitz–Thompson

### Motivation

When units vary greatly in size (e.g., villages with different populations, firms with different output), give **larger units higher probability** of selection. This reduces variance compared to equal-probability sampling.

**Size measure:** \(X_i\) = known size of unit \(i\) (must be positive). \(M = \sum_{i=1}^N X_i\).

**Selection probability proportional to size:** \(p_i = X_i/M\)

---

### PPS With Replacement (PPSWR)

At each of \(n\) independent draws, unit \(i\) is selected with probability \(p_i = X_i/M\).

**Hansen–Hurwitz Estimator of population total:**
\[
\hat{Y}_{HH} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{y_i}{p_i}
\]

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\hat{Y}_{HH}) = Y\) ✓

**Variance:**
\[
V(\hat{Y}_{HH}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^N p_i\left(\frac{Y_i}{p_i} - Y\right)^2
\]

**Unbiased variance estimator:**
\[
\hat{V}(\hat{Y}_{HH}) = \frac{1}{n(n-1)}\sum_{i=1}^n\left(\frac{y_i}{p_i} - \hat{Y}_{HH}\right)^2
\]

---

### Des Raj Estimator for \(n = 2\) (PPSWOR)

For a sample of size \(n = 2\) selected without replacement by PPS:

Let unit \(i\) be drawn first with probability \(p_i\), and unit \(j\) second with conditional probability \(p_j/(1-p_i)\).

Define:
\[
d_1 = \frac{y_i}{p_i}, \qquad d_2 = y_i + \frac{y_j(1-p_i)}{p_j}
\]

**Des Raj ordered estimator:**
\[
\hat{Y}_{DR} = \frac{1}{2}(d_1 + d_2) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y_i}{p_i} + y_i + \frac{y_j(1-p_i)}{p_j}\right)
\]

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\hat{Y}_{DR}) = Y\) ✓

**Variance:**
\[
V(\hat{Y}_{DR}) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i \ne j} p_i p_j^* \left(\frac{Y_i}{p_i} - \frac{Y_j}{p_j}\right)^2
\]
where \(p_j^* = p_j/(1-p_i)\) is the conditional selection probability.

---

### Das Estimator for \(n = 2\)

**A.C. Das (1951)** proposed a set of **unordered** unbiased estimators for PPSWOR. Unlike Des Raj, the Das estimator at each draw does not depend on the \(y\)-values of previously selected units.

For \(n = 2\), if units \(i\) and \(j\) are selected (regardless of order), the Das estimator is:
\[
\hat{Y}_{\text{Das}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y_i}{p_i} + \frac{y_j}{p_j}\right)(p_i + p_j)
\]

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\hat{Y}_{\text{Das}}) = Y\) ✓

**Key distinction from Des Raj:** Des Raj uses an *ordered* estimator (depends on which unit was drawn first); Das uses an *unordered* estimator (symmetric in \(i\) and \(j\)). Both are unbiased for the same PPSWOR design.

---

### Horvitz–Thompson (HT) Estimator

The most general estimator for **any** sampling design where inclusion probabilities are known.

**First-order inclusion probability:** \(\pi_i = P(\text{unit } i \text{ is included in sample})\)

**Second-order inclusion probability:** \(\pi_{ij} = P(\text{both units } i \text{ and } j \text{ are included})\)

**HT Estimator of population total:**
\[
\hat{Y}_{HT} = \sum_{i \in s} \frac{y_i}{\pi_i}
\]

**Unbiasedness:** \(E(\hat{Y}_{HT}) = Y\) ✓ (for any design with \(\pi_i > 0\))

**Variance (Horvitz–Thompson):**
\[
V(\hat{Y}_{HT}) = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{1-\pi_i}{\pi_i}Y_i^2 + \sum_{i\ne j}\frac{\pi_{ij}-\pi_i\pi_j}{\pi_i\pi_j}Y_iY_j
\]

Or equivalently (Sen–Yates–Grundy form):
\[
V(\hat{Y}_{HT}) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i\ne j}(\pi_i\pi_j-\pi_{ij})\left(\frac{Y_i}{\pi_i}-\frac{Y_j}{\pi_j}\right)^2
\]
