The variance does not decrease with sample size — periodogram values at different frequencies are asymptotically independent but each has variance \( \approx f(\omega)^2 \).

**Smoothed Periodogram (consistent estimator):**
To get a consistent estimate, apply a **spectral window** \( \{w_s\} \) (kernel smoother):
\[
\hat{f}(\omega) = \sum_s w_s I(\omega + \omega_s)
\]
Common windows: Bartlett, Parzen, Daniell, Tukey–Hanning. As bandwidth \( \to 0 \) and number of averaged periodogram ordinates \( \to \infty \) (slowly), \( \hat{f}(\omega) \xrightarrow{p} f(\omega) \).

**Computations using FFT:** The DFT of \( n \) points can be computed in \( O(n\log n) \) operations using the Fast Fourier Transform (Cooley–Tukey algorithm) rather than \( O(n^2) \) for direct computation.

---

### Correlogram vs Periodogram

| Feature | Correlogram | Periodogram |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Time domain | Frequency domain |
| Plots | \( \hat{\rho}_k \) vs lag \( k \) | \( I(\omega_j) \) vs frequency \( \omega_j \) |
| Use | Identify ARMA order; test white noise | Detect hidden periodicities; estimate spectrum |
| Consistency | \( \hat{\rho}_k \xrightarrow{p} \rho_k \) ✓ | \( I(\omega_j) \) is inconsistent ✗ (raw) |
| Relationship | Fourier transform pair (Wiener–Khintchine) | |

> **ISS tip (Descriptive):** Three things examiners always check in spectral analysis questions: (1) State the inversion formula \( \gamma_k = \int e^{i\omega k}f(\omega)d\omega \) and set \( k=0 \) to get \( \gamma_0 = \int f(\omega)d\omega \) (total variance). (2) State that the raw periodogram is **asymptotically unbiased but inconsistent** — variance does not go to zero. (3) Smoothing is required for a consistent spectral estimator. The Correlogram and Periodogram are Fourier transform duals — state the Wiener–Khintchine theorem explicitly. For Wold's decomposition: every stationary process = MA(∞) + deterministic part — this justifies Box–Jenkins.

applied.tips:
**Applied Statistics — Final Tips & Exam Strategy:**

1. **Index Numbers — Two guaranteed derivations:** (1) Prove Fisher satisfies TRT: write \( P_{01}^F \times P_{10}^F \), expand both square roots as products of Laspeyres and Paasche, show exact cancellation gives 1. (2) Prove Fisher satisfies FRT: swap \( p \) and \( q \) to form \( Q_{01}^F \), multiply \( P_{01}^F \times Q_{01}^F \), show it equals \( \sum p_1q_1/\sum p_0q_0 \). Write every summation step — partial proofs lose marks.
2. **Demand Analysis — Leontief vs Pigou:** State the data source clearly: Leontief uses time-series (estimates price and income elasticity jointly); Pigou uses cross-section family budget data (estimates income elasticity across households at a point in time). This distinction is a direct exam question.
3. **Trend Determination — All 3 methods:** Know: (1) Least squares — set up and solve normal equations with centred time; (2) Moving averages — state the centring procedure for even \( k \) explicitly; (3) Semi-averages — state limitations. For seasonal indices, normalise so they sum to 1200 (monthly) or 400 (quarterly).
4. **ARIMA identification — The master table:** AR(p): PACF cuts off at \( p \), ACF tails off. MA(q): ACF cuts off at \( q \), PACF tails off. ARMA(p,q): both tail off. White noise: both near zero for \( k>0 \). Write this table in every Box–Jenkins answer. Also state: MA invertibility condition (roots outside unit circle) is needed to write MA as infinite AR.
5. **Spectral Analysis — Three key facts:** (1) \( \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\omega)d\omega = \gamma_0 \) (total variance = area under spectrum). (2) Raw periodogram is asymptotically unbiased but **inconsistent** — smoothing is necessary. (3) Correlogram and Periodogram are Fourier transform duals (Wiener–Khintchine theorem). State Wold's decomposition theorem to justify existence of MA representation.
6. **Hawkins–Simon conditions:** Always state both: \( |I-A| > 0 \) and all diagonal elements of \( (I-A) \) are positive. Give the economic rationale — an industry cannot consume more of its own output than it produces. Practice \( 3\times3 \) matrix inversion for numerical I–O questions.
7. **NSO base year update (2022-23):** The new GDP base year series (released February 2026) replaced 2011-12. Mention this in national income answers — it shows contemporary awareness which examiners reward.

demography.title: Demography and Vital Statistics

sections.label: Sources of demographic data & Indian Census
sections.content:
## Sources of Demographic Data & Indian Census Profile

### 1. Sources of Demographic Data

Demographic data originate from four institutional sources, each with distinct coverage and quality trade-offs:

| Source | Description | Key Strength | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Population Census** | Complete enumeration at a point in time | Universal coverage; baseline for all national projections | Decennial; expensive; under-enumeration of marginalised groups |
| **Civil Registration System (CRS)** | Continuous administrative recording of births, deaths, marriages | Legal authority; continuous | Incomplete in rural/remote areas; cause-of-death coding unreliable |
| **Sample Surveys (SRS, NFHS, DLHS)** | Periodic probability-sample field surveys | Detailed fertility/health histories; faster turnaround | Sampling error; rare events under-captured |
| **Hospital / Institutional Records** | Events at health facilities | Useful for cause-of-death, maternal and infant outcomes | Misses home births/deaths; urban/facility bias |

---

### 2. Sample Registration System (SRS) — India's Flagship

Launched by the Registrar General of India (RGI) in **1969**. Uses a **dual-record design**:
1. A local part-time enumerator continuously registers births and deaths in a sample of villages/urban blocks.
2. An independent retrospective household survey cross-checks the same events.
3. Unmatched records are reconciled via field enquiry; the match rate gives the completeness factor.

SRS is the **only source of annual sub-national CBR, CDR, IMR** estimates in India.

---

### 3. National Family Health Survey (NFHS) & Other Surveys

- **NFHS** (1992–93, 1998–99, 2005–06, 2015–16, 2019–21): Covers fertility, mortality, nutrition, family planning across all districts.
- **DLHS** (District Level Household & Facility Survey): District-level data on reproductive and child health.
- **PLFS** (Periodic Labour Force Survey): Labour-force participation and internal migration data.
- **AHS** (Annual Health Survey): State-level health indicators for high-focus states.

---

### 4. Demographic Profile — India Census 2011

India's decennial census is conducted under the **Census Act, 1948** by the RGI. The **de jure** method is used (usual place of residence).

| Indicator | Value (Census 2011) |
|---|---|
| Total population | 121.09 crore (17.5% of world) |
| Decadal growth (2001–11) | 17.64% |
| Population density | 382/km² |
| Sex ratio (all ages) | 943 females/1000 males |
| Child sex ratio (0–6 yrs) | 914 females/1000 males |
| Literacy rate | 74.04% (Male 82.1%, Female 65.5%) |
| Urban proportion | 31.16% |
| Scheduled Castes | 16.6% of population |
| Scheduled Tribes | 8.6% of population |

**Two phases of Census:**
- **Phase 1:** House-listing and Housing Census — structures, amenities, assets
- **Phase 2:** Population Enumeration — age, sex, education, occupation, migration, language

**2021 Census:** Postponed indefinitely due to COVID-19 pandemic; as of 2025 not yet conducted.

*Real-life example:* The 2011 Census revealed the child sex ratio (CSR 0–6) had fallen to **914** — the lowest since Independence. This single data point triggered the **Beti Bachao Beti Padhao** programme (2015), launched in the 100 most sex-ratio-distorted districts identified through Census maps. It also revealed ~65 million urban slum dwellers (enumerated in slum enumeration blocks for the first time), shaping the Smart Cities Mission targeting.

> **ISS tip:** The four sources must be stated with their limitations — a guaranteed 5-mark question. For SRS: the key feature is the **dual-record system** (enumerator + independent retrospective survey). Indian Census uses **de jure** (usual residence) not de facto. Census Act: **1948**. The Census 2011 child sex ratio (914) and literacy (74.04%) are direct MCQ targets. 2021 Census is postponed — always mention this when asked about current data.

sections.label: Complete life table & Makeham/Gompertz laws
sections.content:
## Complete Life Table & Mortality Laws (Makeham & Gompertz)

### 1. Complete Life Table — All Columns

A **life table** (mortality table) traces a **synthetic cohort** of \( l_0 = 100{,}000 \) hypothetical births, applying observed age-specific death rates at each exact age. It is called "complete" when computed for single-year age groups.

#### Standard Columns and Definitions

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| \( x \) | Age | Exact age at start of year-of-life \([x,\, x+1)\) |
| \( l_x \) | Survivors | Number alive at exact age \( x \); radix \( l_0 = 100{,}000 \) |
| \( d_x \) | Deaths in interval | \( d_x = l_x - l_{x+1} \) |
| \( q_x \) | Probability of dying | \( q_x = d_x / l_x \) |
| \( p_x \) | Probability of surviving | \( p_x = 1 - q_x = l_{x+1}/l_x \) |
| \( L_x \) | Person-years lived in \([x,x+1)\) | \( L_x \approx \tfrac{1}{2}(l_x + l_{x+1}) \) for non-infants |
| \( T_x \) | Total future person-years from age \( x \) | \( T_x = \sum_{y=x}^{\omega} L_y \) |
| \( e_x^\circ \) | Expectation of life at age \( x \) | \( e_x^\circ = T_x / l_x \) |
| \( m_x \) | Central death rate | \( m_x = d_x / L_x \) |
| \( \mu_x \) | Force of mortality (instantaneous hazard) | \( \mu_x = -\dfrac{d}{dx}\ln l(x) \) |

```chart
{"compute":"survivor","title":"lₓ Survivor Function — synthetic cohort of 100,000; decline accelerates after age 60"}
```

#### Key Recursive Relationships

\[
d_x = l_x - l_{x+1}, \qquad q_x = \frac{d_x}{l_x}, \qquad p_x = \frac{l_{x+1}}{l_x}
\]

\[
T_x = T_{x+1} + L_x \qquad \text{(recurse backward from the terminal age } \omega \text{)}
\]

\[
e_x^\circ = \frac{T_x}{l_x}; \qquad e_0^\circ = \frac{T_0}{l_0}
\]

**Reed–Merrell formula** — connecting \( q_x \) and \( m_x \) (assumes deaths uniformly distributed in interval):
\[
q_x = \frac{2\,m_x}{2 + m_x} \iff m_x = \frac{2\,q_x}{2 - q_x}
\]

**Force of mortality and survival function:**
\[
l(x) = l_0\,\exp\!\left(-\int_0^x \mu_t\,dt\right); \qquad \mu_x = -\frac{d\ln l(x)}{dx}
\]

**Infant correction** — \( L_0 \): deaths cluster early in year 0, so the uniform assumption fails:
\[
L_0 = \alpha_0\,l_0 + (1-\alpha_0)\,l_1
\]
where \( \alpha_0 \approx 0.07 \) in high-mortality settings and \( \approx 0.14 \) in low-mortality settings.

#### Uses of the Life Table

1. **Actuarial pricing:** Annuity, whole-life, and term insurance premia use \( q_x \), \( L_x \), \( T_x \).
2. **Population projection:** Survival ratios \( {}_5L_{x+5}/{}_5L_x \) used in cohort-component projection.
3. **Longevity benchmarking:** \( e_0^\circ \) is the health component of the Human Development Index.
4. **Age-standardisation of death rates:** Removes distortion from differing age structures.
5. **Pension/social-security costing:** Government actuaries use \( T_x \) to estimate pension liability.

*Real-life example:* LIC of India prices ₹1 crore term insurance using \( q_x \) from IRDAI's **Indian Assured Lives Mortality (IALM 2012–15)** tables. A 30-year-old non-smoker male has \( q_{30} \approx 0.00113 \) — LIC expects ~113 claims per year per 1,00,000 policies, directly determining the actuarially fair annual premium. India's national life tables (RGI, **2015–19**) show \( e_0^\circ \) = 69.4 years (Male), 72.0 years (Female) — up from ~32 years in 1951.

---

### 2. Makeham and Gompertz Laws of Mortality

These are **parametric models** for the age pattern of the force of mortality \( \mu_x \).

#### Gompertz Law (1825)
\[
\mu_x = B\,c^x \quad (B > 0,\; c > 1)
\]
- Force of mortality increases **geometrically** with age.
- Taking logarithm: \( \ln \mu_x = \ln B + x\ln c \) — **linear on a log-scale** (OLS-fittable on adult ages 30–80).
- Survival: \( l(x) = l_0\,\exp\!\Bigl[-\dfrac{B}{\ln c}(c^x - 1)\Bigr] \)

#### Makeham Law (1860) — Extension
\[
\mu_x = A + B\,c^x \quad (A \ge 0,\; B > 0,\; c > 1)
\]
- Adds a **constant** age-invariant hazard \( A \) = exogenous mortality (accidents, infections, violence).
- \( Bc^x \) retains the Gompertz senescence component.
- Survival: \( l(x) = l_0\,s^x\,\exp\!\Bigl[-\dfrac{B}{\ln c}(c^x-1)\Bigr] \), where \( s = e^{-A} \).
- Setting \( A = 0 \) recovers the Gompertz law.

| Law | \( \mu_x \) | Parameters | Key feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gompertz | \( Bc^x \) | \( B,\,c \) | Log-linear; 2 parameters |
| Makeham | \( A + Bc^x \) | \( A,\,B,\,c \) | Adds accident constant; 3 parameters |

**Fitting Makeham:** Estimate \( A \) from cause-specific accident deaths; then regress \( \ln(m_x - A) \) on \( x \) by OLS to recover \( B \) and \( c \).

*Real-life example:* The Human Mortality Database reports \( c \approx 1.09 \) for adult males across many populations — human senescent mortality **roughly doubles every 8 years** after age 40 (\( \ln 2/\ln c \approx 8 \)). India's constant \( A \) is substantially higher than OECD levels (infectious disease, road-traffic burden), making Makeham a materially better fit than Gompertz for Indian working-age mortality data.

> **ISS tip:** Life table column sequence is a guaranteed write-out question: \( l_x \to d_x \to q_x \to L_x \to T_x \to e_x^\circ \). The two most-tested formulas: (1) \( e_x^\circ = T_x/l_x \) and (2) \( T_x = T_{x+1} + L_x \) (the recursive backward formula). Reed–Merrell: \( q_x = 2m_x/(2+m_x) \). Makeham vs Gompertz: Makeham adds the accident constant \( A \); setting \( A=0 \) gives Gompertz. The India \( e_0^\circ \) figures (69.4 M, 72.0 F; 2015–19) are quoted in answer scripts.

sections.label: UN model tables, abridged tables & stable/stationary populations
sections.content:
## National & UN Model Life Tables, Abridged Tables, Stable & Stationary Populations

### 1. National Life Tables — India (RGI)

- Computed from SRS age-specific death rates combined with Census age distribution.
- Published quinquennially. Latest: **2015–19** — \( e_0^\circ \): Male 69.4 yrs, Female 72.0 yrs.
- Historical trend: \( e_0^\circ \) rose from ~32 yrs (1951) → 58 yrs (1981) → 65 yrs (2001) → 69.4 yrs (2015–19).

---

### 2. Coale–Demeny Model Life Tables (UN; Princeton, 1966, revised 1983)

Coale and Demeny compiled **326 empirically reliable national life tables**, identified four regional age-patterns of mortality by principal-components analysis, and created **four regional families** with **25 mortality levels** each.

**Levels:** Level 1 = \( e_0^\circ \approx 20 \) yrs (very high mortality); Level 25 = \( e_0^\circ \approx 80 \) yrs (very low mortality). Two-way interpolation gives a life table for any target \( e_0^\circ \) and sex.

| Family | Characteristic age-pattern | Historical origin |
|---|---|---|
| **North** | High old-age (50+) mortality relative to child mortality | Scandinavia (historical) |
| **South** | Elevated infant and early-childhood mortality | Southern Europe (historical) |
| **East** | High child (1–4) mortality relative to adult | Central Europe (historical) |
| **West** | Most general / residual — **default for Asia, Africa, India** | Broadly applicable |

**Usage in India:** When district/state-level SRS samples are insufficient for reliable \( _5m_x \), analysts specify \( e_0^\circ \) from SRS and read off \( _5q_x \) from the West-family model table at that level.

*Real-life example:* The India State-Level Disease Burden Initiative (2017) used West-family model life tables calibrated to SRS-derived \( e_0^\circ \) to estimate life expectancy for districts where SRS coverage was below 90%, enabling the Niti Aayog **Health Index** ranking of all 36 states/UTs.

---

### 3. Abridged Life Tables

Abridged life tables use **5-year age groups** \([x, x+5)\) instead of single years:

| Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|
| \( {}_5q_x \) | Prob. of dying between exact age \( x \) and \( x+5 \) |
| \( {}_5m_x \) | Observed 5-year central death rate |
| \( {}_5L_x \) | \( \approx 5 \times \tfrac{l_x + l_{x+5}}{2} \) (most adult ages) |
| \( {}_5a_x \) | Average person-years lived by those dying in interval (\( \approx 2.5 \) for most ages) |

**Reed–Merrell formula (1933):**
\[
{}_5q_x = 1 - \exp\!\left(-5\,{}_5m_x - 0.008\,({}_5m_x)^2 \times 125\right)
\]

**Greville formula (used in SRS abridged tables):**
\[
{}_5q_x \approx \frac{5\,{}_5m_x}{1 + (5 - {}_5a_x)\,{}_5m_x}, \qquad {}_5a_x \approx 2.5 \text{ (adult ages)}
\]

*Real-life example:* India's SRS abridged life tables (2016–20) are published in 5-year groups because state-level SRS samples are too small for reliable single-year \( _1m_x \). Complete single-year tables are released only at the national level.

---

### 4. Stable Population Theory (Lotka, 1907)

**Stable population:** A closed population with **constant** age-specific mortality AND fertility rates. Regardless of the initial age structure, it converges to a fixed age composition and a constant intrinsic growth rate \( r \).

**Age density of stable population:**
\[
c(x) = b\,e^{-rx}\,\ell(x), \qquad \ell(x) = l(x)/l_0
\]
where \( b \) = crude birth rate of the stable population, \( r \) = intrinsic rate of natural increase.

**Euler–Lotka Characteristic Equation** (determines \( r \)):
\[
1 = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} e^{-rx}\,\ell(x)\,m(x)\,dx
\]
where \( m(x) \) = age-specific maternity rate (female births per woman aged \( x \)), \( \alpha \approx 15 \), \( \beta \approx 49 \). Solved **iteratively** (Newton–Raphson or bisection) for \( r \).

**Mean generation length:**
\[
T = \frac{\ln R_0}{r}, \qquad R_0 = \text{NRR} = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \ell(x)\,m(x)\,dx
\]

---

### 5. Stationary Population — Special Case (\( r = 0 \))

A **stationary population** is a stable population with \( r = 0 \):
- Closed (no migration), constant age-specific mortality, **constant annual births**.
- Total size **constant**; **CBR = CDR = \( 1/e_0^\circ \)** (a key numerical shortcut).
- Age composition: \( c_s(x) = L_x/T_0 = L_x/(e_0^\circ \cdot l_0) \) — proportional to the \( L_x \) column of the life table.

| Feature | Stationary \( (r=0) \) | Stable \( (r>0) \) | Stable \( (r<0) \) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Growth | None | Exponential growth | Exponential decline |
| Age structure | \( \propto L_x \) (older) | Younger than stationary | Older than stationary |
| CBR vs CDR | Equal | CBR \( > \) CDR | CBR \( < \) CDR |
| NRR | Exactly 1 | \( > 1 \) | \( < 1 \) |

**Quasi-stable population:** Mortality is declining faster than fertility in many developing countries → not truly stable. Analysts use recent mortality but treat fertility as fixed.

*Real-life example:* Coale and Hoover (1958) applied stable-population theory to India's 1951 data to project population to 2000 and model how fertility decline would raise per-capita savings. Their finding that high fertility depresses living standards directly influenced India's launch of the **world's first national family-planning programme in 1952**.

> **ISS tip:** The Euler–Lotka equation is the single most-tested formula in stable-population theory — derive it by setting up the birth-flow renewal equation \( B(t) = \int_\alpha^\beta B(t-x)\ell(x)m(x)dx \) and setting \( B(t) = b_0 e^{rt} \). Key: setting \( r=0 \) gives NRR = 1 (stationary condition). Stationary ≠ stable (stable is the general case; stationary is the special case \( r=0 \)). In a stationary population: CBR = CDR = \( 1/e_0^\circ \) — a direct numerical shortcut. UN West family is the default for India. Four model-table families: North (old-age heavy), South (infant heavy), East (child heavy), West (general).

sections.label: Fertility measures: CBR, GFR, ASFR, TFR, GRR, NRR
sections.content:
## Measurement of Fertility: CBR, GFR, ASFR, TFR, GRR, NRR

### Overview

Fertility measures form a hierarchy: each successive measure controls for more confounding factors (age structure, sex composition, reproductive span, child mortality). The key sequence is:
\[
\text{CBR} \xrightarrow{\text{restrict denominator}} \text{GFR} \xrightarrow{\text{age-specific}} \text{ASFR} \xrightarrow{\text{sum}} \text{TFR} \xrightarrow{\text{female births}} \text{GRR} \xrightarrow{\text{+ survival}} \text{NRR}
\]

---

### 1. Crude Birth Rate (CBR)

\[
\text{CBR} = \frac{\text{Registered live births in year}}{\text{Mid-year total population}} \times 1000
\]
- Denominator: **all persons** (including non-reproductive ages and males).
- Simplest to compute; heavily influenced by the age-sex structure.
- **India SRS 2021:** CBR ≈ 19.5 per 1000.

*Real-life example:* Bihar (CBR ≈ 25.6) vs Kerala (CBR ≈ 14.2) — the difference partly reflects Bihar's younger and more rural population, not only higher fertility. Comparing CBRs across states without age-standardisation is misleading; this is why TFR is preferred.

---

### 2. General Fertility Rate (GFR)

\[
\text{GFR} = \frac{\text{Live births in year}}{\text{Mid-year women aged 15–49}} \times 1000
\]
- Denominator restricted to **women of reproductive age (15–49)** — a closer approximation to the at-risk population.
- Still confounded by the age distribution **within** 15–49.

---

### 3. Age-Specific Fertility Rate (ASFR)

\[
f_x = \frac{\text{Live births to women aged } [x, x+5)}{\text{Mid-year women aged } [x, x+5)} \times 1000
\]
Computed for 7 five-year groups: 15–19, 20–24, 25–29, 30–34, 35–39, 40–44, 45–49.

- Controls completely for **age composition** of mothers.
- The full set of ASFRs is the complete fertility schedule.

---

### 4. Total Fertility Rate (TFR)

\[
\text{TFR} = n \sum_{x} f_x = 5\sum_{x=15,20,\ldots,45} f_x \qquad (n = 5 \text{ for 5-year groups})
\]
- Interpretation: **average number of children a woman would bear** if she survived through the entire reproductive period and experienced the current ASFRs at each age. A **period** (not cohort) measure.
- **Replacement-level TFR ≈ 2.1** (slightly above 2.0 to offset female infant mortality before age 15).
- **India NFHS-5 (2019–21): TFR = 2.0** — below replacement for the **first time** in Indian history.

*Real-life example:* India's TFR declined from 5.9 (1960) to 2.0 (2021) — one of the fastest demographic transitions globally. At TFR = 2.0, India is at below-replacement fertility, yet the population will continue growing for decades due to **population momentum** (large cohorts of young women entering reproductive age).

---

### 5. Gross Reproduction Rate (GRR)

\[
\text{GRR} = n\sum_{x} f_x^{(F)} = \text{TFR} \times \text{Proportion of female births} \approx \text{TFR} \times 0.4879
\]
- Counts only **female births** per woman — measures female-to-female replacement.
- Ignores the mortality of daughters before reaching reproductive age.
- \( \text{GRR} < \text{TFR} \) always (since only ~48.8% of births are female).

---

### 6. Net Reproduction Rate (NRR) — the definitive replacement measure

\[
\text{NRR} = n\sum_x f_x^{(F)} \cdot \frac{{}_5L_x}{l_0} = \int_\alpha^\beta \ell(x)\,m(x)\,dx
\]
where \( \ell(x) = l_x/l_0 \) = probability a newborn girl survives to age \( x \), \( m(x) \) = age-specific female birth rate.

| NRR value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| \( = 1 \) | Each generation exactly replaces itself; stationary population in the long run |
| \( > 1 \) | Population growing; each mother replaced by more than one daughter |
| \( < 1 \) | Population declining; daughters fewer than mothers |

**Key relationship:**
\[
\text{NRR} = \text{GRR} \times \bar{\ell}
\]
where \( \bar{\ell} \) = average survival probability of daughters over reproductive ages.

**Hierarchy:**
\[
\text{NRR} \le \text{GRR} \le \frac{\text{TFR}}{2.05}
\]

---

### Summary Table — All Fertility Measures

| Measure | Numerator | Denominator | Per | Controls age? | Controls mortality? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CBR | All live births | Total mid-year pop | 1,000 | No | No |
| GFR | All live births | Women 15–49 | 1,000 | Partially | No |
| ASFR \( f_x \) | Births to women \([x,x+5)\) | Women \([x,x+5)\) | 1,000 | Yes | No |
| TFR | \( 5\sum f_x \) | — | — | Yes | No |
| GRR | \( 5\sum f_x^{(F)} \) | — | — | Yes | No |
| NRR | \( 5\sum f_x^{(F)} \cdot {}_5L_x/l_0 \) | — | — | Yes | **Yes** |

*Real-life example:* India's NRR fell below 1.0 in southern states (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh) around 2018–19, indicating long-run population decline in those states — a result of both low ASFR and improved female survival (\( \ell_x \)). This precedes the national NRR crossing below 1.0 by several years.

> **ISS tip:** The denominators are the most-tested trap: CBR and CDR use **total mid-year population**; GFR uses **women 15–49**; ASFR uses **women in each age group**; IMR uses **live births**. NRR is the **only** fertility measure incorporating mortality (via \( \ell_x = l_x/l_0 \)). TFR is a period measure, not cohort. Replacement level is 2.1 (not 2.0) because of female infant mortality. NRR = 1 ↔ stationary population in long run — this directly connects to the Euler–Lotka equation (setting \( r = 0 \)).

sections.label: Mortality: CDR, standardised rates, IMR & cause-specific
sections.content:
## Measurement of Mortality: CDR, Standardised Rates, IMR & Cause-Specific Rates

### 1. Crude Death Rate (CDR)

\[
\text{CDR} = \frac{\text{Registered deaths in year}}{\text{Mid-year total population}} \times 1000
\]
- Denominator: all persons; heavily influenced by age structure.
- **India SRS 2021:** CDR ≈ 6.2 per 1000 — historically low, reflecting India's relatively young age structure.

*Trap:* A country with an older population can have a higher CDR than a healthier younger country. Age-standardisation is needed for valid comparisons.

---

### 2. Age-Specific Death Rate (ASDR / ASMR)

\[
{}_nm_x = \frac{\text{Deaths in age group } [x,\, x+n)}{\text{Mid-year population aged } [x,\, x+n)} \times 1000
\]
- Removes confounding by age structure; comparable across populations.
- Usually computed for 5-year groups: 0, 1–4, 5–9, ..., 80+.

---

### 3. Standardised Death Rates (SDR)

Used to compare mortality across populations with different age structures.

#### 3.1 Direct Standardisation

\[
\text{SDR}_{\text{direct}} = \frac{\sum_x m_x^{(s)}\, P_x^{(\text{std})}}{\sum_x P_x^{(\text{std})}} = \sum_x m_x^{(s)}\, w_x^{(\text{std})}
\]
where \( m_x^{(s)} \) = study population's ASDR, \( P_x^{(\text{std})} \) = standard population's age-group size, \( w_x^{(\text{std})} = P_x^{(\text{std})}/\sum P_x^{(\text{std})} \) = age weights.

- **Requires knowing** the study population's ASDRs at each age.
- Result: the CDR the study population **would have** if it had the standard age structure.
- **Standard population choices:** WHO World Standard (2001), India 1991 Census (used by RGI).

#### 3.2 Indirect Standardisation — Standardised Mortality Ratio (SMR)

\[
\text{SMR} = \frac{\text{Observed deaths in study population}}{\text{Expected deaths}} \times 100
\]
\[
\text{Expected deaths} = \sum_x m_x^{(\text{std})} \times P_x^{(s)}
\]
where \( m_x^{(\text{std})} \) = standard population's ASDRs, \( P_x^{(s)} \) = study population's age-group sizes.

- Use when study population's ASDRs are **unreliable** (small cell counts).
- SMR > 100: study population has **excess mortality** vs standard.
- SMR < 100: study population has **lower mortality** vs standard.

| Method | Requires | Use when |
|---|---|---|
| Direct (SDR) | Study ASDRs + standard age weights | Study ASDRs are reliable |
| Indirect (SMR) | Standard ASDRs + study age counts | Study cell counts are small |

*Real-life example:* RGI uses direct standardisation (1991 Census as standard) to compare state CDRs in Annual Health Survey reports — this removes the artefact that Kerala (older population) appears to have a higher CDR than Bihar (younger population) even though Kerala has vastly better health outcomes.

---

### 4. Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)

\[
\text{IMR} = \frac{\text{Deaths under 1 year of age in year } t}{\text{Live births in year } t} \times 1000
\]
- Denominator: **live births** (not population) — a hybrid cohort/period measure.
- Highly sensitive indicator of public health, nutrition, and maternal care quality.
- **India SRS 2021:** IMR = **25** per 1000 live births (down from 146 in 1971).

**Components of infant mortality:**

| Component | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Neonatal mortality rate (NMR)** | Deaths 0–28 days / live births × 1000 |
| **Post-neonatal mortality rate** | Deaths 29 days–11 months / live births × 1000 |
| **Perinatal mortality rate** | (Stillbirths + deaths 0–6 days) / (live births + stillbirths) × 1000 |
| **Under-5 mortality rate (U5MR)** | Deaths under 5 / live births × 1000 |

*Real-life example:* Kerala's IMR (6 per 1000, SRS 2021) vs Uttar Pradesh's IMR (38 per 1000) reflects disparities in institutional deliveries and skilled birth attendance — data that drives PMSMA (Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan) programme targeting.

---

### 5. Death Rate by Cause (Cause-Specific Mortality Rate)

\[
\text{CSMR}_k = \frac{\text{Deaths from cause } k \text{ in year}}{\text{Mid-year population}} \times 100{,}000
\]
- Expressed per **1,00,000** population (unlike CDR which uses per 1,000).
- Source in India: **Medical Certification of Cause of Death (MCCD)** — covers ~27% of registered deaths (mostly urban/institutional).
- The **Global Burden of Disease (GBD)** study uses cause-of-death models to impute CSMR for India at state level.

**Age-Standardised CSMR:** Apply direct standardisation to CSMR using a standard population (e.g., WHO World Standard Population 2001).

*Real-life example:* The India State-Level Disease Burden Initiative (Lancet 2017) estimated that cardiovascular disease overtook communicable diseases as India's leading cause of death around 2000, using MCCD data supplemented by verbal autopsy surveys — directly influencing the National Programme for NCD Prevention and Control.

---

### Summary Table — All Mortality Measures

| Measure | Numerator | Denominator | Per | Controls age? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CDR | All deaths | Total mid-year pop | 1,000 | No |
| ASDR \( _nm_x \) | Deaths in \([x,x+n)\) | Pop in \([x,x+n)\) | 1,000 | Yes |
| SDR (direct) | \( \sum m_x^{(s)} w_x^{(\text{std})} \) | — | 1,000 | Yes |
| SMR (indirect) | Obs./Exp. × 100 | — | — | Yes |
| IMR | Deaths < 1 yr | Live births | 1,000 | N/A |
| NMR | Deaths 0–28 days | Live births | 1,000 | N/A |
| CSMR | Deaths from cause \( k \) | Total mid-year pop | 1,00,000 | No |

> **ISS tip:** The IMR denominator is **live births** — not mid-year population. This is the most common MCQ trap in mortality measurement. Direct standardisation needs study ASDRs; indirect (SMR) uses only observed totals + standard rates. SMR > 100 = excess mortality. India IMR (SRS 2021): 25. MCCD covers only ~27% of registered deaths (urban-hospital biased). CSMR is per **1,00,000** not per 1,000 — distinguish from CDR.

sections.label: Migration models, projections (logistic) & Indian census
sections.content:
## Migration, Population Projections & Decennial Census in India

### 1. Internal Migration — Definition and Measurement

**Migration** = movement of persons across an administrative boundary involving a change of usual residence for more than a minimum duration.

**Typology of internal migration streams:**

| Stream | Description |
|---|---|
| Rural → Urban | Most dominant in developing countries; primary driver of urbanisation |
| Urban → Rural | Counter-urbanisation; retirement migration; return migration |
| Rural → Rural | Seasonal agricultural and distress labour movement |
| Urban → Urban | Metropolitan-to-metropolitan skilled/professional migration |

**Census measures of migration in India:**
1. **Birth-place method:** Migrants = persons enumerated in a district/state different from their birth district/state.
2. **Last-residence method:** Migrants = persons whose usual residence 5 years ago was in a different district/state.
3. **Duration of residence:** How long the person has resided at current place.

**Net Migration:**
\[
\text{Net Migration} = I - O \quad \text{(In-migrants} - \text{Out-migrants)}
\]

**Balancing Equation of Population Change:**
\[
P_{t+n} = P_t + B_{t,t+n} - D_{t,t+n} + I_{t,t+n} - O_{t,t+n}
\]
where \( B \) = births, \( D \) = deaths, \( I \) = in-migrants, \( O \) = out-migrants.

*Real-life example:* The 2011 Census counted **309 million lifetime migrants** in India (26% of population). Maharashtra and Delhi were the largest net in-migrant states; Uttar Pradesh and Bihar were the largest net out-migrant states — driven by wage differentials. Kerala's large international out-migration generated remittances of ~₹85,000 crore in 2018 (≈31% of state GDP).

---

### 2. Migration Models

#### Gravity Model (Ravenstein 1885; formalised by Zipf 1946)
\[
M_{ij} = k\,\frac{P_i^{\alpha}\,P_j^{\beta}}{d_{ij}^{\gamma}}
\]
Migration between origin \( i \) and destination \( j \) is proportional to population sizes and inversely proportional to distance. Analogous to Newton's gravitational law.

**Ravenstein's Laws of Migration (1885):**
1. Most migrants travel **short distances**.
2. Migration proceeds **step by step** (small towns → regional centres → national capital).
3. Every major current produces a **counter-current**.
4. Long-distance migrants favour **commercial and industrial centres**.
5. **Urban residents** are less migratory than rural.

#### Stouffer's Intervening Opportunities Model (1940)
\[
M_{ij} = k\,\frac{P_j}{X_{ij}}
\]
where \( X_{ij} \) = number of intervening opportunities (jobs, services) between \( i \) and \( j \). Distance matters not per se, but through the opportunities it contains.

#### Lee's Push-Pull Framework (1966)
Migration occurs when **pull factors** at destination (employment, wages, amenities) outweigh **push factors** at origin (poverty, land fragmentation, climate shocks) plus the cost of migration.

**International Migration:** India is the **world's largest source of international migrants** (~18 million diaspora, World Migration Report 2022). Key corridors: India → UAE, USA, UK, Saudi Arabia.

---

### 3. Population Projection Methods

#### 3.1 Arithmetic (Linear) Projection
\[
P(t+n) = P(t) + n\,d, \qquad d = \frac{P(t_2) - P(t_1)}{t_2 - t_1}
\]
Assumes a **constant absolute increment** \( d \) per year. Appropriate only for short periods with linear trends.

#### 3.2 Geometric (Compound Growth) Projection
\[
P(t+n) = P(t)\,(1+r)^n \iff P(t) = P(0)\,e^{rt} \text{ (continuous)}
\]
\[
r = \left(\frac{P_{t_2}}{P_{t_1}}\right)^{1/(t_2-t_1)} - 1
\]
Constant **percentage** growth rate; appropriate for medium-term projections in growing populations.

#### 3.3 Logistic Curve — Pearl–Reed Model

Incorporates a **carrying capacity** \( K \) (upper asymptote):
\[
P(t) = \frac{K}{1 + e^{a + bt}}, \qquad b < 0
\]
```chart
{"compute":"logistic","K":100,"r":0.15,"P0":5,"tMax":60,"title":"Pearl–Reed Logistic Growth: S-curve with carrying capacity K, inflection at K/2"}
```
- **S-shaped (sigmoid) growth:** slow start, accelerating middle, decelerating as \( P \to K \).
- **Inflection point** (maximum growth rate) at \( P = K/2 \), time \( t^* = -a/b \).
- **Differential equation form:** \( \dfrac{dP}{dt} = r_0\,P\!\left(1 - \dfrac{P}{K}\right) \)

**Estimation of parameters (Pearl–Reed three-point method):**
Given observed populations \( P_1, P_2, P_3 \) at equally-spaced times (interval \( h \)):
\[
K = \frac{2\,P_1\,P_2\,P_3 - P_2^2(P_1+P_3)}{P_1\,P_3 - P_2^2}
\]
Then linearise: \( \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K - P(t)}{P(t)}\right) = a + bt \) and estimate \( a, b \) by OLS.

#### 3.4 Cohort-Component Method (Most Accurate)
Projects each age-sex cohort separately:
\[
P_{x+5}(t+5) = P_x(t) \times {}_{5}S_x + \text{Net migrants}
\]
where \( {}_{5}S_x = {}_{5}L_{x+5}/{}_5L_x \) = survival ratio from the life table.

| Model | Growth pattern | Key parameter | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | Constant increment | \( d \) | Short-term, linear |
| Geometric | Constant % growth | \( r \) | Medium-term |
| Logistic | S-shaped; bounded | \( K \) (carrying capacity) | Long-term, resource-limited |
| Cohort-component | Age-sex-specific | Life table + ASFR | Policy projections |

*Real-life example:* Raymond Pearl applied the logistic to US Census data (1790–1910) and predicted a US population ceiling of ~197 million; the actual 1960 population was ~179 million. India's demographers used logistic fitting (among other methods) to project India surpassing China's population around 2023 — confirmed by the UN in April 2023.

---

### 4. Intercensal and Postcensal Estimates

**Intercensal:** Estimate for a date **between** two censuses.
**Postcensal:** Estimate for a date **after** the latest census (no future census to verify).

| Method | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic interpolation | \( P(t) = P_1 + \dfrac{t-t_1}{t_2-t_1}(P_2-P_1) \) | Simple intercensal |
| Geometric interpolation | \( P(t) = P_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(t-t_1)/(t_2-t_1)} \) | Intercensal, better |
| Component (balancing eq.) | \( P_t = P_0 + B - D + NM \) | Postcensal; needs vital registration |
| Ratio method | Sub-area ratio assumed stable | Postcensal for small areas |

**India context:** After 2011 Census, the 2021 Census was postponed (COVID). RGI uses the **component method** (SRS births/deaths + balancing equation) for annual postcensal estimates.

*Real-life example:* India's population crossed **1.4 billion** (postcensal estimate) in 2022 per UN DESA — used by Ministry of Health for vaccine dose procurement and by NITI Aayog for per-capita income calculations.

---

### 5. Decennial Census in India

| Feature | Detail |
|---|---|
| Legal basis | **Census Act, 1948** |
| Authority | Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India (RGI), MHA |
| Frequency | Decennial — years ending in 1 |
| Method | De jure (usual place of residence) |
| Phases | Phase 1: House-listing; Phase 2: Population enumeration |
| Reference moment | 00:00 hrs of 1 March (1 October for snow-bound areas) |
| 2021 Census | **Postponed** due to COVID-19 — not yet conducted as of 2025 |

> **ISS tip:** Projection formulas to memorise: (1) Arithmetic: \( P(t+n) = P(t) + nd \); (2) Geometric: \( P(t+n) = P(t)(1+r)^n \); (3) Logistic: Pearl–Reed formula for \( K = (2P_1P_2P_3 - P_2^2(P_1+P_3))/(P_1P_3-P_2^2) \); (4) Logistic inflection at \( P = K/2 \). Balancing equation: all four components — \( P_{t+n} = P_t + B - D + I - O \). Ravenstein: at least three laws (short distance, step-wise, counter-current). Census Act: 1948; de jure; 2021 postponed. Gravity model formula: \( M_{ij} = kP_iP_j/d_{ij}^2 \) (simplest form). The cohort-component method survival ratio is \( {}_5S_x = {}_5L_{x+5}/{}_5L_x \) — a life-table ratio.

demography.tips:
**Paper IV — Demography & Vital Statistics: Exam Strategy (10 points)**

1. **Sources:** Four sources (Census, CRS, Sample Surveys, Hospital records) with limitations. SRS = dual-record system since 1969. Census Act 1948. De jure method. 2021 Census postponed.
2. **Life table columns:** Sequence \( l_x \to d_x \to q_x \to L_x \to T_x \to e_x^\circ \). Two most-tested formulas: \( e_x^\circ = T_x/l_x \) and \( T_x = T_{x+1} + L_x \). Reed–Merrell: \( q_x = 2m_x/(2+m_x) \). India \( e_0^\circ \) (2015–19): M 69.4, F 72.0.
3. **Makeham vs Gompertz:** Makeham = \( A + Bc^x \); Gompertz = \( Bc^x \) (i.e., \( A=0 \)). \( A \) = constant accident/exogenous hazard. \( c \approx 1.09 \) → mortality doubles every ~8 years. Makeham is linear in \( \ln(\mu_x - A) \).
4. **UN Model Tables:** Four families — North (old-age heavy), South (infant heavy), East (child heavy), **West (default for India)**. 25 levels keyed to female \( e_0^\circ \) from 20 to 80 years.
5. **Stable vs Stationary:** Stable: \( r = \text{const} \), age structure \( c(x) = be^{-rx}\ell(x) \). Stationary: \( r = 0 \), \( c_s(x) \propto L_x \), CBR = CDR = \( 1/e_0^\circ \). Euler–Lotka: \( 1 = \int e^{-rx}\ell(x)m(x)dx \) — setting \( r=0 \) gives NRR = 1.
6. **Fertility denominators (guaranteed MCQ):** CBR/CDR → total mid-year population. GFR → women 15–49. ASFR → women in that age group. IMR → live births (NOT population). NRR is the only measure with mortality (via \( \ell_x \)). Replacement TFR ≈ 2.1, not 2.0. India NFHS-5 TFR = 2.0 (2021, first below replacement).
7. **NRR logic:** NRR = 1 → stationary (long run). NRR = GRR × \( \bar\ell \). Hierarchy: NRR ≤ GRR ≤ TFR/2.05.
8. **Standardisation:** Direct: needs study ASDRs → SDR. Indirect: needs standard ASDRs + study counts → SMR. SMR = (Observed/Expected) × 100; SMR > 100 = excess mortality. IMR = deaths under 1 / live births × 1000 (India 2021: 25).
9. **Projections:** Logistic Pearl–Reed 3-point formula \( K = (2P_1P_2P_3 - P_2^2(P_1+P_3))/(P_1P_3-P_2^2) \). Inflection at \( P=K/2 \). Cohort-component survival ratio = \( {}_5L_{x+5}/{}_5L_x \). Balancing equation: \( P_{t+n} = P_t + B - D + I - O \).
10. **India Census 2011 key figures:** Population 121.09 cr, density 382/km², sex ratio 943, child sex ratio 914, literacy 74.04%, urban 31.16%. Beti Bachao Beti Padhao triggered by CSR = 914.

quality.title: Quality Control

sections.label: QC foundations: causes of variation, chart theory & WECO rules
sections.content:
## QC Foundations: Process Control Theory, Causes of Variation & WECO Rules

### 1. Quality — Definitions and Key Distinctions

**Quality** (Juran): *"Fitness for use"* — the totality of features that satisfy stated or implied needs.
**Quality** (Crosby): *"Conformance to requirements."*

**Two complementary strategies:**
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** On-line monitoring while production is running. Detects when the process shifts to an out-of-control state so assignable causes can be identified and removed.
- **Acceptance Sampling (Product Control):** Off-line inspection of finished lots to decide acceptance or rejection. Does not prevent defectives from being made; filters them after the fact.

**Why SPC?** Without control, variability is unchecked → defectives reach customers → warranty, recall, and reputation costs. SPC catches assignable causes early, before a run of bad product accumulates — reducing scrap, rework, and downstream inspection costs.

*Real-life example:* In 2000, the **Firestone tyre recall** (USA) — 6.5 million tyres recalled, 271 deaths attributed to tread-belt separations — was traced to a manufacturing process that went out of control at the Decatur plant. Post-incident investigation found that SPC signals had appeared on the X-bar chart weeks earlier but were not acted upon. The total economic cost exceeded USD 3 billion — a definitive case study in the cost of neglecting control chart signals.

---

### 2. Causes of Variation — Shewhart's Fundamental Distinction

Shewhart (1924) identified two fundamentally different sources of variation:

| Type | Also called | Nature | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common (chance) causes** | Random / inherent / natural variation | Many small, independent, unpredictable; always present | Reduce only by **redesigning** the process — management decision |
| **Assignable (special) causes** | Systematic / non-random variation | Identifiable specific source: tool wear, bad raw-material batch, untrained operator | **Remove immediately** when detected — operator/supervisor action |

**Shewhart's dichotomy:** A process in statistical control has only common causes; all output follows a stable, predictable distribution. A process with assignable causes is **out of statistical control** — the distribution is changing.

---

### 3. General Theory of Control Charts

A **Shewhart control chart** plots a sample statistic \( W \) (e.g., \( \bar{X} \), \( R \), fraction nonconforming \( \hat p \)) over sample number with three horizontal reference lines:

\[
\text{UCL} = \mu_W + 3\sigma_W, \qquad \text{CL} = \mu_W, \qquad \text{LCL} = \mu_W - 3\sigma_W
\]

**3-sigma rationale (Shewhart):** For a normally distributed statistic, \( P(|W - \mu_W| > 3\sigma_W) = 0.0027 \) — only **27 false alarms per 10,000 samples** when the process is in control. This gives:
\[
\text{ARL}_0 = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370
\]
A point beyond the 3-sigma limits when the process is in control occurs, on average, only every 370 samples.

**Two types of error:**
- **Type I (false alarm):** Point outside control limits when process is in control. Probability = \( \alpha = 0.0027 \) for 3-sigma limits.
- **Type II (missed signal):** Point inside limits when process is out of control. Probability = \( \beta(\delta) \) — depends on shift magnitude \( \delta \).

---

### 4. Rational Subgrouping

**Rational subgroup** (Shewhart): A group of items produced under **essentially the same conditions** — same machine, same operator, same short time window. The within-subgroup variation should reflect only **common causes**.

**Guideline:** Subgroup size \( n = 4 \) or \( 5 \) is standard for \( \bar{X}/R \) charts. Items in a subgroup should be consecutive (captured in one period). Do **not** mix product from different shifts, machines, or raw-material lots in one subgroup.

**Why rational subgrouping matters:** If subgroups are formed irrationally (mixing different machines), between-machine variation contaminates the estimate of \( \sigma \), widening control limits and masking real assignable causes.

---

### 5. Out-of-Control Criteria — Western Electric (WECO) Rules

The basic rule is one point outside \( \pm 3\sigma \), but the following **supplementary rules** detect subtler patterns (runs, trends, stratification):

| Rule | Pattern | Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule 1** | 1 point beyond \( \pm 3\sigma \) | Basic OOC signal |
| **Rule 2** | 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond \( \pm 2\sigma \) on the **same side** | Impending shift |
| **Rule 3** | 4 of 5 consecutive points beyond \( \pm 1\sigma \) on the **same side** | Gradual drift |
| **Rule 4** | 8 consecutive points on the **same side** of the centre line | Run — mean shift |
| **Rule 5** | 6 consecutive points **steadily increasing or decreasing** | Trend — tool wear |
| **Rule 6** | 15 consecutive points within \( \pm 1\sigma \) of CL | Stratification (subgroups mixing strata) |

**Trade-off:** More rules → higher probability of detecting real shifts (lower \( \beta \)) but also more false alarms (higher effective \( \alpha \)). Western Electric guidelines use all 4 primary rules simultaneously.

---

### 6. Process Monitoring and Control — The Control Chart Cycle

1. **Phase I (retrospective analysis):** Gather ~25 preliminary subgroups; estimate \( \mu \) and \( \sigma \) from the data; compute trial control limits; identify and remove OOC points due to assignable causes; revise limits.
2. **Phase II (prospective monitoring):** Apply the fixed limits from Phase I to future production; signal OOC, investigate cause, take corrective action.

*Real-life example:* A pharmaceutical tablet-compression line at Cipla uses a Phase I study of 30 subgroups (4 tablets each, weight measured) to establish \( \bar{\bar{X}} \) and \( \bar{R} \) control limits. Three subgroups during Phase I were OOC — traced to a worn punch; after replacement and re-establishment of limits, Phase II monitoring runs stably, catching punch-wear drift via the WECO trend rule (Rule 5) before tablets fall outside specification.

> **ISS tip:** The 3-sigma rationale: \( P(\text{beyond } \pm 3\sigma) = 0.0027 \), so \( \text{ARL}_0 = 1/0.0027 \approx 370 \). Two error types: Type I = false alarm (\( \alpha = 0.0027 \)); Type II = missed detection (\( \beta \)). Shewhart's two cause types — common and assignable — are exam staples. Rational subgrouping principle: within-subgroup variation should reflect only common causes. For WECO rules: memorise at minimum Rule 1 (beyond \( \pm 3\sigma \)), Rule 4 (run of 8), and Rule 5 (trend of 6) — these three are the most frequently examined.

sections.label: Attribute charts: p, np, c, u — distributions & control limits
sections.content:
## Control Charts for Attributes: p, np, c, u Charts

### Overview

**Attribute data** classify items as conforming or nonconforming (pass/fail, go/no-go) or count the number of nonconformities per inspection unit. Two underlying distributions apply:
- **Binomial:** for counts/fractions of **nonconforming units** → p-chart, np-chart
- **Poisson:** for counts of **nonconformities** (defects) per unit → c-chart, u-chart

---

### 1. p-Chart (Fraction Nonconforming)

**Underlying model:** Each item independently is nonconforming with probability \( p \). Number of nonconformities \( D_i \sim \text{Bin}(n_i, p) \).

\[
\hat{p}_i = \frac{D_i}{n_i}, \qquad E(\hat{p}_i) = p, \qquad \text{Var}(\hat{p}_i) = \frac{p(1-p)}{n_i}
\]

**Control limits (constant \( n \)):**
\[
\text{UCL} = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}, \quad \text{CL} = \bar{p}, \quad \text{LCL} = \max\!\left(0,\; \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}\right)
\]
\[
\bar{p} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^m D_i}{\sum_{i=1}^m n_i} \quad \text{(overall fraction nonconforming — the centre line)}
\]

**Variable \( n \):** Compute individual limits for each \( n_i \) (exact), or use \( \bar{n} = \sum n_i/m \) as an approximation (if variation in \( n_i \) is less than ±25%).

---

### 2. np-Chart (Number Nonconforming)

Plots the actual **count** \( D_i \) (not fraction). Requires **constant** \( n \).

\[
E(D_i) = n\bar{p}, \qquad \text{Var}(D_i) = n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})
\]
\[
\text{UCL} = n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}, \quad \text{CL} = n\bar{p}, \quad \text{LCL} = \max\!\left(0,\; n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}\right)
\]

**Equivalence:** p-chart and np-chart give identical out-of-control signals; np-chart is preferred when operators find raw counts more interpretable than fractions.

---

### 3. c-Chart (Count of Nonconformities per Unit)

**Underlying model:** Nonconformities occur according to a Poisson process with rate \( \lambda \) per inspection unit of **fixed size**. Count per unit: \( c_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\bar{c}) \).

\[
E(c_i) = \bar{c}, \qquad \text{Var}(c_i) = \bar{c} \qquad \text{(Poisson: mean = variance)}
\]
\[
\text{UCL} = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}, \quad \text{CL} = \bar{c}, \quad \text{LCL} = \max\!\left(0,\; \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}\right)
\]
\[
\bar{c} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m c_i \quad \text{(average nonconformities per unit)}
\]

**Critical assumption:** The inspection unit (e.g., one circuit board, one metre of fabric, one castings batch of defined size) must be **identical** for every sample.

---

### 4. u-Chart (Nonconformities per Unit — Variable Sample Size)

When the inspection unit size varies from sample to sample (\( n_i \) units inspected):

\[
u_i = \frac{c_i}{n_i}, \qquad E(u_i) = \bar{u}, \qquad \text{Var}(u_i) = \frac{\bar{u}}{n_i}
\]
\[
\text{UCL}_i = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n_i}}, \quad \text{CL} = \bar{u}, \quad \text{LCL}_i = \max\!\left(0,\; \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n_i}}\right)
\]
\[
\bar{u} = \frac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i} \quad \text{(total nonconformities / total units inspected)}
\]

Note: Unlike p-chart and np-chart (which limit \( \hat{p}_i \) to \( [0,1] \)), \( u_i \) can exceed 1 when an inspection unit has more than one nonconformity per sub-unit.

---

### 5. Comparison Table — All Four Attribute Charts

| Chart | Plots | Distribution | \( n \) or unit size | Denominator constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| p | Fraction nonconforming \( \hat{p}_i \) | Binomial | Can vary | None |
| np | Count nonconforming \( D_i \) | Binomial | **Must be constant** | Fixed \( n \) |
| c | Count of nonconformities \( c_i \) | Poisson | **Fixed unit size** | Same inspection unit |
| u | Nonconformities per unit \( u_i \) | Poisson | Can vary | None |

**Phase I estimation:** If target (standard) \( p_0 \), \( c_0 \), \( u_0 \) is known from past data, substitute directly. If unknown (Phase I), estimate from the preliminary samples as above.

*Real-life example — c-chart:* A PCB manufacturer monitors solder-joint defects. Each inspection unit is one PCB with 500 joints. The c-chart plots defects per board; when the c-chart signals OOC (sudden jump in \( c_i \)), investigation reveals a new solder-paste batch with incorrect flux concentration — a classic assignable cause detectable by the Poisson-based c-chart before a full shift of boards is scrapped.

*Real-life example — u-chart:* A textile mill in Surat monitors fabric defects (weaving faults, stains). Because roll lengths vary, they plot \( u_i = c_i/n_i \) (defects per metre). A sustained upward run triggered Rule 4, traced to a worn loom heddle that was introducing extra weft breakages.

> **ISS tip:** The four-chart selection rule: p and u handle **variable** sample/unit size; np and c require **constant** size. Distribution: p/np → **Binomial** (items pass/fail); c/u → **Poisson** (count of events in a region). The LCL for all attribute charts is \( \max(0, \text{computed LCL}) \) — a count or fraction cannot be negative. Variance of Poisson = mean = \( \bar c \) (hence \( 3\sqrt{\bar c} \) spread around the CL). In an exam numerical, always state the distribution, compute \( \bar{p} \) or \( \bar{c} \), then plug into the three-sigma formula.

sections.label: Variable charts: X-bar, R, S — constants & chart pairs
sections.content:
## Control Charts for Variables: \( \bar{X} \), R, and S Charts

### 1. Variable Data and Subgroup Statistics

**Variable data** = a measured quality characteristic (diameter, weight, tensile strength, fill volume). Carries more information than attribute data for the same \( n \), making variable charts more sensitive.

**Notation:** \( m \) subgroups, each of size \( n \). For subgroup \( i \):
\[
\bar{X}_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^n X_{ij}, \quad R_i = \max_j X_{ij} - \min_j X_{ij}, \quad S_i = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_j (X_{ij}-\bar{X}_i)^2}
\]

**Grand mean and average range/SD:**
\[
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bar{X}_i, \qquad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m R_i, \qquad \bar{S} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m S_i
\]

---

### 2. SPC Constants — Origin and Values

For normally distributed data, the distribution of \( R/\sigma \) (and \( S/\sigma \)) has been tabled:

| Constant | Definition | Role |
|---|---|---|
| \( d_2 \) | \( E(R/\sigma) \) | \( \hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2 \) — unbiased estimator of \( \sigma \) |
| \( d_3 \) | \( \text{SD}(R/\sigma) \) | Used to compute R-chart limits |
| \( c_4 \) | \( E(S/\sigma) \) | \( \hat{\sigma} = \bar{S}/c_4 \) — unbiased estimator of \( \sigma \) |
| \( A_2 = 3/(d_2\sqrt{n}) \) | Factor for \( \bar{X} \) chart with \( \bar{R} \) | UCL/LCL = \( \bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2\bar{R} \) |
| \( A_3 = 3/(c_4\sqrt{n}) \) | Factor for \( \bar{X} \) chart with \( \bar{S} \) | UCL/LCL = \( \bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_3\bar{S} \) |
| \( D_3 = 1 - 3d_3/d_2 \) | LCL factor for R-chart | \( \text{LCL}_R = D_3\bar{R} \) |
| \( D_4 = 1 + 3d_3/d_2 \) | UCL factor for R-chart | \( \text{UCL}_R = D_4\bar{R} \) |
| \( B_3, B_4 \) | LCL, UCL factors for S-chart | \( \text{LCL}_S = B_3\bar{S} \), \( \text{UCL}_S = B_4\bar{S} \) |

**Tabulated values for common subgroup sizes:**

| \( n \) | \( d_2 \) | \( A_2 \) | \( D_3 \) | \( D_4 \) | \( c_4 \) | \( A_3 \) | \( B_3 \) | \( B_4 \) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.128 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 0.7979 | 2.659 | 0 | 3.267 |
| 3 | 1.693 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 0.8862 | 1.954 | 0 | 2.568 |
| **4** | **2.059** | **0.729** | **0** | **2.282** | 0.9213 | 1.628 | 0 | 2.266 |
| **5** | **2.326** | **0.577** | **0** | **2.114** | 0.9400 | 1.427 | 0 | 2.089 |
| 6 | 2.534 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 0.9515 | 1.287 | 0.030 | 1.970 |
| 10 | 3.078 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 0.9727 | 0.975 | 0.284 | 1.716 |

Note: For \( n \le 6 \): \( D_3 = 0 \) (LCL of R-chart = 0); \( B_3 = 0 \) for \( n \le 5 \) (LCL of S-chart = 0).

---

### 3. R-Chart (Range Chart) — Monitor Within-Group Dispersion

The R-chart must **always be plotted and examined first.** If dispersion is out of control, the \( \sigma \) estimate is unreliable and the \( \bar{X} \) chart limits are invalid.

\[
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
\]
\[
\text{UCL}_R = D_4\,\bar{R}, \quad \text{CL}_R = \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_R = D_3\,\bar{R}
\]

For \( n \le 6 \): \( D_3 = 0 \) so \( \text{LCL}_R = 0 \) — small samples give very variable ranges, so a lower bound of zero is natural.

---

### 4. \( (\bar{X}, R) \) Chart Pair — Monitor Mean and Dispersion Simultaneously

\( \bar{X} \)-chart limits (using \( \bar{R} \) as the estimate of process \( \sigma \)):
\[
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\,\bar{R}, \quad \text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\,\bar{R}
\]

**Interpretation of \( A_2\bar{R} \):** This equals \( 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = 3\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 3(\bar{R}/d_2)/\sqrt{n} = (3/d_2\sqrt{n})\bar{R} = A_2\bar{R} \) — confirming the formula is exactly 3-sigma.

**Phase I procedure:**
1. Collect \( m \approx 25 \) subgroups, compute \( \bar{X}_i \) and \( R_i \).
2. Plot R-chart first; remove OOC points (assignable causes), recompute \( \bar{R} \).
3. Plot \( \bar{X} \) chart with revised limits; remove OOC points.
4. Use final limits for Phase II monitoring.

---

### 5. \( (\bar{X}, S) \) Chart Pair — Preferred for Larger Subgroups (\( n \ge 10 \))

For large \( n \), the range \( R \) becomes statistically inefficient (uses only 2 of \( n \) observations). The sample standard deviation \( S \) is preferable.

\( \bar{X} \)-chart limits (using \( \bar{S} \)):
\[
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_3\,\bar{S}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_3\,\bar{S}
\]

S-chart limits:
\[
\text{UCL}_S = B_4\,\bar{S}, \quad \text{CL}_S = \bar{S}, \quad \text{LCL}_S = B_3\,\bar{S}
\]

---

### 6. Individuals and Moving Range (I-MR) Chart — For \( n = 1 \)

When the process produces one item per time period (e.g., a chemical batch, a daily measurement):

\[
\overline{MR} = \frac{1}{m-1}\sum_{i=2}^m |X_i - X_{i-1}|, \quad \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\overline{MR}}{d_2\big|_{n=2}} = \frac{\overline{MR}}{1.128}
\]
\[
\text{UCL}_X = \bar{X} + 3\,\hat{\sigma} = \bar{X} + 2.660\,\overline{MR}, \quad \text{LCL}_X = \bar{X} - 2.660\,\overline{MR}
\]
\[
\text{UCL}_{MR} = D_4\,\overline{MR} = 3.267\,\overline{MR} \quad (D_3 = 0 \text{ for } n=2)
\]

---

### 7. When to Use Which Variable Chart

| Situation | Chart | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| \( n = 1 \) (one measurement per period) | I-MR | Cannot form subgroup |
| \( 2 \le n \le 9 \) | \( \bar{X} \& R \) | Range is efficient; easy to compute manually |
| \( n \ge 10 \) | \( \bar{X} \& S \) | Range loses efficiency; S is better |

*Real-life example:* Maruti Suzuki's engine-block boring operations use \( (\bar{X}, R) \) charts with \( n = 5 \) (five consecutive bored blocks per subgroup). Constants \( A_2 = 0.577 \), \( D_4 = 2.114 \) are pre-printed on the shop-floor control sheet. When the R-chart signalled OOC (range doubled over 3 subgroups), the cause was traced to a worn boring tool — identified and replaced within 20 minutes, preventing an entire shift of out-of-spec cylinder bores.

> **ISS tip:** The full constants table for \( n = 4 \) and \( n = 5 \) is a guaranteed numerical: \( n=4 \): \( A_2=0.729, D_3=0, D_4=2.282 \); \( n=5 \): \( A_2=0.577, D_3=0, D_4=2.114 \). Always check R-chart first — if it is OOC, the \( \bar{X} \) chart limits are invalid. The formula derivation: \( A_2\bar{R} = 3\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 3(\bar{R}/d_2)/\sqrt{n} \) — knowing this derivation lets you re-derive \( A_2 \) without tables. For I-MR chart: \( \hat\sigma = \overline{MR}/1.128 \); \( \text{UCL}_X = \bar X + 2.660\,\overline{MR} \) (memorise 2.660).

sections.label: OC curve, ARL, process capability (Cp, Cpk) & gauging
sections.content:
## OC Curve, ARL, Process Capability & Control by Gauging

### 1. Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve for Control Charts

The **OC curve** of a control chart plots \( \beta(\delta) \) — the probability of **not** detecting a shift of magnitude \( \delta\sigma \) on the next sample — against \( \delta \).

For an \( \bar{X} \) chart with 3\(\sigma\) limits, subgroup size \( n \), and a mean shift of \( \delta\sigma \):

\[
\beta(\delta) = P(\text{LCL} \le \bar{X} \le \text{UCL} \mid \mu_1 = \mu_0 + \delta\sigma)
= \Phi(3 - \delta\sqrt{n}) - \Phi(-3 - \delta\sqrt{n})
\]

where \( \Phi \) is the standard normal CDF.

**Key values:**

| Shift \( \delta \) | \( n = 1 \) | \( n = 4 \) | \( n = 9 \) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (false alarm) | 0.9973 | 0.9973 | 0.9973 |
| 1.0 \(\sigma\) | 0.9772 | 0.8413 | 0.5000 |
| 2.0 \(\sigma\) | 0.8413 | 0.3085 | 0.0228 |
| 3.0 \(\sigma\) | 0.5000 | 0.0228 | 0.0001 |

**Shape:** Starts near 1 at \( \delta = 0 \), falls to 0 as \( \delta \to \infty \). Larger \( n \) makes the curve fall more steeply (better discrimination). Wider limits (\( k = 2\sigma \) instead of \( 3\sigma \)) also improve detection but increase false alarms.

```chart
{"compute":"oc-xbar","n":[1,4,9],"k":3,"title":"OC Curve — X̄ chart (3σ limits): β(δ) for n = 1, 4, 9"}
```

---

### 2. Average Run Length (ARL)

**ARL = expected number of samples** until the chart signals (correctly or as a false alarm).

\[
\text{ARL}(\delta) = \frac{1}{P(\text{signal on one sample})} = \frac{1}{1-\beta(\delta)}
\]

| Scenario | Symbol | Formula | Design target |
|---|---|---|---|
| In-control (false alarm period) | \( \text{ARL}_0 \) | \( 1/\alpha \) | **Large** (\( \ge 200 \)); 3-sigma gives 370 |
| Out-of-control (detecting shift \( \delta \)) | \( \text{ARL}_1 \) | \( 1/(1-\beta(\delta)) \) | **Small** (detect fast) |

**ARL comparisons for a 1-sigma shift (\( \delta = 1 \)):**

| Chart | \( n \) | \( \text{ARL}_1(\delta=1) \) |
|---|---|---|
| Shewhart \( \bar{X} \) | 1 | ~44 |
| Shewhart \( \bar{X} \) | 4 | ~6.3 |
| CUSUM (\( k=0.5, h=5 \)) | 1 | ~10.4 |
| EWMA (\( \lambda=0.2 \)) | 1 | ~11.0 |

```chart
{"compute":"arl","n1":1,"n2":4,"title":"ARL₁ vs mean shift δ: Shewhart (n=1,4) vs CUSUM(k=0.5,h=5) vs EWMA(λ=0.2)"}
```

**Key insight:** Shewhart charts detect **large** shifts (\( \delta \ge 2 \)) quickly but are **slow for small persistent shifts** (\( \delta \le 1 \)). CUSUM and EWMA are dramatically better for small shifts. This motivates the use of memory charts.

---

### 3. Process Capability

**Process capability** measures whether the natural variability of an in-control process fits within its engineering specification limits (LSL, USL).

**The process \( \sigma \)** used is the **within-subgroup (short-term) estimate:**
\[
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\bar{S}}{c_4}
\]
(Note: the sample standard deviation of all individual measurements, \( S_{\text{overall}} \), overestimates the within-subgroup \( \sigma \) if the process mean drifts — use the control-chart estimate.)

#### 3.1 \( C_p \) — Potential Capability (Spread Only)

\[
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}
\]
- Measures whether the **process spread** (\( 6\sigma \)) fits the tolerance band.
- Does **not** account for whether the process is centred.

#### 3.2 \( C_{pk} \) — Actual Capability (Centering Included)

\[
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \hat{\mu}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\; \frac{\hat{\mu} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right) = C_p\left(1 - k\right)
\]
where \( k = |\hat{\mu} - M|/((\text{USL}-\text{LSL})/2) \) and \( M \) = midpoint of spec. \( C_{pk} \le C_p \) always; equality only when \( \hat\mu = M \) (perfectly centred).

#### 3.3 \( C_{pm} \) — Taguchi Capability Index

\[
C_{pm} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\hat{\mu} - T)^2}}
\]
where \( T \) = target value (may differ from midpoint \( M \)). Penalises deviation from target even if within specs.

**Industry standards:**

| \( C_{pk} \) | PPM defective (Normal) | Industry verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 0.67 | ~45,500 ppm | Poor — not capable |
| 1.00 | 2,700 ppm | Minimally capable |
| 1.33 | 64 ppm | **Industry standard minimum** |
| 1.67 | 0.57 ppm | Highly capable |
| 2.00 | 0.002 ppm | Six Sigma level |

```chart
{"compute":"capability","cp":1.33,"shift":0.5,"title":"Process Capability: Cp=1.33 (industry minimum), process shifted 0.5σ off-centre"}
```

**Process capability vs process control:**
- **In control + capable:** Ideal — stable and producing within spec.
- **In control but NOT capable:** Process is stable but too variable for specs → reduce \( \sigma \) (process improvement).
- **Capable but NOT in control:** On average within spec, but erratic → find and remove assignable causes first.

*Real-life example:* A Sun Pharma tablet-press has USL = 505 mg, LSL = 495 mg, \( \hat\mu = 501 \) mg, \( \hat\sigma = 1.2 \) mg. Then \( C_p = 10/7.2 = 1.39 \) (spread fits) but \( C_{pk} = \min(4/3.6, 6/3.6) = \min(1.11, 1.67) = 1.11 \) — the process is off-centre, so \( C_{pk} < 1.33 \): below the industry minimum despite adequate spread.

---

### 4. Control by Gauging

**Gauging** uses a **go/no-go gauge** to classify items as conforming or nonconforming without measuring the exact value:
- **Go gauge:** Checks that the part is not **too large** (tests against upper spec).
- **No-go gauge:** Checks that the part is not **too small** (tests against lower spec).

When gauging is used for process monitoring:
- Sample information is attribute (pass/fail) → use **p-chart** or **np-chart**.
- **Efficiency loss:** Discards metric information; for the same \( n \), a p-chart is less powerful than an \( \bar{X} \) chart (requires ~3–4 times larger \( n \) for equivalent ARL performance).
- **Advantages:** Faster inspection; no expensive measuring instrument; suitable for go/no-go production environments.

*Real-life example:* A fastener manufacturer uses go/no-go gauges to check bolt thread dimensions — each bolt takes 2 seconds to gauge vs 15 seconds to measure with a micrometer. With a production rate of 10,000 bolts/shift, gauging makes 100% inspection feasible; a p-chart on the hourly fraction failing the no-go gauge detects changes in the thread-rolling die condition.

> **ISS tip:** OC curve formula for \( \bar X \) chart: \( \beta(\delta) = \Phi(3-\delta\sqrt{n}) - \Phi(-3-\delta\sqrt{n}) \) — know how to evaluate at specific \( \delta, n \). ARL formula: \( 1/(1-\beta) \); in-control ARL = 370 for 3-sigma. Process capability: \( C_p \) ignores centering; \( C_{pk} \) is the binding constraint (\( \le C_p \)). Minimum industry standard: \( C_{pk} \ge 1.33 \). Six Sigma: \( C_{pk} \ge 2.0 \). The capability vs control distinction is a direct exam question: a process can be in control but not capable (stable but too variable) or capable but not in control (correct mean variability but erratic).

sections.label: MA, EWMA, CUSUM (V-mask & tabular) & economic design
sections.content:
## MA, EWMA, CUSUM Charts & Economic Design of the \( \bar{X} \) Chart

### 1. Motivation — Why Memory Charts?

Shewhart control charts are based only on the **current sample** — they have no memory of previous observations. Consequently:
- For large shifts (\( \delta \ge 2\sigma \)), Shewhart charts signal quickly (ARL₁ ≈ 2–6).
- For **small persistent shifts** (\( \delta \le 1\sigma \)), Shewhart charts are slow (ARL₁ ≈ 44 for \( \delta = 1\sigma, n=1 \)).

**Memory charts** (MA, EWMA, CUSUM) exploit the **history** of the process to detect small shifts much faster.

---

### 2. Moving Average (MA) Chart

At time \( t \), the **moving average** of span \( w \) is:
\[
M_t = \frac{1}{w}\sum_{i=t-w+1}^{t} X_i, \qquad \sigma_{M_t}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{w}
\]

Control limits:
\[
\text{UCL}/\text{LCL} = \mu_0 \pm \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{w}}
\]

**Limitation:** All \( w \) observations receive **equal weight** — the oldest observation in the window contributes as much as the most recent. The chart forgets all information before \( w \) periods ago.

---

### 3. EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) Chart

Proposed by Roberts (1959). The EWMA statistic:
\[
Z_t = \lambda X_t + (1-\lambda)Z_{t-1}, \quad 0 < \lambda \le 1, \quad Z_0 = \mu_0
\]
Expanding: \( Z_t = \lambda\sum_{j=0}^{t-1}(1-\lambda)^j X_{t-j} + (1-\lambda)^t\mu_0 \)

Weights **decline geometrically** — the most recent observation has weight \( \lambda \), the previous \( \lambda(1-\lambda) \), and so on. Older observations are **down-weighted exponentially**.

**Variance of EWMA:**
\[
\sigma_{Z_t}^2 = \sigma^2\,\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}\left[1-(1-\lambda)^{2t}\right] \xrightarrow{t\to\infty} \sigma_Z^2 = \frac{\lambda\,\sigma^2}{2-\lambda}
\]

**Steady-state control limits:**
\[
\text{UCL} = \mu_0 + L\,\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}}, \qquad \text{LCL} = \mu_0 - L\,\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}}
\]
where \( L \approx 3 \) (exact value fine-tuned by simulation to achieve target \( \text{ARL}_0 \)).

```chart
{"compute":"arl","n1":1,"n2":4,"title":"ARL comparison — EWMA and CUSUM detect small shifts far faster than Shewhart"}
```

**Recommended design values:**
- For detecting **small shifts** (\( \delta \approx 0.5\sigma \)): \( \lambda = 0.1 \), \( L = 2.7 \) → ARL₁ ≈ 10.
- **Balanced detection** (\( \delta \approx 1.0\sigma \)): \( \lambda = 0.2 \), \( L = 3.0 \) → ARL₀ ≈ 400, ARL₁ ≈ 11.

**Key properties:**
- As \( \lambda \to 1 \): EWMA → Shewhart \( X \) chart (all weight on current observation).
- As \( \lambda \to 0 \): Extremely smooth; responds slowly (heavy smoothing).
- EWMA is equivalent to an **ARIMA(0,1,1)** one-step-ahead forecast.

*Real-life example:* A continuous petrochemical reactor at IOCL monitors outlet temperature using EWMA (\( \lambda = 0.15 \)). Catalyst deactivation causes a slow temperature drift (~0.8σ per 10 hours). The EWMA chart signals in ~15 samples vs ~40 samples for a Shewhart \( X \) chart — saving ~25 hours of off-spec production before the catalyst is regenerated.

---

### 4. CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Chart

The CUSUM accumulates signed deviations from the target \( \mu_0 \), making it optimal (by the SPRT theory) for detecting a **specific shift** \( \delta \).

#### 4.1 Tabular (Algorithmic) CUSUM

Two one-sided statistics, reset to zero when negative:
\[
C_i^+ = \max\!\left(0,\; (X_i - \mu_0) - k + C_{i-1}^+\right), \quad C_0^+ = 0
\]
\[
C_i^- = \max\!\left(0,\; -[(X_i - \mu_0) + k] + C_{i-1}^-\right), \quad C_0^- = 0
\]

**Signal:** Out of control if \( C_i^+ > h \) (upward shift) or \( C_i^- > h \) (downward shift).

**Design parameters:**
- \( k \) = **reference (allowance) value** = \( \delta/2 \) (halfway between in-control mean and the shift to detect).
- \( h \) = **decision interval** (threshold).

**Standard recommended design** (Montgomery; for \( \delta = 1\sigma \)):
- \( k = 0.5 \), \( h = 5 \) → ARL₀ ≈ 465, ARL₁ ≈ 10.4 — versus Shewhart ARL₁ ≈ 44.

#### 4.2 V-Mask (Graphical CUSUM — Barnard 1959)

Plot the cumulative sum \( S_n = \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu_0) \). Place a V-shaped mask with vertex at the **current point** (or ahead by distance \( d \)):

\[
\tan\theta = k = \frac{\delta}{2}, \qquad d = \frac{h}{k}
\]

**Signal:** If **any previous cumulative sum point** falls outside the arms of the V-mask, the process is out of control.

**Equivalence:** Tabular CUSUM and V-mask give identical signals for the same \( (k, h) \) values. The tabular form is preferred in practice (computer-friendly; easier to automate).

| Parameter | Tabular CUSUM | V-mask |
|---|---|---|
| Reference value | \( k \) | \( \tan\theta \) |
| Decision threshold | \( h \) | \( d \) (lead distance) |
| Relationship | — | \( \tan\theta = k \), \( d = h/k \) |

---

### 5. Comparison: Shewhart vs CUSUM vs EWMA

| Feature | Shewhart | CUSUM | EWMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | None (current sample only) | Full history (accumulated) | Geometric exponential weights |
| Best for | Large shifts (\( \delta \ge 2\sigma \)) | Small persistent shifts (\( \delta \le 1\sigma \)) | Small to medium shifts |
| ARL₁ (\( \delta=1\sigma, n=1 \)) | ~44 | ~10.4 | ~11 |
| Interpretability | Simple | Moderate | Moderate |
| Equivalent model | — | SPRT-based | ARIMA(0,1,1) |

*Real-life example:* Intel's wafer-fabrication yield monitoring uses CUSUM (\( k=0.5, h=5 \)) to detect a gradual 0.5% yield drop per shift (caused by chamber contamination). Shewhart would need ~155 samples; CUSUM detects in ~26 samples — saving ~130 wafers of scrap per detection cycle.

---

### 6. Economic Design of the \( \bar{X} \) Chart (Duncan's Model, 1956)

The **economic design** chooses all three chart parameters — sample size \( n \), sampling interval \( h \), and control limit width \( k \) (in \( \sigma \) units) — **simultaneously** to minimise the expected total cost per unit time.

**Duncan's cost components:**
| Cost | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling cost | \( a_2 + a_3 n \) | Fixed \( a_2 \) + variable \( a_3 \) per unit sampled |
| False alarm cost | \( a_0 \) | Investigation + restart cost per false alarm |
| Out-of-control cost | \( a_1 \) | Cost per unit time process is OOC (defective production) |
| Process shift rate | \( \lambda \) | Poisson rate: shifts occur at rate \( \lambda \) (mean OOC run = \( 1/\lambda \) |

**Optimisation:** Minimise \( E[\text{cost per unit time}] = E[\text{cost per cycle}]/E[\text{cycle length}] \) over \( (n, h, k) \) jointly, subject to ARL₀ being acceptably large.

**Key practical findings:**
- Larger shift magnitude \( \delta \) → smaller optimal \( n \) (big shifts need less averaging).
- Higher \( a_1/a_0 \) ratio (expensive OOC production vs cheap investigation) → tighter control, more frequent sampling.
- Optimal \( k \) is often **not** exactly 3 — may be wider (if false alarms are costly) or narrower (if OOC costs are very high).

*Real-life example:* A BHEL turbine-blade forging line with OOC cost \( a_1 = \text{₹}600/\text{min} \) (yield loss) and investigation cost \( a_0 = \text{₹}2500 \) (machine stoppage) — the economic optimum gives \( n=3 \), \( h=30 \) min, \( k=2.8\sigma \) — smaller subgroups and slightly narrower limits than the standard \( n=5, k=3\sigma \) rule, because the cost of undetected defective production is very high.

> **ISS tip:** CUSUM dual parameters: tabular → \( (k, h) \); V-mask → \( (\theta, d) \); mapping is \( \tan\theta = k \) and \( d = h/k \). Standard recommendation: \( k = 0.5 \), \( h = 5 \) for detecting a 1-sigma shift. EWMA formula: \( Z_t = \lambda X_t + (1-\lambda)Z_{t-1} \); steady-state variance \( = \lambda\sigma^2/(2-\lambda) \). Limits: \( \mu_0 \pm L\sigma\sqrt{\lambda/(2-\lambda)} \). Economic design key insight: the naive \( n=5, k=3\sigma \) rule is not always optimal — Duncan's model can give \( k \ne 3 \) and \( n \ne 5 \). Shewhart vs CUSUM ARL₁ for 1-sigma shift: 44 vs 10.4 — memorise this comparison.

sections.label: Acceptance sampling: single & double plans, OC curve, AOQL
sections.content:
## Acceptance Sampling: Single & Double Plans, OC Curve, AOQ/AOQL

### 1. Concept and Purpose

**Acceptance sampling** (statistical product control) decides whether to **accept or reject a lot** of items based on inspection of a random sample. Applied when:
- 100% inspection is too costly, slow, or impossible.
- Testing is destructive (tensile strength, explosive fuse testing).
- Receiving lots from a supplier before use in production (incoming inspection).

**Key distinction from SPC:** Acceptance sampling does not prevent defectives from being produced — it filters them **after the fact**. SPC prevents them during production.

**Standard references:** ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (attribute sampling), IS 2500 (BIS; based on ISO 2859), MIL-STD-105E.

---

### 2. Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Lot size | \( N \) | Total items in the lot |
| Sample size | \( n \) | Items inspected |
| Acceptance number | \( c \) | Max defectives allowed; accept if \( d \le c \) |
| Fraction defective | \( p \) | Actual proportion of defectives in lot |
| AQL | \( p_1 \) | Acceptable Quality Level — "good" quality the consumer routinely accepts |
| LTPD (RQL) | \( p_2 \) | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective — "bad" quality the consumer wants to reject |
| Producer's risk | \( \alpha \) | \( P(\text{reject} \mid p = p_1) \) — rejecting a good lot |
| Consumer's risk | \( \beta \) | \( P(\text{accept} \mid p = p_2) \) — accepting a bad lot |

Typical design: \( \alpha = 0.05 \), \( \beta = 0.10 \).

---

### 3. OC Curve for Acceptance Sampling

The **OC (Operating Characteristic) curve** plots \( L(p) = P(\text{accept lot}) \) against the lot fraction defective \( p \).

**Exact distribution:** Hypergeometric — used when \( n/N > 0.10 \) (large sampling fraction).

**Binomial approximation** (large \( N \), fixed \( p \)):
\[
L(p) = P(X \le c) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}
\]

**Poisson approximation** (small \( p \), large \( n \), so \( np \) is moderate):
\[
L(p) \approx \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^x}{x!}
\]

**OC curve properties:**
- \( L(0) = 1 \): If there are no defectives, always accept.
- \( L(1) = 0 \): If every item is defective, always reject.
- **Larger \( n \)** (same \( c/n \) ratio): Steeper OC curve → better discrimination.
```chart
{"compute":"oc-sampling","n":100,"c":3,"pMax":0.12,"title":"OC Curve — Single Sampling Plan (n=100, c=3): L(p) vs incoming fraction defective"}
```
- **Larger \( c \)** (same \( n \)): Curve shifts right → more lenient plan.
- **Ideal OC curve:** Vertical step at a threshold \( p^* \) — only achievable with 100% inspection.

**AQL and LTPD on the OC curve:**
\[
L(p_1) = 1 - \alpha \qquad \text{(probability of accepting at AQL = } 1 - \text{producer's risk)}
\]
\[
L(p_2) = \beta \qquad \text{(probability of accepting at LTPD = consumer's risk)}
\]

---

### 4. Single Sampling Plan \( (n, c) \) — Design

**Procedure:** Inspect \( n \) items from a lot of \( N \). Count defectives \( d \).
- If \( d \le c \): **Accept** the lot.
- If \( d > c \): **Reject** the lot (screen the lot — 100% inspect, replace defectives).

**Design given \( (p_1, \alpha, p_2, \beta) \)** using Poisson approximation:

Let \( \mu_1 = np_1 \) and \( \mu_2 = np_2 \). The operating ratio is \( \mu_2/\mu_1 = p_2/p_1 \).

1. Find \( c \) and \( \mu_1 \) from Poisson OC curve tables such that:
   - \( P(X \le c \mid \mu_1) \ge 1-\alpha \)
   - \( P(X \le c \mid \mu_2) \le \beta \)
2. Compute \( n = \mu_1/p_1 = \mu_2/p_2 \).

**Worked example:** \( p_1 = 0.01 \), \( \alpha = 0.05 \), \( p_2 = 0.05 \), \( \beta = 0.10 \). Operating ratio = 5. From Poisson tables: \( c = 3 \), \( \mu_1 = 1.10 \), \( \mu_2 = 6.68 \). So \( n = 1.10/0.01 = 110 \).

*Real-life example:* A FMCG company (HUL) receives 10,000 bottle caps per lot. Using a single sampling plan \( (n=200, c=5) \): inspect 200 caps, accept if ≤ 5 defective. The Binomial-based plan has AQL = 1% and LTPD ≈ 5%, protecting both the supplier (from routine rejection of good lots) and the consumer (from accepting bad lots).

---

### 5. Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and AOQL

When rejected lots are **100% screened** (all defectives found and replaced with conforming items):

\[
\text{AOQ}(p) = \frac{p \cdot L(p) \cdot (N-n)}{N} \approx p \cdot L(p) \quad \text{for } N \gg n
\]

**Shape of the AOQ curve:** Starts at 0 (when \( p=0 \), no defectives enter), rises to a maximum, then falls back to 0 (when \( p=1 \), all lots are rejected and screened → perfect outgoing quality).

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):**
\[
\text{AOQL} = \max_p\,[\text{AOQ}(p)]
\]
```chart
{"compute":"aoq","n":100,"c":3,"pMax":0.15,"title":"AOQ Curve — AOQL (peak) is the worst-case outgoing quality after 100% rectification"}
```
